They were written, like Schubert's last piano sonatas, in the shadow of approaching death; Brahms completed the chorale preludes in the summer of 1896, the year before he died, and never heard them performed. They are not large: most last only two or three minutes. But they have a concentrated emotional depth that makes them — to those who know them — among the most moving things Brahms ever wrote. The chorale melody appears in each piece like a remembered voice, the harmonisation around it drawn from a lifetime's accumulated wisdom. Anne Horsch's complete Brahms organ works on CPO in an SACD recording CD 117 is an important disc: this repertoire is rarely recorded at all, and rarely recorded as thoughtfully as this. It is the necessary counterweight to Liszt's extroversion: the organ as a medium for private meditation rather than public display.

Aristide Cavaillé-Coll and the French Romantic School

If one man is responsible for creating the French Romantic organ tradition, it is Aristide Cavaillé-Coll (1811–1899), the Toulouse-born instrument builder who installed his magnificent organs in the great Paris churches — Saint-Sulpice, Notre-Dame, the Madeleine, the Trinité — and in doing so created the sonic world that inspired César Franck, Charles-Marie Widor, Camille Saint-Saëns and the entire French organ school of the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. A Cavaillé-Coll organ is a different creature from a North German Baroque instrument: warmer, more homogeneous, with a characteristic tone in the foundations that is simultaneously full and smooth, and a capacity for crescendo — the gradual addition of stops to build from a single soft flute to the full power of the instrument — that Baroque organs could not match. His instruments respond to the player with a physical immediacy, the wind pressure and valve mechanism designed so that each note speaks cleanly and promptly regardless of how many stops are engaged.

Your collection contains a unique document of this tradition: the Hommage à Aristide Cavaillé-Coll disc recorded on the Grand Orgue of the Abbatiale Saint-Gilles de Montoire CD 511, with Marie-Andrée Morisset-Balier at the organ and Michel Morisset on trumpet. This particular instrument, built by Cavaillé-Coll himself, preserves the sound world of the French Romantic tradition in something close to its original state: the tone colours available, the way the instrument breathes and responds, the acoustic of the abbey church combining with the organ to produce that characteristic French grandeur. Hearing this disc alongside any of the Bach recordings gives a vivid impression of how differently two great traditions conceived the same instrument.

The central figure of the French Romantic school was César Franck (1822–1890), the Belgian-born organist of the Trinité in Paris, whose three Chorals for organ are among the supreme achievements of late Romantic keyboard music. Written in the last year of his life — Franck completed them on his deathbed — they combine the rigour of the chorale prelude tradition inherited from Bach with a harmonic language of extraordinary richness and the full sonority of a great Cavaillé-Coll instrument. They are not well-known outside organ circles but they deserve the widest possible audience: the E major Choral in particular has a quality of sustained lyrical beauty that is unlike anything else in the organ repertoire. Peter Hurford's survey disc of organ favourites on Decca includes Franck alongside Widor, Boëllmann and others CD 514, providing a useful introduction to the French Romantic tradition in a single accessible disc.

Charles-Marie Widor (1844–1937) composed ten organ symphonies between 1872 and 1900 — extended multi-movement works designed to exploit every resource of the Cavaillé-Coll instrument, combining Franck's harmonic seriousness with a more overtly theatrical grandeur. The famous Toccata from his Fifth Symphony, with its motoric repeated-chord accompaniment and soaring melody, has become the most played piece of organ music after Bach's D minor Toccata and Fugue. It appears, among other works, on Peter Hurford's disc CD 514.

The Organ Symphony: Saint-Saëns

The most famous work for organ and orchestra — arguably the most famous piece of French symphonic music — is Camille Saint-Saëns's Symphony No. 3 in C minor Op. 78, known universally as the Organ Symphony. Composed in 1886 and dedicated to the memory of Liszt (who died while Saint-Saëns was finishing the work), it is one of the most exhilaratingly scored pieces in the repertoire: two movements each divided into two linked sections, with the organ appearing first in a chorale of hushed grandeur at the movement's climax, then in the finale as the foundation of an overwhelming statement of triumph.

Saint-Saëns himself said of it: “I have given everything to it I was able to give. What I have here accomplished, I will never achieve again.” The finale, which transforms the gloomy C minor of the opening into a blazing C major, with the organ pedals underpinning a theme of unstoppable momentum and the full orchestra above it, is one of the great thrilling moments in all of nineteenth-century music. The work became the most popular orchestral showpiece using organ until Poulenc's Organ Concerto of 1938 offered a twentieth-century alternative.

Your collection holds it in two recordings that offer an instructive contrast. Peter Hurford with the Montreal Symphony Orchestra on Decca CD 381 pairs it with the Carnival of the Animals and piano works: a Francophone recording in which the organ playing has the idiomatic naturalness of a player steeped in the French tradition, the sound of the Montreal organ warm and resonant. Herbert von Karajan with the Berliner Philharmoniker on Deutsche Grammophon CD 382 takes a quite different approach: grander, more symphonic, with Karajan treating the organ almost as one voice among many in a vast orchestral canvas. The Berlin Philharmonic's string sound, in particular, gives the opening pages a weight and warmth that the smaller Montreal strings cannot match. The two recordings illuminate different aspects of the same work: the Hurford is more specifically French in character; the Karajan is more internationally symphonic. Both are worth hearing, and the comparison illustrates the fundamental difference between understanding a composer's cultural tradition and imposing a broader interpretative personality on the music.

The Great Organs of Britain and Europe

One of the most pleasurable aspects of the organ discography is the way recordings serve as portraits of specific instruments in specific buildings. Your collection contains several such portraits, each illuminating a different aspect of the organ's variety.

Simon Preston at Westminster Abbey CD 516 presents one of the great British cathedral organs in its natural habitat. The Westminster Abbey organ — like most British cathedral organs, installed high in a stone screen above the choir — has a particular tonal character: bright and clear in the upper registers, with a quality in the foundations that reflects the building's long reverberation and vast acoustic. Preston, who was Sub-Organist at Westminster Abbey before his appointment at Christ Church Oxford, knows the instrument's character intimately, and the recital programme is chosen to show what it does best.

Philip Ledger at King's College Chapel, Cambridge CD 512 offers a collection of organ voluntaries — the English tradition of short pieces played before and after services — that ranges from Baroque through to twentieth-century British composers. King's College Chapel is one of the most acoustically generous buildings in England, its stone vaulting creating a reverberant sound that gives organ music a sustained, almost infinite quality; Ledger's programme of voluntaries is the ideal vehicle for exploring what this acoustic does to the instrument.

The Baroque Organ of St Anne's Church in Warsaw CD 513, played by Przemysław Kapituła, documents a specific historical instrument in Central Europe: the kind of modestly scaled, carefully preserved church organ that Bach himself might have played, quite unlike the massive modern instruments of the concert hall or the vast cathedral organs of England and France. The intimacy of the sound — the smaller scale, the characteristic tone of the eighteenth-century pipes — puts the Bach repertoire in a different light from any of the large-instrument recordings.

The Father Willis organ at Salisbury Cathedral, heard in a choral recital of the Cathedral choir CD 517, represents yet another tradition: Henry Willis (1821–1901) was the nineteenth century's greatest British organ builder, whose instruments — at the Royal Albert Hall, St Paul's Cathedral, and Salisbury — defined the sound of the Victorian Anglican church. The Father Willis tone is warmer and more blended than the bright clarity of a Baroque organ, with a characteristic quality in the reeds that became the identifying mark of the English cathedral sound. The Salisbury disc shows this organ in its proper liturgical context, accompanying the choir in choral evensong and voluntaries.

Portrait Gallery: The Organists in Your Collection

Simon Preston (born 1938) is the most significant single figure in your collection's organ holdings, appearing in eight discs. He was Sub-Organist of Westminster Abbey, then Organist of Christ Church Oxford (succeeding Thomas Armstrong), then Organist and Master of the Choristers of Westminster Abbey, and he maintained a parallel career as a solo recitalist and recording artist of the first rank. His Handel recordings with Trevor Pinnock and the English Concert CD 211 CD 212 are among the finest HIP recordings of the repertoire. His Bach CD 62 CD 515 has a clean, well-paced intelligence that makes it consistently satisfying. And his Westminster Abbey recital CD 516 shows what a great organist can do with a great instrument that he knows intimately from years of daily use. He also appears as conductor in several of your Handel choral recordings CD 225, demonstrating the breadth of a career that extended well beyond the organ bench.

Helmut Walcha (1907–1991) was, by the consensus of several generations of musicians and listeners, the greatest Bach organist of the twentieth century. Born in Leipzig and trained at the conservatory there, he lost his sight completely at the age of nineteen as a result of a vaccination against smallpox. Rather than abandoning the keyboard, he adapted: his students read music to him note by note while he sat at the keyboard and memorised it; his concert programmes were learned in this way, piece by piece, over years. His two complete recordings of the Bach organ works for Archiv — the second of which, made in the 1970s on the great Schnitger organs of Germany and the Netherlands, is represented in your collection CD 60 — have a quality that many listeners find irreplaceable: an absolute purity of contrapuntal voice, a rhythmic precision that owes nothing to visual distraction, and a depth of interpretative thought accumulated over decades of living with this music.

Marie-Claire Alain (1926–2013) was the most prolific Bach organist of the twentieth century — not the most profound, but the most comprehensive and historically well-informed. The sister of the composer Jehan Alain (killed at the age of twenty-nine in 1940), she carried on the family's musical legacy through an extraordinarily productive career that included three complete Bach organ cycles CD 59 and recordings of virtually the entire French organ repertoire. Her contribution to the historical understanding of French Baroque organ music was particularly significant: she commissioned the building of historical-copy instruments and brought the scholarly rigour of the HIP movement to repertoire that had previously been played on entirely inappropriate Romantic instruments.

Ton Koopman (born 1944) has already appeared extensively in the HIP article, but his work as an organist — distinct from his role as conductor of the Amsterdam Baroque Orchestra — deserves separate acknowledgment. His organ recordings in your collection CD 61 CD 518 show an organist of complete historical awareness and great rhythmic vitality, at his best in the toccatas and fantasias where the combination of contrapuntal rigour and improvisatory freedom is most demanding. His Handel Organ Concertos CD 210 have a particular energy and wit that reflect his theatre instincts: these are concert pieces, he reminds us, not sacred meditations.

Peter Hurford (born 1930) was the most prolific British organist of the late twentieth century — extraordinarily productive as a recording artist, most notably in the ten-volume complete Bach organ works for Decca that remains one of the standard complete cycles available. His survey disc of organ favourites CD 514 and his Saint-Saëns Organ Symphony CD 381 show two different aspects of his gifts: the ease with which he moves between styles, and the warmth and confidence of his playing in the French Romantic tradition.

Piet Kee (born 1927) spent most of his career as organist of the Grote Kerk in Haarlem — which means he had decades of daily access to the great Müller organ that is heard in your collection CD 519. His relationship with that instrument is the defining fact of his musicianship: he understands its character as only someone who has played it every day for years can, and his Bach on the Müller organ has a combination of historical appropriateness and deep personal knowledge that is quite different from any studio recording.

The Trumpet and Organ: An Ancient Partnership

The combination of trumpet and organ is one of the oldest and most natural of all musical partnerships — both instruments are at their best in large resonant spaces, both belong to the tradition of outdoor ceremonial music, and the brightness of the natural trumpet is the ideal complement to the organ's fuller, more sustained tone. Alison Balsom and Quentin Thomas's disc on EMI CD 533 brings together two players of exceptional quality in a programme that exploits this combination to the full: Balsom, arguably the finest trumpet soloist of her generation, and Thomas at the organ in a varied programme that ranges from Baroque to the twentieth century. The disc is a reminder that the organ is not only a solo instrument or a cathedral presence, but a flexible vehicle for ensemble music-making of many kinds.

The Twentieth Century and the Continuing Tradition

The organ's repertoire in the twentieth century was dominated by the French tradition that Franck had founded. Olivier Messiaen (1908–1992), who served as organist at the Trinité in Paris for over sixty years, wrote music for the instrument of staggering ambition: La Nativité du Seigneur (1935), Les Corps glorieux (1939), the Livre du Saint Sacrement (1984) — long, complex, deeply theological works that pushed the instrument's sonic boundaries as far as they would go. Messiaen's organ music is not represented in your collection but belongs on the wishlist: it is unlike anything else in the repertoire, simultaneously the summit of the French organ tradition and a complete world unto itself.

Maurice Duruflé (1902–1986) and Jean Langlais (1907–1991) continued the Franck/Widor tradition with rather more restraint: Duruflé's few but exquisitely crafted organ works — the Prélude, Ada&gio et Choral varié and the four Motets — are among the finest French organ pieces of the century, their modal harmonies and dignified restraint placing them firmly in the sacred tradition their teacher Vierne had established.

Recommended Recordings Not in the Collection

Bach: Complete Organ Works — Helmut Walcha
Deutsche Grammophon Archiv  ·  14-disc box set  ·  recorded 1947–52 and 1956–71

The complete cycles by the musician who, despite blindness from the age of nineteen, became the undisputed master of this repertoire. The second cycle, recorded on the great Schnitger organs of Germany and the Netherlands, is the one to seek: the combination of Walcha's absolute clarity of contrapuntal voice with these historic instruments is uniquely compelling. Your collection already has the Great Organ Works disc; the complete set extends this across all fourteen volumes.

Franck: Complete Organ Works — various organists
Various labels  ·  2-disc set

The three Chorals, the Six Pièces and the Trois Pièces represent the summit of the French Romantic organ tradition, and they are tragically underrepresented in most collections. Marie-Claire Alain's Erato recording, on a Cavaillé-Coll instrument, is the most stylistically authentic; Wayne Marshall's more recent account on Virgin Classics is more broadly appealing. Either is essential for understanding what the French organ school was actually about.

Messiaen: Livre du Saint Sacrement — Gillian Weir
Collins Classics  ·  2-disc set  ·  1988

The summit of the twentieth-century organ repertoire: Messiaen's last and largest organ work, completed in 1984 at the age of seventy-six. Eighteen pieces of vast scope and spiritual intensity, drawing on bird-song, Hindu rhythms, modes of limited transposition and the full resources of a great modern organ. Gillian Weir is the supreme Messiaen interpreter; her Collins recording remains the finest available. An essential document of what the organ can do in a composer's hands who loved it completely and understood it profoundly.

Widor: Complete Organ Symphonies — Jeanne Demessieux
Various labels  ·  5-disc set

The ten symphonies complete, on Cavaillé-Coll instruments in the Paris churches for which they were written. Demessieux (1921–1968) was the finest woman organist of the twentieth century and the most naturally gifted Widor interpreter on record; her early death at forty-six cut short a career that had already produced recordings of exceptional quality. The famous Toccata from Symphony No. 5 in context of the complete work reveals how much more there is to Widor than his most popular page.

Further Reading

Books
The Organ: An Encyclopedia
Douglas E. Bush & Richard Kassel (eds.)  ·  Routledge, 2006

The comprehensive reference work on the instrument, its builders, its composers and its performers, with entries covering all aspects of organ history from the hydraulis of Alexandria to the digital instrument of the present day. Authoritative and exhaustively cross-referenced, it is the standard English-language reference for anyone who wants to go deeper into the instrument's history than any general survey can take them.

The Organ Music of J.S. Bach
Peter Williams  ·  Cambridge University Press, 3 vols., 1980–84; revised 2003

The standard analytical study of Bach's organ works, covering every piece with a combination of historical context, source criticism and musical analysis. Williams is the world's leading Bach organ scholar and writes with great clarity and occasional wit. Not a book to read from cover to cover, but invaluable as a companion when exploring the recordings in your collection: the entries on the major Preludes and Fugues and the chorale preludes are particularly illuminating.

Messiaen: A Critical Introduction
Paul Griffiths  ·  J&W Chester, 1985

The most readable introduction to Messiaen's life and work, by the same Paul Griffiths who wrote the string quartet history. The chapter on the organ works is the best short account available, giving sufficient musical detail to make the recordings comprehensible while remaining accessible to the general reader. Essential preparation for exploring the Messiaen recordings recommended above.

Online
Organs of the World
organdatabase.com

A comprehensive database of organs worldwide, with photographs, technical specifications and in many cases audio recordings. Particularly useful for finding information about the specific instruments heard in your collection: the Müller organ at St Bavo's Haarlem, the Father Willis organ at Salisbury, and the Cavaillé-Coll instruments of Paris are all well-documented here.

BBC Radio 3 — Choral Evensong and Organist's Choice
bbc.co.uk/radio3

Radio 3's weekly Choral Evensong broadcast from a different British cathedral or chapel every week is the ideal vehicle for hearing the organ in its natural liturgical context — accompanying the choir, playing voluntaries, providing an acoustic foundation for the entire service. The archive on BBC Sounds goes back several years and provides a remarkable survey of the great British cathedral and collegiate organs. The occasional Organist's Choice programmes, in which a major organist discusses and plays their favourite pieces, are similarly valuable.

A Musical Portrait

The Piano and Its Music

Three Centuries of the Instrument That Changed Everything  ·  c.1700 – present

The Instrument That Contains a History

No other instrument has so completely documented the history of Western classical music. From the moment Bartolomeo Cristofori built the first workable piano in Florence around 1700, the instrument has been at the centre of almost every significant musical development: Classical elegance, Romantic passion, modernist disruption, the singer-songwriter tradition, jazz, minimalism. The piano is the instrument on which composers think, on which students learn, on which amateurs engage with music at home, and on which the greatest performers have left the most comprehensive body of recorded evidence. It contains multitudes.

What makes it so dominant is a combination of two qualities that no other acoustic instrument possesses simultaneously: the ability to play many notes at once (like the organ or guitar) and the ability to vary dynamics and touch with extreme sensitivity (unlike the harpsichord, which is one of its ancestors). Press a key softly and the hammer strikes gently; press it hard and it strikes powerfully. Hold the key down and the note sustains; release it and it dies. Press the sustaining pedal and the strings continue to vibrate even after the key is released, blending harmonics in a wash of sound. These qualities together make the piano capable of imitating, however imperfectly, almost any other instrument — and of doing things no other instrument can do at all.

Your collection holds what is probably the richest piano section of any collection in this catalogue. Murray Perahia appears in sixteen separate discs; Mitsuko Uchida in eleven; András Schiff in nine. There are six separate recordings of the Goldberg Variations, two complete Mozart piano concerto cycles, four accounts of Rachmaninov's Third Concerto, three of Tchaikovsky's First. The depth of the holdings makes this one of the most rewarding areas of the entire collection to explore — not just for the great works themselves but for the opportunity to compare how different minds approach the same musical problem.

Before the Piano: Harpsichord and Fortepiano

The piano did not arrive fully formed. Its predecessor, the harpsichord, had dominated keyboard music for two centuries: a magnificent instrument, incapable of varying its dynamic level, that produced a clear, plucked sound ideally suited to the contrapuntal textures and dance rhythms of Baroque music. Rameau's complete harpsichord works, played by Pieter-Jan Belder on the original instrument CD 373, give a clear impression of what Baroque keyboard music sounded like in the instrument's own time: bright, clear, incisive, rhythmically precise, but unable to swell or diminish, unable to sing in the way a bowed string can sing.

The fortepiano — Cristofori's invention, developed through the eighteenth century by German builders including Silbermann, Stein and Walter — offered something revolutionary: a keyboard instrument that responded to the player's touch, louder when struck firmly, softer when struck gently. The name itself contains the innovation: forte (loud) and piano (soft), abbreviated eventually to simply “piano”. The earliest fortepianos were lighter and more responsive than the modern concert grand, with a rapid decay of tone that made sustained singing melody difficult but gave the instrument a transparency and a clarity of inner voices that the later instrument sometimes obscures.

Andreas Staier's recording of the Mozart Piano Concertos Nos. 9 and 17 on a historical fortepiano with Concerto Köln CD 288 is the clearest illustration in your collection of what the instrument was like in Mozart's own time: the balance between solo and orchestra is fundamentally different from any modern-piano recording, the fortepiano sitting within the orchestral texture rather than dominating it, and the rapid decay of the upper register giving the ornamental passages a lightness that the modern Steinway cannot replicate. The CPE Bach harpsichord concertos with Andreas Staier and the Freiburger Barockorchester CD 51 show the same player on the older instrument in music that genuinely requires its plucked, non-sustaining quality. Comparing these two discs is itself a short history of keyboard development in the eighteenth century.

Bach on the Piano: A Special Argument

The question of whether Bach's keyboard music should be played on the piano — an instrument he never used — has been argued for nearly a century, and it shows no signs of being resolved. The HIP movement has produced outstanding Bach on harpsichord, clavichord, and fortepiano. But the greatest pianists of the modern era have also produced Bach on the modern concert grand that has found a permanent place in the repertoire, and the argument is not simply about historical accuracy but about what different instruments reveal in the same music.

The central exhibit is the Well-Tempered Clavier, which your collection holds in four recordings on modern piano alone. Angela Hewitt's Hyperion account CD 7 treats the music with the natural lyricism of a pianist who loves Bach above all composers: the preludes sing, the fugues breathe, the whole set has a sense of being freshly discovered rather than reverently delivered. András Schiff's separate recordings of the two books CD 8 CD 9 are more searching and more varied: he uses the piano's sustaining qualities to explore the harmonic richness of the preludes in ways a harpsichord cannot, and his fugues have an architectural clarity that reveals contrapuntal detail. Sviatoslav Richter's legendary 1969 recordings CD 10 are the most overtly pianistic of all: slow, grand, monumental, treating the music with the architectural weight of a late Beethoven sonata. Some find this too much; others find it the most profound Bach piano playing ever recorded.

Andrea Bacchetti's fresh account of the Two-Part Inventions and Sinfonias CD 18 treats these teaching pieces as the exquisite miniatures they are, finding real character in each of the thirty pieces. Murray Perahia brings his characteristic combination of vocal singing tone and structural clarity to the Partitas CD 19 CD 20 and English Suites CD 24, while Angela Hewitt plays the complete Six Partitas CD 21 with an idiomatic fluency that makes the dances feel genuinely danced rather than merely played. And Perahia conducts from the keyboard in the Keyboard Concertos with the Academy of St Martin-in-the-Fields CD 25, an approach that emphasises the chamber-music intimacy of works originally intended for small-scale, private performance.

The Goldberg Variations: A Collection Within the Collection

The Goldberg Variations BWV 988 is one of the most recorded single works in the piano repertoire, and your collection's six separate accounts make it the most comprehensively covered work in the entire catalogue. This is worth pausing to consider: six pianists, six recordings, one set of thirty variations on a bass line from a 1725 aria. What can they possibly all say differently?

The Goldbergs were composed around 1741 — published as the fourth volume of the Clavier-Übung series — and consist of an Aria in G major followed by thirty variations arranged in a systematic pattern: every third variation is a two-voice canon at an increasing interval, while the others alternate between character pieces of widely varying mood, including a quodlibet (a cheerful combination of folk songs) as the penultimate variation and a return to the original Aria as the conclusion. The bass line and harmonic structure of the Aria return in every variation; the Aria itself, with its ornamented, almost improvisatory top line, is the emotional frame that surrounds everything.

Glenn Gould's two recordings — the 1955 debut recording that made him famous overnight and the 1981 recording made weeks before his death, both on a single Soli Deo Gloria set CD 11 — bracket the entire history of the work's modern performance tradition. The 1955 recording is startling even now: fast, rhythmically incisive, contrapuntally clear, stripped of the Romantic heaviness that had characterised earlier performances, played by a twenty-two-year-old who sounds as if he has just discovered something nobody else has noticed. The 1981 recording is completely different: slower, more expressive, more inward, each variation given its own reflective weight. Gould died of a stroke ten days after recording it. The two recordings together form one of the most extraordinary documents in the piano discography — a life's relationship with a single work.

András Schiff recorded the Goldbergs twice as well, both accounts in your collection CD 13 CD 14, separated by some years and showing how his relationship with the work deepened. Murray Perahia's Sony recording CD 15 brings a vocal cantabile to the music that is entirely his own: the variations sing rather than argue, and the return of the Aria at the end has a quality of recognition that is genuinely moving. Angela Hewitt CD 16 brings her characteristic clarity and affection for the music; and Beatrice Rana's Warner account CD 12 is the newest voice of the six, a young Italian pianist bringing both technical brilliance and a somewhat different temperament to music that most interpreters approach with deep solemnity. To play all six back to back is an education in how the same notes can contain genuinely different musical worlds.

Mozart and the Piano Concerto: His Greatest Achievement

Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart did not invent the piano concerto, but he transformed it so completely that every subsequent practitioner has had to come to terms with what he did. His twenty-seven piano concertos — written between 1767 and 1791, spanning his entire mature career — are the most completely achieved single body of concerto writing in the repertoire, each one a different personality, each one a different solution to the fundamental question of how a solo instrument and an orchestra can engage with each other in a dialogue that is simultaneously musical and dramatic.

What Mozart did to the concerto form is to make the interaction between soloist and orchestra genuinely conversational: not the soloist playing and the orchestra accompanying, but two participants in a dialogue, sometimes agreeing, sometimes disagreeing, sometimes pursuing independent threads that eventually converge. The orchestra has themes the soloist does not play; the soloist has passages that transform what the orchestra said into something new. And unlike Beethoven, who would later make the concerto a drama of conflict and resolution, Mozart's concertos have the quality of witty, perfectly judged conversation between equals.

Your collection holds two complete Mozart piano concerto cycles, which is a remarkable duplication that reflects the extraordinary quality of both. Murray Perahia with the English Chamber Orchestra recorded the complete concertos for CBS/Sony over many years CD 286 CD 296 CD 297 CD 298, and the set is one of the most admired in the discography: warm, naturally musical, with a singing tone in the slow movements that is immediately recognisable as Perahia's. He also plays the two-piano concertos with Radu Lupu CD 295 and the three-piano concertos in a delightful collaboration CD 287 — works that reveal a more playful, improvisatory side of Mozart's concerto writing. Mitsuko Uchida with the same orchestra covers the great late concertos across three volumes CD 307 CD 308 CD 309 plus individual discs CD 293 CD 316, and her approach is subtly different from Perahia's: more probing, more willing to explore the darker implications of the minor-key works, with a sense of structural architecture that reveals how carefully Mozart organised the long-range harmonic plans of each concerto.

Emil Gilels' recording of the final Piano Concerto K.595 with his daughter Elena on a companion piece CD 311 is one of the great Mozart recordings of the twentieth century. Gilels plays the concerto at a slower tempo than most of his contemporaries, with a weight and a depth of tone that make the famous slow movement's theme feel like a final farewell — which it may well have been, as the concerto was composed in Mozart's last year. And Alicia de Larrocha's piano sonata disc CD 301 adds another voice to the Mozart piano picture: the great Spanish pianist whose natural elegance in this repertoire is immediately recognisable, and whose late recordings of the early Mozart sonatas have a wisdom that transcends mere historical reconstruction.

Mitsuko Uchida's complete piano sonatas CD 294 are the standard recording for this repertoire — refined, historically well-informed, with a depth of character in even the most apparently straightforward sonatas. Mikhail Pletnev's eccentric but consistently fascinating accounts CD 290 offer a completely different perspective: slower, more Romantic in temperament, treating the sonatas with the weight of Beethoven. And Claudio Arrau's earlier recordings CD 289 have a classical clarity and a natural dignity that belongs to a now-vanished school of pianism.

Beethoven and the Expanding Piano

Beethoven's relationship with the piano was simultaneously a love affair and an argument. He was, in his youth, one of the most formidable pianists in Vienna — celebrated for his improvisatory power, his physical force at the keyboard, and a technique that sometimes literally broke the strings of the instruments available to him. He was perpetually dissatisfied with those instruments, always waiting for builders to catch up with what his musical imagination required: more dynamic range, a fuller bass, a more singing treble. His piano music can be read as a series of demands that the instrument of his time could not quite meet, and that the modern concert grand meets almost but not quite completely.

The five piano concertos cover his three periods with remarkable completeness. The First and Second Concertos (written actually in reverse order) are still essentially Classical in spirit; the Third, in C minor, is the turning point — the first of his concertos that announces the Romantic drama of the middle period. The Fourth, in G major, is perhaps the most subtly original: its extraordinary opening, in which the piano begins alone with a quiet G major chord that the orchestra then answers in the foreign key of B major, establishes from the first bar that this will not be a conventional concerto. And the Fifth, the “Emperor”, is the most overtly heroic — the full flowering of the grand Romantic concerto style that dominated the century after it.

Vladimir Ashkenazy's recordings of the Third Concerto CD 74 and the First CD 75 with the Vienna Philharmonic represent the polished, thoroughly professional school of Beethoven playing: technically impeccable, musically well-considered, with Mehta providing sensitive support. Claudio Arrau's Emperor with the Staatskapelle Dresden CD 103 has a larger, more monumental character — Arrau was one of the last representatives of the nineteenth-century grand tradition, trained by Liszt's pupil Martin Krause, and his Beethoven has a weight and a sense of occasion that belongs to a different era from Ashkenazy's cooler approach. Alfred Brendel's Emperor CD 89 is the most intellectually searching of the three: Brendel, one of the great Beethoven scholars among pianists, brings to every bar a level of analytical thought that reveals structural details other accounts pass over. The Murray Perahia account of the Third and Fourth with the Concertgebouw and Haitink CD 90 adds a fourth perspective: more naturally lyrical than either Brendel or Arrau, finding the singing quality in Beethoven that is often obscured by emphasis on his drama.

The piano sonatas are represented by a range of approaches that between them cover most of the interpretative possibilities. Paul Lewis's recording of the Diabelli Variations Op. 120 CD 92 treats this monumental work — thirty-three variations on a waltz theme by the publisher Anton Diabelli, who had sent the theme to fifty composers and asked them each for a variation; Beethoven, who initially described it as a “cobbler's patch”, eventually wrote thirty-three of his own — with the architectural understanding it demands. The variations range from the comic to the cosmic, from a tiny fugue to a sublime slow variation of overwhelming beauty; Lewis navigates the whole with a combination of technical security and genuine musical intelligence.

András Schiff's recording on Beethoven's own Broadwood piano CD 93 is one of the most unusual and rewarding discs in the entire collection. Beethoven received this particular English grand piano as a gift in 1818; it has a different character from modern instruments — lighter action, less power in the bass, a transparency in the middle register — and playing the late sonatas on it reveals dynamic and tonal relationships that modern instruments distort. Schiff's approach on this instrument is revelatory: the famous opening of the Hammerklavier sounds entirely different at the correct pitch and on the instrument for which it was conceived.

Schubert: The Piano as Song

Schubert's piano music is among the most immediately personal in the repertoire: not theatrical, not heroic, but lyrical and inward in a way that reflects his particular genius for continuous melody and his characteristic harmonic sidesteps into unexpected lights. Where Beethoven's piano music is fundamentally architectural — about structure, conflict, resolution — Schubert's is fundamentally vocal: melody sustained and varied, harmonies shifted and recoloured, time expanded to accommodate the full span of a feeling.

The eight Impromptus — four in Op. 90 and four in Op. 142 — are the most immediately accessible of his piano works: relatively short, each one complete in a single mood, the melodic writing consistently beautiful. Murray Perahia recorded them twice, both accounts in your collection CD 384 CD 385, and the two discs show how a great pianist's relationship with familiar repertoire can deepen over time. Mitsuko Uchida's Schubert disc CD 388 includes both impromptus and sonata movements, with the quality of listening intelligence she always brings: never a phrase that is not considered, never an effect that is not earned. András Schiff's piano sonatas disc CD 386 includes the early A major Sonata D.664, which has a youthful grace entirely different from the weight of the late works. Brigitte Engerer's Hymne an die Nacht disc CD 393, presenting Schubert songs transcribed for piano alongside the Liszt transcriptions of the same songs, shows how the Romantic tradition of the song transcription worked in practice: Liszt heard songs and immediately imagined what a pianist might do with them that a voice and piano together could not.

Schumann: The Literary Piano

Robert Schumann came to music from literature and never quite left it. His early piano works — Carnaval, Kinderszenen, Kreisleriana, the Davidsbündlertänze, the Fantasie in C major — are saturated with literary references, private codes, and invented characters: Florestan the passionate extrovert and Eusebius the dreaming introvert appear as designated performers in individual pieces, and the collection as a whole has the quality of a Roman à clef in which the musical ideas are simultaneously abstract and autobiographical. He wrote these pieces in the late 1830s during his frustrated courtship of Clara Wieck, and the music carries the urgency and frustration of that period on every page.

Mitsuko Uchida's recording of the Fantasie Op. 17 and the Davidsbündlertänze Op. 6 CD 411 is among the finest Schumann recordings available: she brings both the literary understanding and the purely musical intelligence that these works require, finding in the Fantasie's first movement in particular a sustained emotional narrative that makes the piece feel like a complete dramatic arc rather than a series of beautiful episodes. Radu Lupu's Kinderszenen and Kreisleriana CD 412 have a quality of absolute naturalness: Lupu was one of those rare pianists whose playing never sounds effortful, for whom the music simply appears to exist in the air around the instrument, and his Schumann has a warmth and a spontaneity that makes even the most familiar pieces sound freshly composed. Sviatoslav Richter's disc of Schumann's late Bunte Blätter paired with Brahms's Op. 118 Klavierstücke CD 415 shows two of the most inward and searching works of the Romantic piano literature played by a musician who understood their particular emotional world profoundly.

Chopin: The Piano Complete

Frédéric Chopin wrote almost exclusively for the piano. Unlike Schumann, who eventually composed symphonies and chamber music; unlike Brahms, who wrote concertos for other instruments; unlike Liszt, who was as much an orchestral and choral composer as a keyboard one, Chopin returned again and again to the instrument he knew most intimately, finding in its particular combination of singing tone, harmonic richness and rhythmic flexibility the exact medium for his musical imagination. His complete output for solo piano is one of the most unified and personal bodies of work any composer produced: the nocturnes, études, mazurkas, polonaises, preludes, ballades, scherzos, waltzes and impromptus together constitute a world as complete and consistent as Bach's Well-Tempered Clavier — and as inexhaustibly various.

Maurizio Pollini's complete Nocturnes on Deutsche Grammophon CD 149 represent one of the most authoritative Chopin recordings of the modern era. Pollini is not a Romantic pianist in the old-fashioned sense — he is precise, clear-textured, sparing with rubato, less indulgent in the expressive freedoms that characterise the salon Chopin tradition — but his clarity of line reveals the architectural organisation of these apparently spontaneous pieces with unusual precision. Nikolai Demidenko's mixed Chopin programme CD 150 represents a completely different temperament: more Russian in flavour, more improvisatory, more willing to linger where an emotion is particularly intense. Alexandre Tharaud's Préludes CD 151 bring a French clarity and elegance to music that has a French connection — Chopin lived most of his adult life in Paris — but is fundamentally Polish in its rhythmic and harmonic character.

The piano concertos bring out the most overtly Romantic side of his writing: the long-spun themes, the ornamental passagework, the relationship between the yearning solo line and the supporting orchestra. Martha Argerich with the Montreal Symphony under Charles Dutoit CD 152 is one of the most celebrated recordings in the Chopin discography: Argerich's Chopin has an electricity and a spontaneity that no subsequent recording has quite matched, the ornamental passages flashing by with the quality of improvisation, the slow movements aching with a particular kind of Polish nostalgia. Ingrid Fliter with the Scottish Chamber Orchestra CD 147 brings a more classical restraint; Ivo Pogorelich's famous recording with Abbado and the Chicago Symphony CD 148 is deliberately controversial — Pogorelich plays the Second Concerto at a tempo so slow it becomes almost a different piece, but the intensity of the slow movement in his version is genuinely extraordinary.

Brahms: The Piano's Philosopher

Brahms wrote for the piano throughout his career and in virtually every available form: the three early sonatas that announced his ambitions, the sets of variations that demonstrated his contrapuntal command, the two great concertos that represent the grandest contribution to the form since Beethoven, and the late Klavierstücke that are among the most intimate music he ever wrote. The piano was, for him, the instrument on which he thought — he composed at the keyboard and his piano writing has a quality of being thought through rather than merely performed.

Alfred Brendel's recording of the First Concerto in D minor Op. 15 with the Berliner Philharmoniker under Abbado CD 116 is one of the most admired accounts of one of the most demanding works in the repertoire. The concerto began as a symphony, was reworked as a sonata for two pianos, and only finally found its form as a concerto — which explains why the piano writing has a density and an orchestral weight that makes it physically exhausting to perform. Brendel meets the demands with complete authority, and Abbado provides orchestral support of equal quality. Misha Dichter's recording of the Second Concerto with Kurt Masur and the Gewandhaus CD 121 is paired with a Beethoven sonata: the two composers in juxtaposition illuminate each other clearly, Brahms's harmonic language taking the post-Beethoven tradition as its starting point and pushing it as far as it will go without breaking.

Jonathan Plowright's recording of the First Piano Sonata Op. 1 with the Handel Variations and Fugue CD 123 is a disc devoted to the young Brahms: the First Sonata announced himself as a pianist-composer of Beethoven's ambition in music of enormous scale and passionate rhetoric, while the Handel Variations show the contrapuntal discipline that would balance that rhetoric throughout his career. The fugue that concludes the Handel Variations is a statement of where Brahms was placing himself in the tradition — not merely Romantic but rooted in the German contrapuntal heritage going back through Beethoven to Bach himself.

The Romantic Virtuoso: Rachmaninov, Tchaikovsky and the Concerto Tradition

The nineteenth century produced a tradition of piano playing — and piano concerto writing — centred on the idea of the virtuoso as a figure of heroic dimensions: technically superhuman, physically commanding, capable of producing from the piano a range of sonority that rivalled the orchestra. Liszt established the template; Brahms resisted it while simultaneously embodying it; and Tchaikovsky and Rachmaninov brought it to its most popular and enduring expression.

Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky's First Piano Concerto in B flat minor is one of the most immediately recognisable opening gestures in all of music: that sequence of massive chords from the orchestra followed by the piano's sweeping cascade, establishing the key of D flat major (not the home key of B flat minor) with a grandeur that has made it the most performed piano concerto in the repertoire. Your collection holds it in three recordings that span sixty years of performance practice. Martha Argerich with the Berliner Philharmoniker under Abbado CD 450 is the most celebrated account of the modern era: incandescent, physically overwhelming, played at a tempo that sometimes defies belief but always generates electricity. Stephen Hough with the Minnesota Orchestra under Vänskä CD 455 provides a complete Tchaikovsky piano concerto survey — all three concertos, including the rarely performed Second and Third — with a combination of technical completeness and musical intelligence that is characteristic of everything Hough does. And Ivo Pogorelich with the London Symphony under Abbado CD 461 brings the same wilful individuality he applies to Chopin: slower in the lyrical passages than any other pianist, with a quality of brooding intensity in the slow movement that is completely his own.

Sergei Rachmaninov wrote four piano concertos, and all four are in your collection. Vladimir Ashkenazy's complete set with the London Symphony and André Previn CD 364 CD 365 was for a generation the standard recording: polished, technically accomplished, with the warmth of tone that Ashkenazy brought to everything. The Third Concerto in D minor has attracted particularly intense competition — it is the most technically demanding concerto in the standard repertoire, and the quality of a pianist's Third tells you a great deal about their ambitions. Vladimir Horowitz's recording with Ormandy and the New York Philharmonic CD 366 is a document from another era: Horowitz had a relationship with this concerto that was part autobiography (Rachmaninov himself had approved of Horowitz's playing), and his New York recording has a ferocity and a grandeur that remains unsurpassed. Evgeny Kissin with the London Symphony under Gergiev CD 363 brings a more modern brilliance: technically perfect, less overtly Romantic in temperament than Horowitz, but with a sense of structural command that reveals the concerto's architecture with unusual clarity. And Yuja Wang with the Simón Bolívar Symphony Orchestra under Dudamel CD 491 represents the current generation: a technique of such staggering completeness that the technical difficulties seem simply to disappear, leaving only the music.

Simon Trpčeski's recording of the First and Fourth Concertos with the Royal Liverpool Philharmonic CD 369 illuminates the less frequently performed extremes of Rachmaninov's concerto output: the First, an early work revised twice, with a youthful exuberance that the later concertos contain but do not always display; the Fourth, a more elusive and introspective work that has never achieved the popularity of the Second and Third.

Hélène Grimaud's recital disc coupling Chopin and Rachmaninov CD 191 and her broader mixed programme CD 86 show a pianist of unusual intellectual breadth: French by training, with a sound that is simultaneously precise and deeply coloured, she brings to the Russian Romantic repertoire a perspective shaped by the French analytical tradition rather than the Russian Romantic one.

The French School: Debussy, Ravel and Saint-Saëns

French piano writing from the late nineteenth century onwards developed a distinctive aesthetic: clarity of texture, a preference for tonal colour over harmonic weight, an emphasis on the piano's overtone spectrum and the way sustained notes blend together when the sustaining pedal is used. This is the pianistic world that the Impressionist movement in painting helped to shape, and it produced some of the most immediately beautiful keyboard music in the repertoire.

Walter Gieseking's recordings of Debussy's Préludes CD 154 remain the standard against which all subsequent accounts are measured. Made in the 1950s, they have a quality of tonal refinement and atmospheric imagination that has never been surpassed: Gieseking seems to locate the overtone characteristics of the music's specific harmonies and exploit them with the precision of a composer rather than a performer. His Debussy is not just played; it is realised in sound at the deepest level the recording technology of his time allowed. Yuja Wang's Ravel concertos with the Tonhalle-Orchester Zürich CD 492 bring the complete opposite of Gieseking's veiled mystery: Wang's Ravel is brilliant, precise, rhythmically incisive, finding in the G major Concerto's jazz-inflected outer movements exactly the kind of controlled exhilaration that Ravel intended. Stephen Hough's complete Saint-Saëns piano and orchestra works with the City of Birmingham Symphony Orchestra CD 380 covers a repertoire that has been unjustly neglected: Saint-Saëns as pianist-composer was one of the most formidable technicians of his era, and his five piano concertos contain music of elegant, sometimes brilliant quality.

The Twentieth Century Piano

The twentieth century produced piano music of extraordinary variety — from the neo-Baroque precision of Prokofiev to the tonal clusters of Henry Cowell, from the minimalist repetitions of Philip Glass to the transcendental complexity of Ligeti. Your collection captures several important strands of this variety.

Tatiana Nikolayeva's recording of Shostakovich's 24 Preludes and Fugues Op. 87 CD 434 has already been discussed in the Fugue article; but it belongs here too, as the central document of the Soviet piano tradition applied to a work of direct personal significance. Nikolayeva's additional disc of Borodin, Liadov and Prokofiev piano works CD 109 fills in the background of the Russian piano tradition from which Shostakovich emerged. Peter Donohoe's recording of the Prokofiev War Sonatas Nos. 6, 7 and 8 CD 362 gives us three of the most powerful piano sonatas of the century: written between 1939 and 1944, during the Soviet-German conflict, they combine Prokofiev's characteristic rhythmic violence with a new kind of savage energy that owes as much to the historical moment as to purely musical development. Lev Vinocour's disc of Prokofiev's own piano transcriptions CD 361 shows a different Prokofiev: the brilliant, witty arranger who took his own orchestral and ballet music and found its keyboard equivalent with the same inventive intelligence.

Yuja Wang's solo recital discs — the Transformation programme mixing Stravinsky, Scarlatti, Brahms and Ravel transcriptions CD 493, the Fantasia encore disc CD 495, and the Chopin/Scriabin/Liszt/Ligeti recital CD 496 — show the most complete technique available among contemporary pianists, deployed across a repertoire that ranges from the eighteenth century to the present. Wang plays everything with the same complete physical command, and the variety of her programming reflects an intelligence that is not content to specialise. The Stravinsky two-piano arrangements with Marc-André Hamelin and Leif Ove Andsnes CD 441 — the Rite of Spring and the Concerto for Two Pianos — are a reminder that the piano can accommodate music of orchestral complexity and rhythmic violence that the instrument was never designed for, if the players are of sufficient quality. John Lenehan's Philip Glass disc CD 499 offers the gentler face of the late twentieth century: Glass's piano music is essentially meditative, built on slowly evolving patterns that require a particular kind of focused listening to appreciate fully.

The Grieg Tradition: The Nordic Piano

Edvard Grieg occupies a unique position in piano literature: he wrote no symphony, no opera, no string quartet of lasting significance, but his piano music — particularly the ten books of Lyric Pieces — has a freshness and a national character that has made it beloved across generations. Leif Ove Andsnes, the finest Norwegian pianist of his generation, has recorded both the Lyric Pieces CD 189 and the Piano Concerto with the Berliner Philharmoniker under Jansons CD 192 in accounts of complete idiomatic authority. The concerto, composed when Grieg was twenty-five, remains the most popular Romantic piano concerto after Schumann's in A minor — which it closely resembles in its opening gesture — and Andsnes brings to it a combination of physical directness and structural clarity that makes it sound both perfectly natural and rigorously considered. Andsnes also appears in your collection in the remarkable Stravinsky two-piano disc with Hamelin CD 441 and the Haydn Piano Concertos with the Norwegian Chamber Orchestra CD 250, demonstrating the range of a musician who is equally at home in Classical, Romantic and twentieth-century repertoire.

The John Field Nocturnes CD 178 and complete Piano Concertos CD 184 played by Mícheál O'Rourke fill an important gap in the collection's picture of the Romantic piano. Field (1782–1837) was the Irish pianist who invented the nocturne — the slow, singing, ornamental piece for solo piano that Chopin took as his model for his own nocturnes, openly acknowledging the debt. Field's originals are less emotionally complex than Chopin's but have their own quiet beauty, and hearing them before the Chopin gives the latter a historical context that enriches the experience considerably. The Fauré Nocturnes played by Charles Owen CD 180 complete the nocturne sequence: three composers, three nationalities, three different approaches to the same form over nearly a century, all available in the collection.

Portrait Gallery: The Major Pianists

Murray Perahia (born 1947) appears in sixteen discs in this collection — more than any other musician — and represents perhaps the most completely satisfying piano style available in modern recording. American-born and trained, he studied with Mieczysław Horszowski and the harpsichordist Rosalyn Tureck, and his playing shows the influence of both: a naturally singing tone, a clarity of contrapuntal voice, and a historical awareness that informs but never dominates his interpretations. His Bach, Mozart and Schubert are all outstanding; his Beethoven is warm and serious; his Brahms is among the finest available. He plays both the keyboard and the conducting role in his recordings, and the combination of close musical thinking and physical engagement produces playing of exceptional integration. His Bach Partitas and English Suites CD 19 CD 20 CD 24, the Goldberg Variations CD 15, the Mozart complete concycles CD 286, and the Schubert Impromptus CD 384 CD 385 are all recordings of the very first quality.

Mitsuko Uchida (born 1948) is the finest Mozart pianist of her generation and one of the most profound Schumann interpreters available. Japanese-born, trained at the Vienna Academy, she combines an exceptional technical precision with a philosophical depth that makes her the natural successor to Alfred Brendel as the great analytical pianist of the current era. Her complete Mozart piano sonatas CD 294 are the standard recording; her Mozart concerto surveys with the English Chamber Orchestra CD 307 CD 308 CD 309 are deeply considered and consistently illuminating; her Schumann Fantasie and Davidsbündlertänze CD 411 is one of the finest Schumann recordings of the modern era; and her Schubert CD 388 has a quality of thoughtful engagement that reveals this often-underestimated repertoire at its deepest. Her mixed recital disc CD 291 gives a flavour of her range across multiple composers.

András Schiff (born 1953) is the most versatile pianist in the collection, appearing in Bach, Mozart, Schubert, Dvořák and the complete Beethoven music for piano and cello. Hungarian-born and trained in Budapest, he combines the Central European pianism tradition with a profound historical awareness and a natural musicality that makes his playing consistently beautiful without ever becoming merely ornamental. His four Mozart sonata discs, his two Goldberg Variations accounts CD 13 CD 14, and his Beethoven on Broadwood CD 93 are all recordings that belong in any serious collection.

Alfred Brendel (born 1931) was for four decades the dominant intellectual force in European piano playing — a thinker as much as a performer, whose written essays on music were as influential as his recordings. His Beethoven is the benchmark against which other accounts are measured: analytical, structurally transparent, with a quality of insight that comes from having thought very carefully about every bar. His Emperor Concerto CD 89, Brahms First Concerto CD 116, Haydn Sonatas CD 241 and Schubert Trout Quintet CD 390 are all essential.

Martha Argerich (born 1941) is the most electrifying pianist of her generation — technically overwhelming, temperamentally unpredictable, the only pianist whose live performances consistently generate the same physical excitement as a great athletic event. Her Chopin concertos CD 152, Tchaikovsky First CD 450, and Bach Cello Sonatas with Maisky CD 29 CD 39 are all recordings of legendary status. She has also largely retreated from solo recording in later years, preferring chamber music, which makes the earlier solo discs all the more valuable.

Radu Lupu (1945–2022) was the most naturally musical of all Romanian pianists — a figure of legendary reticence who rarely played in public and made relatively few recordings, but whose Schubert, Schumann and Brahms had a quality of absolute naturalness that made them the standard references in their respective repertoires. His Schumann disc CD 412 and his duo recordings with Perahia CD 287 CD 295 are among the collection's most treasured discs.

Vladimir Horowitz (1903–1989) was the defining piano virtuoso of the twentieth century: a technique of unparalleled brilliance combined with a personality of volcanic intensity. His recording of the Rachmaninov Third with Ormandy CD 366 and the Horowitz in Moscow recital CD 497 — the extraordinary 1986 return to the Soviet Union after sixty-one years of absence, Horowitz aged eighty-two playing a sold-out Moscow Conservatory to an audience that wept throughout — are both irreplaceable documents of what the piano can do in the hands of a player who has devoted an entire life to it.

Dinu Lipatti (1917–1950) died of leukaemia at the age of thirty-three, having had time to record only a small number of discs. But those discs — his Bach, his Mozart, his Schubert, his Chopin, all represented in your collection across two separate compilations CD 27 CD 498 — show a player of such natural musical intelligence and such complete technical command that his early death is among the greatest losses in the history of recorded music. His recording of Schubert's B flat Impromptu from his last public recital in 1950 — given while he was already gravely ill, and interrupted periodically while he received injections of cortisone — is one of the most moving documents in the piano discography.

Sviatoslav Richter (1915–1997) was possibly the most technically gifted pianist of the twentieth century — his range extended from Bach to Prokofiev, and his command of the instrument was so complete that he once sight-read, at a public recital, a substantial work he had never seen before. His recordings in your collection — the Well-Tempered Clavier CD 10, the Dvořák Concerto CD 144, the late Schumann and Brahms CD 415 — show three different aspects of an enormous musical personality.

Yuja Wang (born 1987) is the most technically accomplished pianist currently before the public — a combination of physical completeness and musical intelligence that places her in the tradition of Argerich and Horowitz. Her six discs in the collection CD 491 CD 492 CD 493 CD 494 CD 495 CD 496 show an unusual range for a young pianist: not just the expected Rachmaninov and Prokofiev but Ravel, Scarlatti, Brahms transcriptions, Ligeti études. She is, in a sense, the emblem of the current moment in piano playing: technically without limits, musically curious, culturally omnivorous.

Recommended Recordings Not in the Collection

Schubert: Piano Sonata in B flat major D.960 — Sviatoslav Richter
Various labels  ·  Single CD  ·  multiple recordings

The greatest piano sonata of the Romantic era, and the most significant gap in the collection's Schubert piano holdings. Richter's various live recordings of D.960 — particularly the 1972 Vienna account — are the definitive performances: slow, immense, with a quality of sustained inwardness in the first movement that makes even Uchida's fine studio recording seem slightly hurried. Brendel's three recordings are also excellent. This sonata should be the first Schubert piano addition to the wishlist.

Beethoven: Complete Piano Sonatas — Alfred Brendel
Philips  ·  9-disc box set  ·  recorded 1992–2001

Brendel recorded the complete Beethoven sonatas three times; this, his third and final cycle, is the summit of his Beethoven playing and arguably the finest complete cycle available. The late sonatas Op. 109, Op. 110 and Op. 111 in particular are magnificent: Brendel treats them as the profound philosophical statements they are, without sacrificing their purely musical character. Given the collection's exceptional depth in Beethoven, the complete sonata cycle seems the natural complement to the concerto recordings already present.

Chopin: Complete Mazurkas — Arthur Rubinstein
RCA Victor  ·  2-disc set  ·  1965–66

Arthur Rubinstein was Chopin's greatest interpreter — Polish-born, he understood the music's national roots from the inside, and his mazurkas have a rhythmic freedom and a quality of idiomatic rightness that no subsequent recording has matched. The mazurkas are the most essentially Polish of all Chopin's forms — stylised Polish dances with a characteristic rhythmic lilt — and they are the least represented form in the collection's Chopin holdings.

Brahms: Late Piano Pieces Opp. 116–119 — Radu Lupu
Decca  ·  Single CD

The late Brahms Klavierstücke — the four sets of intermezzos, capriccios and romances that he wrote in the last decade of his life — are among the most intimate piano music in the repertoire, and Lupu's Decca recording is universally regarded as the finest available. Given how well Lupu is represented elsewhere in the collection and how much his particular quality of naturalness suits this music, this is the most obvious single gap in the Brahms piano holdings.

Further Reading

Books
The Great Pianists
Harold C. Schonberg  ·  Simon & Schuster, 1963 (revised 1987)

The most readable survey of piano performance history from the early nineteenth century to the mid-twentieth, by the long-serving music critic of the New York Times. Schonberg writes with infectious enthusiasm and genuine knowledge about the personalities and playing styles of everyone from Liszt and Clara Schumann through to Horowitz, Rubinstein and Gould. Inevitably somewhat dated in its coverage of more recent pianists, but invaluable as a portrait of the tradition.

Night Music: A Journey Through Piano Music
John Bell Young  ·  Amadeus Press, 2007

A more recent survey focusing specifically on the repertoire rather than the performers: a composer-by-composer guide to the piano literature, with analytical commentary and recording recommendations. Particularly strong on the Romantic and early twentieth-century repertoire, and useful as a companion volume to Schonberg's performer-centred history.

Alfred Brendel on Music
Alfred Brendel  ·  A Cappella Books, 2001

A collection of Brendel's essays on music, covering Beethoven, Schubert, Schumann, Liszt and the nature of piano playing in general. Brendel is that rare thing, a great performer who is also a great writer about music, and these essays — precise, illuminating, occasionally acid — are the best single document of what it feels like to think about this repertoire from the inside of a performing life. His essays on the late Schubert piano sonatas and on the Beethoven Bagatelles are particularly outstanding.

Online
Gramophone — Piano Reviews
gramophone.co.uk

The Gramophone's piano review archive is one of the most comprehensive available, with comparative reviews of all major recordings going back several decades. Searching for any of the works or pianists mentioned in this article will produce multiple reviews that contextualise each recording within the broader discography. The Gramophone Editor's Choice and Recording of the Month designations are reliable guides to the best recent releases.

Hyperion Records — The Romantic Piano Concerto Series
hyperion-records.co.uk

Hyperion Records has been systematically recording the complete Romantic piano concerto literature since the 1980s, with over eighty volumes now available. The series covers dozens of composers whose concertos are virtually unknown — Sterndale Bennett, Hummel, Henselt, Moscheles, Ries, Alkan — and makes the case for a tradition much broader and richer than the standard half-dozen works. Your collection already includes two volumes; the series rewards systematic exploration.

An Explorer's Guide

The String Quartet: A Listener's Map

What to Seek Out Next  ·  From Haydn to Kurtág

What This Article Is For

The earlier articles on chamber music and on the great quartets of the last fifty years covered the form's history and its major ensembles. This article takes a different approach: a practical guide to the repertoire you haven't yet explored, composer by composer and work by work, with specific recording recommendations for each. Its purpose is to serve as a map for the territory still ahead — a wishlist with musical context.

Your existing quartet holdings are strong in certain areas and entirely absent in others. You have Beethoven extensively but only from Op. 59 onwards — the early and middle-period quartets are completely missing. You have Schubert in depth. You have the complete Shostakovich multiple times over. You have Haydn, Smetana, Vaughan Williams, Elgar, Mendelssohn, Ravel and Ligeti. What you do not have at all: Bartók (by far the most significant gap), Brahms, Dvořák, Janáček, Debussy, Schumann, Borodin, or the Viennese modernists. The journey ahead is long and consistently rewarding.

Haydn: The Mountains You Haven't Climbed Yet

Of the sixty-eight string quartets Haydn composed, only the six of Op. 76 are in your collection CD 240. The rest — representing the entire development of the form from its tentative beginnings through to its Classical perfection — constitute an unexplored mountain range of the highest importance.

The place to start is Op. 33, published in 1781 and the set in which Haydn claimed to have written in “an entirely new and special manner.” He was right. The six quartets of Op. 33 — nicknamed the “Russian” quartets because they were dedicated to the Grand Duke Paul of Russia — are the first in which all four voices are genuinely equal participants in the musical argument, the viola and cello given melodic material of real substance rather than merely supporting the two violins. The second quartet has the subtitle “The Joke” because of its peculiar finale, which begins what sounds like a final cadence several times before simply ending with a long silence while the audience waits for music that never comes. The third, “The Bird,” has a graceful, chirping first violin theme of complete charm. These are the works that directly inspired Mozart's six “Haydn” Quartets, composed in tribute after hearing Op. 33.

The Op. 20 quartets (“The Sun”) of 1772 are earlier and more experimental — written during Haydn's “Sturm und Drang” period, full of unexpected emotional intensity and minor-key turbulence — while the Op. 64 set includes the famous “Lark” quartet, whose first violin soars in its opening theme with the freedom of a bird in flight. And the Seven Last Words of Our Saviour on the Cross, originally an orchestral work arranged for string quartet, is a sustained meditation in seven slow movements of unusual depth and gravity. For all of this, the Kodály Quartet on Naxos — already represented in your collection for Op. 76 CD 240 — have recorded the complete Haydn quartets in an excellent complete series at modest cost. The Esterházy Quartet on Nimbus and the Angeles Quartet on Decca also offer outstanding accounts of individual sets.

Mozart: The Quartets You Have Missed

Your collection holds the Mozart string quintets, which are the summit of his chamber writing for strings, but none of the twenty-three string quartets. The six “Haydn” Quartets K.387, K.421, K.458, K.464, K.465 and K.499 are the essential ones. Mozart composed them between 1782 and 1785 and dedicated them to Haydn in one of the most remarkable dedications in musical history, describing them as “the fruit of long and laborious effort.” They are the works in which he absorbed and then surpassed everything Haydn had taught him about the form.

K.465, nicknamed “Dissonance,” is the most famous: its slow introduction begins in C minor with a sequence of unresolved dissonances that so alarmed contemporaries that some assumed the engraver had made errors. The dissonances are entirely deliberate, and when the Allegro bursts into C major the relief is physical. K.458, “The Hunt,” has an opening movement of infectious rhythmic energy; K.421 in D minor is Mozart's most emotionally searching quartet. These six works, along with the two later “Prussian” Quartets K.589 and K.590 (written for a cello-playing king and technically challenging for all four instruments), represent a repertoire of the highest quality that perfectly complements the quintets already in your collection.

The Quartetto Italiano, who play the quintets in your collection CD 304, also recorded a distinguished account of the complete Mozart quartets on Philips that shares their characteristic warmth and classical elegance. More recently the Mosaïques Quartet on Astrée has recorded the six “Haydn” quartets on period instruments with remarkable sensitivity. Either is an excellent starting point.

Beethoven: The Early and Middle Quartets

Your collection reaches Beethoven's quartet output at Op. 59 — the three “Razumovsky” Quartets that mark his full maturity. The six early quartets of Op. 18 (1800) and the two middle-period quartets Op. 74 “Harp” and Op. 95 “Serioso” remain entirely unexplored.

The Op. 18 quartets are remarkable works that should not be dismissed as merely “early Beethoven.” The first in F major, the fourth in C minor, and the sixth in B flat are genuinely ambitious pieces that already show the structural impatience that would define his mature style. The second, in G major, contains a slow movement that Beethoven himself described as depicting the vault scene from Romeo and Juliet — an unusual admission of extra-musical imagery. Op. 74 “Harp” gets its nickname from the pizzicato arpeggios in its first movement, which give the music an unusual textural lightness; its slow movement is one of his most purely beautiful. And Op. 95 “Serioso” is the most compressed and emotionally concentrated quartet he wrote — twelve minutes of music so dense with incident and feeling that it requires several hearings to begin to unpeel. The complete Beethoven cycles already recommended in the Quartets article — Takács on Decca, Emerson on DG, Belcea on Alpha — are of course the ideal approach, giving all sixteen quartets in sequence. But if the early quartets are the specific gap, the Alban Berg Quartett's EMI recordings of Op. 18 are the finest individual accounts available.

Schubert: The Rosamunde

Your Schubert quartet holdings are among the deepest in the collection — four recordings of Death and the Maiden, the G major D.887 in two accounts, the C major String Quintet twice, the Quartettsatz, and the late chamber string works. The one significant gap is the String Quartet No. 13 in A minor D.804, nicknamed “Rosamunde” because its slow movement is a set of variations on a theme from his incidental music to the play Rosamunde. Written in February 1824, it is the first major chamber work he wrote after the devastating diagnosis of syphilis two years earlier, and the emotional atmosphere is distinctive: more resigned and inward than either the Death and the Maiden or the G major, the lyricism slightly veiled, the emotional temperature cool rather than intense. The slow movement's variations unfold with a quality of patient, almost hypnotic beauty that places it among the most affecting of all his slow movements.

The Alban Berg Quartett's recording on EMI, the Takács on Decca, and the Lindsay account (which may already be present as the second work on one of your existing Schubert discs) are all outstanding. The work pairs naturally with the Death and the Maiden — two quartets written in the same month of the same year, from the same darkened emotional landscape, approaching the shadow from different angles.

Brahms: The Twenty-Year Project

The three Brahms string quartets are among the most underrated major chamber works in the repertoire. Brahms was the most rigorous self-critic in the history of composition: he destroyed more than twenty string quartets before he was satisfied enough to publish any. The three that survived — Op. 51 Nos. 1 and 2 (published 1873) and Op. 67 (1876) — represent a lifetime's accumulated discipline applied to the most demanding of chamber forms.

Op. 51 No. 1 in C minor is the darkest and most austere: dense in texture, serious in tone, with a sustained emotional weight throughout all four movements that makes it the Brahms quartet most comparable to late Beethoven. Op. 51 No. 2 in A minor is warmer, with a particularly beautiful slow movement in which the viola carries the main melody — characteristic of Brahms's tendency to give the less glamorous inner voices the most expressive material. Op. 67 in B flat major, the last quartet he wrote, is the most relaxed of the three and the most immediately appealing: its first movement has a quality of good-humoured energy, and its variations finale is among the most inventive finales in the quartet literature.

The Emerson String Quartet's Deutsche Grammophon recording is part of their complete Brahms chamber series and is the most technically accomplished account available. The Takács Quartet on Decca bring a warmer, more Central European temperament. The Jerusalem Quartet on Harmonia Mundi offer a more recent alternative of real quality. All three make a strong case for music that concert programmers and recording companies have consistently underserved.

Dvořák: The American and Its Companions

Antonín Dvořák's String Quartet No. 12 in F major Op. 96, universally known as “The American,” is the most beloved string quartet composed outside the Austro-German tradition — as immediately appealing as the New World Symphony it was written alongside, and considerably more personal. Composed in twelve days in June 1893 while Dvořák was spending the summer in Spillville, Iowa (a Czech-immigrant community), it is saturated with the landscapes and sounds of the American Midwest: wide-spanned pentatonic themes that Dvořák associated with Native American and African-American music, bird calls (the scarlet tanager he heard at Spillville appears in the second violin of the slow movement), and a quality of open-air spaciousness entirely unlike his European chamber music.

The first movement's main theme — that long, stepwise rising and falling line on the viola, then taken up by the first violin — is one of the most immediately recognisable openings in chamber music, and the rest of the quartet sustains the freshness and lyrical generosity of that opening throughout. The slow movement's bird-call episodes and the folk-inflected scherzo are equally memorable.

His Op. 51 in E flat major (1878) is a more overtly Czech work, with a Slavonic dance-influenced Furiant as its scherzo and a slow movement of comparable lyrical beauty to the American. The late Op. 105 in A flat major (1895), written immediately after the American, shows a Dvořák at the height of his powers in a more autumnal and complex mood. The Pavel Haas Quartet's Supraphon recording of the American, already recommended in the Quartets article, remains the finest currently available — the Czech ensemble bringing an idiomatic directness that no non-Czech group has quite matched.

Borodin: A Love Letter in Four Movements

Alexander Borodin's String Quartet No. 2 in D major (1881) occupies a peculiar place in the quartet literature: it is an unambiguous masterpiece that is simultaneously the most popular and the least studied of the Russian Romantic quartets. Popular because its third movement — the Notturno, a long-spun cello melody above softly undulating harmonies — has become one of the most immediately recognisable pieces of chamber music in the repertoire, used as the basis for the song “And This Is My Beloved” from the musical Kismet.
