EMI's financial difficulties led to a series of ownership changes: acquired by Terra Firma in 2007, restructured, and ultimately broken up in 2012 when Universal acquired the recorded music division. The classical catalogue passed to Warner Classics, which continues to release recordings from the archive under the EMI Classics brand.

Philips: The Dutch School

Philips Classics was the recording arm of the Dutch electronics giant, established in the late 1940s and built around a roster of distinguished artists that included Alfred Brendel, Colin Davis, Marriner and the ASMF, the Beaux Arts Trio, Grumiaux, Szeryng, and the complete Beethoven string quartets by the Italian Quartet. Its sound was characteristically warm and natural, with a preference for acoustic clarity over sonic spectacle. The Duo series — two discs for the price of one — and the Silver line budget reissues made the Philips back catalogue widely accessible.

Your collection holds 15 Philips CDs plus 11 of the Duo two-disc sets, making it the third most represented label after DG and EMI. The Mozart string quintets in two volumes CD 303 CD 304, the Quartetto Italiano Beethoven late quartets CD 78 CD 79, and the Julian Lloyd Webber cello concerto disc CD 523 are among the highlights. Philips was absorbed into Universal Music Group in 1998 and subsequently merged with Decca; the Philips Classics label was discontinued in 2013, though the catalogue remains available through Universal.

Pentatone, which appears in your collection with the Dichter/Masur Brahms SACD CD 121, was founded by former Philips executives in 2001 specifically to release the classic Philips analogue recordings in SACD transfers, using the original multi-channel master tapes. It represented a form of curatorial fidelity to the Philips heritage that the corporate succession had not guaranteed.

Sony Classical and CBS Masterworks

CBS Masterworks — the classical division of the Columbia Broadcasting System — had one of the most distinguished rosters in American recording history: Glenn Gould, Isaac Stern, Yo-Yo Ma, the Cleveland Orchestra under George Szell, the Columbia Symphony under Bruno Walter, Leonard Bernstein with the New York Philharmonic. When Sony acquired CBS Records in 1988 and renamed it Sony Classical, it acquired this extraordinary archive alongside the ongoing artist relationships.

Ten CBS Masterworks and ten Sony Classical CDs appear in your collection, covering the full range of the label's strengths. The CBS Masterworks catalogue in particular contains some of the most important recordings of the 1960s and 1970s: Bruno Walter's late Mahler and Beethoven; George Szell's razor-sharp Cleveland Beethoven and Brahms; Glenn Gould's uniquely idiosyncratic Bach. The Sony years brought continuing investment in new recordings alongside the reissue of the CBS heritage.

Hyperion: The Independent Ideal

Hyperion Records was founded in London in 1980 by Ted Perry, and it represents the most successful model of what an independent classical label can achieve with artistic integrity, editorial focus and a refusal to compete with the majors on their own terms. It has never had a major corporate owner; it operates from modest offices in south London; and it has produced, over four decades, one of the finest catalogues in classical recording.

Hyperion's particular strengths are romantic piano music (the Hyperion Schubert series, the Romantic Piano Concerto series, which currently extends beyond one hundred volumes), English choral and song repertoire, and specialist Baroque and Renaissance music. Its Helios budget imprint reissues catalogue recordings at mid-price with full documentation. With 19 main-label and 5 two-disc Hyperion CDs in your collection, it is the most significant independent label represented, including the Britten choral disc with Stephen Layton CD 113, the Finzi Clarinet Concerto with Robert Plane CD 183, the Stanford clarinet quintet disc with Thea King CD 507, the Boccherini String Quintets with Ensemble Explorations CD 111, and the Nash Ensemble Mozart/Brahms clarinet quintets CD 302. The label's presentation — detailed booklets, attractive design, consistently high recording quality — has made it a collector's favourite and a model for subsequent independent labels.

Chandos: The British Independent

Chandos Records was founded in 1979 in Colchester by Brian Couzens, initially as a vehicle for recordings by artists and ensembles that the major labels ignored. It grew rapidly through the 1980s and 1990s into one of the most important independent labels in the world, with particular strengths in British orchestral music, Romantic and twentieth-century repertoire, and choral music. Its recording quality — warm, spacious, with a natural acoustic that suited large-scale orchestral music — became one of its defining characteristics.

Chandos's house conductors — Neeme Järvi, Richard Hickox, Bryden Thomson, Vernon Handley — built systematic traversals of repertoire that the majors ignored: Bax, Rubbra, Alwyn, Bantock, the complete Vaughan Williams symphonies, the complete Walton orchestral works. The Walton film music disc with Marriner and the ASMF CD 481 and the Vaughan Williams Christmas choral music with Hickox and the City of London Sinfonia CD 486 are representative of Chandos at its best: repertoire that matters, performances of genuine commitment, recording quality that does justice to both. Seventeen main-label Chandos CDs appear in your collection.

Harmonia Mundi: The Early Music Label

Harmonia Mundi was founded in France in 1958 and became the most important dedicated early music label in the world, recording the pioneers of the historically informed performance movement — Leonhardt, Harnoncourt, Christie, Herreweghe, Savall — at a time when the major labels showed little interest. Its catalogue is unrivalled in breadth and consistency for music from the medieval period through the Baroque.

Seven main-label and six two-disc Harmonia Mundi CDs appear in your collection, reflecting its particular strength in the Baroque and early Classical repertoire. The label's aesthetic — natural acoustic, period instruments, scholarly documentation — influenced the entire classical recording industry's approach to old music. Harmonia Mundi USA and Harmonia Mundi France operated as effectively separate labels with overlapping catalogues; both appear in your collection.

Naxos: The Budget Revolution

Naxos was founded in Hong Kong in 1987 by Klaus Heymann, with a simple and radical proposition: release classical recordings of professional quality at budget price — initially around five dollars — by recording orchestras in Eastern Europe and Asia where costs were lower, and distribute them through mainstream retailers rather than specialist shops. The classical establishment greeted this with scepticism and occasional contempt; within a decade, Naxos had become the largest classical record label in the world by volume.

The quality of Naxos recordings has steadily improved since its early years, and its catalogue has expanded to include premium recordings alongside its original budget releases, as well as the Marco Polo imprint for rare repertoire and the Naxos Historical series for transfers of early recordings. With 24 CDs your collection, it is the second most represented label after DG — a reflection of the genuine value it offers: the Finzi Clarinet Concerto with Robert Plane CD 183, the Ireland piano works with John Lenehan CD 259, the Holst Planets with Dutoit CD 194 (a Gramophone Award winner transferred to the Naxos catalogue), and the Elgar orchestral miniatures with James Judd CD 175. Naxos demonstrated conclusively that price and quality need not be inversely related, and in doing so it permanently altered the economics of classical recording.

The Specialist Labels: BIS, ECM, Nimbus and Others

Beyond the major and semi-major labels lies a constellation of specialist independents, each with its own character and its own contribution to the recorded repertoire. BIS, a Swedish label founded in 1973, built its reputation on recordings of Scandinavian repertoire and on consistently high technical quality; it has been one of the most committed advocates for SACD, and its recordings of Sibelius, Nielsen and Nordic chamber music are widely regarded as definitive. Five BIS SACDs appear in your collection. ECM New Series, the classical imprint of the celebrated jazz label, is distinguished by a very specific aesthetic: sparse, precisely reverberant, with a quality of meditative attention that is either beautiful or austere depending on the listener. One ECM disc appears in the collection.

Nimbus Records, a British independent founded in 1971 in Wyastone Leys, Monmouth, was a pioneer of ambient recording techniques — using an “Ambisonics” approach designed to capture the full three-dimensional acoustic of the recording space. Its advocacy of British and twentieth-century repertoire, including the William Boughton Britten disc CD 131 and the Medici String Quartet Vaughan Williams CD 487, gave significant repertoire its most accessible recordings. The Priory label, specialist in British choral and organ music, is represented by the St George's Windsor Magnificat disc CD 534 — exactly the kind of niche repertoire that only a dedicated independent would have the conviction to record. The Soli Deo Gloria label, founded by John Eliot Gardiner to release his own Bach cantata cycle, represents the furthest development of this tendency: an artist-owned label created specifically to ensure that a single major project was documented on the artist's own terms.

Further Reading

Books
Sleeve Notes
Walter Legge  ·  ed. Anne Legge  ·  Duckworth, 1994

A posthumous collection of writings by the most influential producer in classical recording history, covering his relationships with Furtwängler, Karajan, Schwarzkopf, Callas and many others. Opinionated, incisive and occasionally savage, Legge's prose gives a vivid picture of the classical recording industry at its mid-century zenith, from the perspective of the man who shaped it more than any other.

The Gramophone Classical Music Guide
James Jolly (ed.)  ·  Gramophone, annual

The authoritative annual survey of the classical CD catalogue, with reviews of thousands of recordings organised by composer and genre. Invaluable for tracking the label affiliations of major recordings, identifying the best available version of any given work, and understanding which labels have been most significant for which repertoire. The Hall of Fame section provides a historical perspective on the most important recordings ever made.

Online
Discogs
discogs.com

The most comprehensive database of recorded music releases, with detailed information on every pressing, reissue and catalogue variant for virtually every recording ever commercially released. Indispensable for identifying specific pressings of classical CDs, tracking down information on label histories and catalogue numbers, and understanding the secondary market value of particular releases. The classical section has a dedicated community of contributors who document releases with exceptional thoroughness.

Presto Music
prestomusic.com

One of the finest online classical record shops, with a comprehensive catalogue of new releases and back-catalogue titles across all the major and specialist labels. The site's editorial coverage includes label profiles, artist interviews and curated recommendations that make it useful as a research tool as well as a retail destination.

String Quartets — Eight Composers

Brahms  ·  Borodin  ·  Schumann  ·  Debussy  ·  Ravel  ·  Janáček  ·  Bartók  ·  Stravinsky
This sheet covers eight composers who each made significant but relatively compact contributions to the string quartet repertoire — from Brahms's three late-Romantic masterpieces to Bartók's six works that represent the form's twentieth-century summit. Together they span roughly 140 years of quartet writing (1842–1994) and cover the full range from high Romanticism through Impressionism to Modernism. Composers are arranged in order of birth.
Robert Schumann (1810–1856) — 3 String Quartets Op. 41 Nos. 1–3  ·  all composed 1842  ·  dedicated to Felix Mendelssohn
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 41 No. 1 A minor — 1842 All three Op. 41 quartets were written in a single burst of creative energy in June–July 1842 — Schumann's 'chamber music year.' He had spent the preceding months studying the Beethoven and Mozart quartets intensively. The A minor opens with a slow introduction of brooding intensity before the energetic Allegro. The most dramatic of the three, with a concentrated emotional directness that is immediately characteristic
2 41 No. 2 F major — 1842 The most lyrical and relaxed of the three — four movements of warm, singing melody, with a particularly beautiful slow movement. The influence of Mendelssohn (the dedicatee) is most audible here. Often considered the most immediately accessible of the set and the best starting point for a listener new to Schumann's chamber music
3 41 No. 3 A major — 1842 The most ambitious and formally complex of the three — five movements, with an Adagio of unusual depth and a finale of considerable rhythmic energy. Widely regarded as the finest of the set and one of the great Romantic quartets. The slow movement's theme is one of Schumann's most memorable melodic inspirations. Frequently performed alongside the Beethoven and Brahms quartets in concert programmes
Schumann wrote no further string quartets after 1842. The three Op. 41 works stand alone in his output — a concentrated achievement that he never revisited, perhaps because the Piano Quintet Op. 44 (also 1842) seemed to him a more natural vehicle for his musical personality.
Johannes Brahms (1833–1897) — 3 String Quartets Op. 51 Nos. 1 & 2 · Op. 67  ·  1873–1876  ·  20 years in gestation
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 51 No. 1 C minor — 1873 Brahms is said to have written and destroyed over twenty string quartets before allowing these to be published. The C minor is the most intense and argumentative of the three — four movements of considerable density, with a first movement that drives relentlessly from its opening. The shadow of Beethoven's Op. 18 No. 4 (also C minor) lies over it, but Brahms's harmonic language and developmental technique are entirely his own
2 51 No. 2 A minor — 1873 Published simultaneously with No. 1, and in many ways its complement — more lyrical and introspective where No. 1 is dramatic and argumentative. The slow movement's extended singing melody is among Brahms's finest. The finale's cross-rhythms — three against two — are characteristic of his rhythmic complexity. Perhaps the most immediately appealing of the three quartets
3 67 B♭ major — 1876 The most unusual of the three — more playful and varied in character, with a hunting-horn theme in the first movement and a finale that is a set of variations on a theme presented first by the viola. The B♭ major key is warmer and more relaxed than the minor-key of the first two. Written two years after Op. 51 and showing a lighter touch; dedicated to Theodor Wilhelm Engelmann
Brahms wrote no further string quartets after Op. 67. Like Beethoven, he approached the form with extreme seriousness — and like Beethoven, he ultimately found the piano quartet and piano quintet a more natural vehicle. The three quartets are among the most challenging works in the Romantic repertoire for performers: technically demanding, rhythmically complex, and requiring a chamber ensemble of the highest calibre.
Alexander Borodin (1833–1887) — 2 String Quartets (+ unfinished No. 3) Nos. 1 & 2  ·  1874–1881  ·  composed alongside Prince Igor
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 — A major — 1874–79 Borodin was a professional chemist and amateur composer — one of 'The Five' nationalist Russian composers alongside Balakirev, Cui, Mussorgsky and Rimsky-Korsakov. The First Quartet shows the Beethoven influence acknowledged by Borodin himself — the opening theme is close to Beethoven's Op. 130 — but the Russian melodic character is unmistakable. A fluent and attractive work, less frequently performed than No. 2
2 — D major — 1881 One of the most beloved works in the quartet repertoire — warm, lyrical, and immediately accessible. The famous Nocturne (third movement), with its long cello melody over a pizzicato accompaniment, became the basis for the popular song 'And This Is My Beloved' in the musical Kismet (1953). The Scherzo bristles with energy; the finale dances with irresistible momentum. Borodin said it was written 'for his wife' and its emotional directness has won it a vast audience beyond the specialist chamber music world
3 — E♭ minor (frag.) Unfinished 1886–87 Left unfinished at Borodin's sudden death from a heart attack in February 1887. Two movements survive in draft. Occasionally performed in completions by other hands but rarely encountered in concert
Claude Debussy (1862–1918) — 1 String Quartet Op. 10 · G minor · 1893  ·  the only completed quartet
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 Op. 10 G minor — 1893 Debussy's only completed string quartet and one of the pivotal works of the late nineteenth century — the point at which French chamber music broke decisively from the German Romantic tradition. The four movements are linked by a single cyclic theme that appears in different transformations throughout. The second movement's pizzicato scherzo is one of the most original movements in the quartet literature; the slow movement anticipates the luminous, floating textures of the Prélude à l'Après-midi d'un faune of the same year. Dedicated to the Ysaÿe Quartet, who gave the premiere. Debussy planned a second quartet but never wrote it — a loss comparable to Schubert's abandonment of the Quartettsatz
The Debussy and Ravel quartets are almost always performed together on concert programmes — a natural pairing that has become one of the most popular double-bills in chamber music. The two works, separated by ten years, show the evolution from late Romanticism to full-blown Impressionism with remarkable clarity.
Maurice Ravel (1875–1937) — 1 String Quartet F major · 1903  ·  dedicated to Gabriel Fauré
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 — F major — 1903 Written when Ravel was twenty-eight, as a student of Fauré at the Paris Conservatoire. Fauré famously told him 'do not change a note' — remarkable advice from a conservative teacher to an avant-garde student. Like the Debussy quartet, it uses a cyclic theme across all four movements. The second movement's cross-rhythms between pizzicato and bowed strings are one of the most recognisable sounds in French chamber music. The slow movement has an improvisatory, almost cadenza-like quality; the finale builds to a brilliant conclusion. Ravel never wrote another quartet, apparently feeling he had said everything he needed to say in this one work
Leoš Janáček (1854–1928) — 2 String Quartets Kreutzer Sonata · Intimate Letters  ·  1923 & 1928  ·  late masterpieces
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 — — Kreutzer Sonata 1923 Inspired by Tolstoy's novella of the same name — a story of jealousy, passion and murder — and written when Janáček was sixty-eight, in the remarkable Indian summer of creativity that produced most of his major works. The music is characteristically angular and intense — short, fragmentary motifs rather than long melodies, abrupt dynamic contrasts, the kind of nervous energy that Czech critics called his 'speech-melody' technique, derived from the rhythms of spoken Czech. Nothing else in the quartet repertoire sounds remotely like it
2 — — Intimate Letters 1928 Janáček's last major work, completed two months before his death at seventy-four. A thinly veiled love letter in music to Kamila Stösslová, a married woman thirty-eight years his junior with whom he had been obsessively in love for eleven years. The four movements trace the emotional arc of this impossible attachment with a directness that borders on the confessional. Dedicated to Stösslová; she attended the premiere but apparently found the experience uncomfortable. The most personal and emotionally naked of all Janáček's works — and arguably his finest
Janáček's two quartets stand entirely outside the mainstream of quartet tradition — they owe nothing to the Viennese Classical heritage, to Brahms or Schumann, and very little even to Debussy or Ravel. Their closest relatives are perhaps the late Beethoven quartets in their refusal of conventional beauty and their willingness to be raw, abrupt and emotionally unguarded. Both are essential listening.
Béla Bartók (1881–1945) — 6 String Quartets Nos. 1–6  ·  1909–1939  ·  the twentieth century's greatest quartet cycle
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 Op. 7 A minor — 1909 Bartók's first mature quartet, written under the influence of Debussy and late Beethoven. A three-movement fugal structure shows his early interest in Renaissance and Baroque forms; the Hungarian folk music that would become central to his language is already present in the melodic contours. Substantial and serious but still rooted in the late Romantic tradition
2 Op. 17 — — 1917 Three movements — fast, slow, fast — showing Bartók's language becoming more dissonant and individual. The folk music influence is now fully integrated rather than decorative. Written during the First World War, a period of personal and artistic crisis. The first quartet that fully announces the mature Bartók style
3 — — — 1927 The most compressed of the six — a single movement in two main sections with a recapitulation, lasting about fifteen minutes. The most dissonant and technically demanding of the early quartets, with extended techniques (col legno, sul ponticello, glissandi) used throughout. Won first prize ex-aequo at the 1929 Philadelphia Musical Fund Society competition
4 — — — 1928 Five movements in a symmetrical arch form (ABCBA) — a structure Bartók used in several major works of this period. The central movement is a slow Lento, flanked by two scherzos, flanked by two outer movements of comparable character. The night-music textures of the third movement — quiet, fragmentary, unsettling — became one of Bartók's most distinctive sound worlds. Extended string techniques throughout, including the 'Bartók pizzicato' (snapping the string against the fingerboard). One of the cornerstones of the twentieth-century quartet repertoire
5 — — — 1934 Another five-movement arch structure — but warmer and more lyrical than No. 4, with a slow movement of sustained beauty and a scherzo of irresistible rhythmic drive. Written for the Coolidge Foundation; premiered by the Kolisch Quartet. Often paired with No. 4 in concert programmes as the two central pillars of the cycle. More immediately accessible than Nos. 3 and 4 without sacrificing any of the formal rigour
6 — — — 1939 The last and most autumnal of the six, written as Bartók prepared to flee Nazi-occupied Europe for America (he emigrated in 1940). Each of the four movements opens with a mesto (sad) introduction that grows progressively longer until the finale is nothing but the mesto theme, extended into a full slow movement of desolate beauty. A valediction to Europe and to the world he had known — comparable in its finality to Shostakovich's Fifteenth Quartet
Bartók's six quartets are to the twentieth century what Beethoven's are to the nineteenth: the works that define what the form is capable of, technically and expressively. The complete cycle runs to about two and a half hours in performance. The Takács Quartet (Decca) and Emerson String Quartet (DG) recordings are the most widely recommended modern cycles.
Igor Stravinsky (1882–1971) — 3 works for string quartet Three Pieces · Concertino · Double Canon  ·  1914–1959
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
— — — Three Pieces 1914 Three short movements of extreme individuality — the first a mechanical, hypnotic ostinato; the second a portrait of the eccentric English clown Little Tich; the third a chorale of ritualistic austerity. Written in the same year as The Rite of Spring. Unlike anything else in the quartet literature: the influence of Russian folk music and Stravinsky's own reinvention of rhythm are already fully formed
— — — Concertino 1920 A single-movement work of about six minutes, written for the Flonzaley Quartet. The first violin takes a concertante role against the other three instruments — hence the title. Neoclassical in idiom, with a tightly argued structure and the characteristic Stravinsky dry wit. Revised in 1952 for twelve instruments
— — — Double Canon 1959 A miniature — barely two minutes — written in memory of the painter Raoul Dufy. Stravinsky's late serial style in concentrated form: two simultaneous canons, one for violins and one for viola and cello, woven together with his characteristic precise economy. A memorial gesture of extraordinary concentration
Stravinsky never wrote a full-scale string quartet in the conventional sense — his three quartet works are all either miniatures or single movements. He is included here because the Three Pieces and Concertino regularly appear on quartet programmes alongside the standard repertoire, and their originality and brevity make them ideal programme companions.
Composers in order of birth: Schumann (1810) · Brahms (1833) · Borodin (1833) · Debussy (1862) · Ravel (1875) · Janáček (1854) · Bartók (1881) · Stravinsky (1882).
Total completed works: 21 quartets + 1 fragment (Borodin No. 3) + 2 single-movement/miniature works (Stravinsky). The essential listening from this sheet: Brahms Op. 51 No. 2 · Borodin No. 2 · Debussy Op. 10 · Ravel F major · Janáček Intimate Letters · Bartók Nos. 4 & 5.

Dmitri Shostakovich — Complete String Quartets

15 quartets in four groups  ·  1938–1974
Note on the quartets: Shostakovich's fifteen string quartets span thirty-six years, from 1938 to 1974 — three years before his death. They form the most significant body of quartet writing by any composer since Beethoven, and like Beethoven's late quartets they move progressively inward: from the relatively extrovert early works to the harrowing introspection of the final five. They are inseparable from the political context of their composition. Shostakovich lived his entire creative life under Soviet rule, working in the shadow of Stalin's terror, the catastrophic losses of the Second World War, and the post-war ideological campaigns that twice threatened to destroy his career. The quartets — unlike his more public symphonies — were his private journal: more candid, more unguarded, more personal than almost anything else he wrote. The key of E minor recurs with particular frequency and seems to carry a specific emotional charge. Quotations from his own earlier works appear throughout the later quartets, giving them an autobiographical dimension unique in the repertoire.

The standard performing ensemble is the Beethoven Quartet, who gave the premieres of Nos. 2–13 and maintained a close working relationship with the composer throughout his life. For recordings, the Fitzwilliam Quartet's complete cycle (Decca, 1970s) was the first Western recording, made with Shostakovich's participation; the Emerson String Quartet (DG) and Borodin Quartet (EMI/Teldec) cycles are the most widely recommended modern complete sets.
Nos. 1–3 — The Early Quartets 1938–1946  ·  3 quartets  ·  Craft and concealment
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 49 C major — 1938 Written in the immediate aftermath of the Fifth Symphony — the work that had rehabilitated Shostakovich after Stalin's denunciation of Lady Macbeth of Mtsensk in 1936. The First Quartet is deliberately modest and lyrical, almost Haydnesque in its good humour and formal clarity. A work of deliberate understatement by a composer learning to live with self-censorship. Premiered by the Glazunov Quartet in Leningrad, 1938
2 68 A major — 1944 Written during the Second World War — the year of the Leningrad siege. More ambitious than No. 1, with a long, ruminative first movement and a waltz-like third movement that carries darker undertones than its surface suggests. The Beethoven Quartet gave the premiere in November 1944. The influence of Bartók's quartets — which Shostakovich greatly admired — begins to show
3 73 F major — 1946 The most substantial of the early quartets — five movements, markedly more complex and emotionally wide-ranging than its predecessors. The programme (deliberately concealed from Soviet authorities) follows the course of the Second World War: calm before the storm, invasion, resistance, the dead, and finally peace — but a peace shadowed by memory. One of the most overtly dramatic quartets before the late period
The first three quartets establish the formal framework Shostakovich would use throughout: multi-movement structures, a preference for moderate tempos, and a surface often more lyrical and well-mannered than the emotional content requires. The concealment of personal or political meaning beneath an acceptable surface was a survival strategy he had perfected after 1936.
Nos. 4–9 — The Middle Period 1949–1964  ·  6 quartets  ·  Terror, grief and sardonic humour
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
4 83 D major — 1949 Written immediately after the Second Zhdanov Decree of 1948, which attacked Shostakovich and other leading composers for 'formalism' — and then suppressed for three years, not premiered until 1953. Uses Jewish musical idioms throughout, a conscious act of solidarity with persecuted Soviet Jews at a time of state anti-Semitism. The sardonic, double-edged quality that will characterise the middle-period quartets appears here fully formed
5 92 B♭ major — 1952 A more inward and lyrical work than No. 4, with a long, searching slow movement at its centre. Written in the final year of Stalin's rule and coloured by the atmosphere of fear and exhaustion that pervaded Soviet cultural life. The finale's brittle cheerfulness feels deliberately unconvincing — a mask over deeper unease
6 101 G major — 1956 Written three years after Stalin's death, in the relative thaw of the Khrushchev era. More relaxed and lyrical than its immediate predecessors, though the characteristic Shostakovich irony is never far from the surface. The passacaglia slow movement is one of the finest in his quartet output
7 108 F♯ minor — 1960 Dedicated to the memory of his first wife Nina Varzar, who had died in 1954. The shortest of the quartets — three movements, barely twelve minutes — but among the most concentrated. The work ends with a fugue of remarkable austerity. Premiered by the Beethoven Quartet in 1960, the year Shostakovich reluctantly joined the Communist Party under pressure
8 110 C minor — 1960 The most performed and recorded of all fifteen quartets. Written in three days in Dresden while Shostakovich was working on the film score for Five Days, Five Nights. Saturated with self-quotation — themes from the First Symphony, the Piano Trio, the Cello Sonata, and other works — and woven around his musical monogram DSCH (D–E♭–C–B). Dedicated 'to the memory of the victims of fascism and war' but widely understood as also a valedictory self-portrait. Shostakovich apparently intended it as a suicide note; he later said he was 'ashamed' of it for that reason
9 117 E♭ major — 1964 Five movements of considerable complexity, including a set of variations as the final movement. More ambiguous in mood than No. 8 — quieter and more lyrical, but with an undertow of unease. The dedication is to his third wife, Irina Supinskaya, whom he had recently married. Premiered by the Beethoven Quartet in 1964
The middle-period quartets coincide with the most turbulent years of Shostakovich's political life — the 1948 Zhdanov Decree, Stalin's death, the Hungarian uprising, and his forced Party membership of 1960. No. 8 is the pivot of the entire cycle: the work in which autobiography and self-quotation become primary compositional materials, pointing forward to the entirely introspective world of the late quartets.
Nos. 10–13 — The Late Quartets I 1964–1970  ·  4 quartets  ·  Deepening inwardness
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
10 118 A♭ major — 1964 Written in the same year as No. 9, dedicated to Moisei Weinberg — the Polish-Jewish composer who was one of Shostakovich's closest friends and greatest admirers. Four movements of spare, searching lyricism; the slow movement contains some of the most nakedly expressive writing in any of the quartets. The beginning of the final creative phase
11 122 F minor — 1966 Dedicated to Vasily Shirinsky of the Beethoven Quartet, who had died that year. Seven short movements played without a break — a formal innovation that points toward the even more radical structures of the final quartets. The work is elegy throughout, the seven movements functioning as seven aspects of grief. One of the most formally original of the fifteen
12 133 D♭ major — 1968 Dedicated to Dmitri Tsyganov, first violinist of the Beethoven Quartet. Two movements of extreme contrast: a long, slow, almost motionless first movement and a restless, dissonant second. The twelve-tone row that opens the work — remarkable for a composer who had always resisted serialism — gradually dissolves into more characteristic Shostakovich. A work of quiet but unsettling strangeness
13 138 B♭ minor — 1970 A single movement of approximately eighteen minutes — the most radical formal departure in the cycle. No themes in the conventional sense; instead, a continuous unfolding of textures, silences and fragmentary ideas. The viola is given particular prominence — unusual in the quartet literature. Dedicated to Vadim Borisovsky, the Beethoven Quartet's violist. Among the most austere and demanding works in the entire repertoire
Nos. 14–15 — The Final Quartets 1973–1974  ·  2 quartets  ·  Valediction
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
14 142 F♯ major — 1973 Dedicated to Sergei Shirinsky, cellist of the Beethoven Quartet — the last surviving member of the original ensemble. Three movements of extraordinary restraint and transparency; the textures are often reduced to two or even one line. The cello is given unusual prominence, as if in tribute to the dedicatee. A work of extreme economy, every note precisely weighted
15 144 E♭ minor — 1974 The last completed work Shostakovich wrote before his death in August 1975. Six Adagio movements played without a break — all slow, all in the same key, all without the sardonic humour or rhythmic energy that had characterised even the darkest earlier quartets. An elegy without consolation. The final movement ends in total stillness. It is one of the most uncompromising valedictions in Western music — comparable in its finality to Beethoven's Op. 135
Shostakovich died on 9 August 1975. He had composed the Fifteenth Quartet the previous year, when he was already gravely ill with the lung cancer and progressive muscular weakness that would kill him. In his final decade he had written six quartets — each more inward, more austere, more formally stripped than the last. The Fifteenth, with its six consecutive Adagios, represents a complete withdrawal from the world: music that has nothing left to prove and nowhere left to go. It is among the most devastating endings to any large body of work in the chamber repertoire.
Total: 15 string quartets, Op. 49–144, 1938–1974. All dedicated to members of the Beethoven Quartet (from No. 2 onwards) or close friends.
Recommended complete cycles: Fitzwilliam Quartet (Decca, 1970s — with the composer's involvement)  ·  Borodin Quartet (EMI/Teldec)  ·  Emerson String Quartet (DG)  ·  St Petersburg Quartet (Hyperion)

Antonín Dvořák — Complete String Quartets

14 quartets in four groups  ·  1862–1895
Note on numbering: Dvořák's quartets are numbered 1–14 in the standard catalogue but the numbering does not always reflect the composition order, since some early works were not published until after his death and others were posthumously renumbered. The opus numbers are the most reliable guide, supplemented by the B. numbers from the Burghauser catalogue (the standard Dvořák thematic catalogue, published 1960). The first five quartets are early apprentice works rarely encountered in concert; the essential repertoire begins with No. 9 Op. 34 and culminates in the three late masterpieces — Nos. 12, 13 and 14.

The American Quartet (No. 12, Op. 96) is one of the most beloved and frequently performed works in the entire chamber repertoire. No. 13 Op. 106 and No. 14 Op. 105 (written in reverse order) complete a late trilogy of equal distinction but much less frequently played.
Nos. 1–5 — The Apprentice Quartets 1862–1870  ·  5 quartets  ·  Early works, rarely performed
No.Op. / B.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 B. 8 A major — 1862 Dvořák's first attempt at the quartet form, written when he was twenty-one and working as a violist in the Bohemian Provisional Theatre orchestra. Unpublished in his lifetime. The influence of Beethoven and Schubert is strong, but the melodic invention already shows an unmistakably Czech character. Rarely performed; more of historical than musical interest
2 B. 17 B♭ major — 1869 A more ambitious work than No. 1, showing Dvořák beginning to absorb the influence of Wagner and Liszt, whose music he had encountered through his theatre work. Also unpublished in his lifetime. The five-movement structure — unusually expansive for a string quartet — reflects his growing formal ambition
3 B. 18 (posth.) D major — 1870 One of three quartets composed in 1870, a year of extraordinary productivity. The Romantic influence of Wagner is still present but Dvořák is beginning to find his own language. Posthumously published
4 B. 19 (posth.) E minor — 1870 The most dramatic of the early quartets, with a turbulent first movement that looks forward to the expressive world of the mature works. Still unpublished in Dvořák's lifetime but occasionally revived by ensembles interested in the complete survey
5 Op. 9 / B. 37 F minor — 1873 The first quartet Dvořák published in his lifetime and the first to receive a public performance. A significant step forward: four well-proportioned movements showing real command of the form. The Czech folk element is now more consistently present. Still rarely performed, but worth seeking out as the bridge between the juvenilia and the mature style
The first five quartets are the work of a composer still forming his language. Dvořák destroyed several early works he considered beneath his standard; that these survived suggests he valued them as records of his development even when he judged them not ready for the public. The essential Dvořák quartet repertoire begins with No. 8.
Nos. 6–11 — The Middle Period 1873–1882  ·  6 quartets  ·  Growing mastery; the Brahms influence
No.Op. / B.KeyNicknameYearNotes
6 Op. 12 / B. 40 A minor — 1873 Written in the same year as No. 5. The minor key gives it a more intense character; the slow movement in particular shows a new emotional directness. Still unpublished in his lifetime but occasionally revived. Dvořák was by now earning a modest living as a church organist in Prague and beginning to receive recognition for his larger works
7 Op. 16 / B. 45 A minor — 1874 The year Brahms first noticed Dvořák's talent, recommending him to his publisher Simrock. The influence of Brahms — the most important single influence on Dvořák's middle-period chamber music — is increasingly evident: dense textures, motivic development, and a structural seriousness that marks a clear advance on the earlier works
8 Op. 80 / B. 57 E major — 1876 The first Dvořák quartet to enter the regular repertoire. Published late (as Op. 80, to confuse the chronology of his output at Simrock's commercial instigation), it is a warmly lyrical work of real distinction — the slow movement in particular has the unhurried, singing quality that will characterise the late masterpieces. Well worth seeking out
9 Op. 34 / B. 75 D minor — 1877 The first quartet Dvořák himself considered fully mature. Dedicated to Brahms, who had by now become his friend and champion. Four substantial movements of assured craftsmanship; the Czech folk element is integrated rather than decorative. The first quartet that would not be out of place on a serious concert programme alongside the late works
10 Op. 51 / B. 92 E♭ major Slavonic 1879 Sometimes called the 'Slavonic' quartet for its pervasive use of Czech and Slavonic folk idioms. The dumka slow movement — a Ukrainian folk form alternating between elegy and dance that Dvořák made distinctively his own — appears here for the first time in a quartet. One of his most immediately appealing chamber works; the most frequently performed of the middle-period quartets
11 Op. 61 / B. 121 C major — 1882 Written for the Hellmesberger Quartet in Vienna — the leading professional ensemble of the day — and his most publicly ambitious quartet to date. Large in scale, technically demanding, and formally confident. The slow movement's long-breathed melody is one of the finest things in the middle-period works. A significant step towards the late trilogy
The middle-period quartets coincide with Dvořák's emergence as an internationally celebrated composer. The friendship with Brahms, the Simrock publications that brought his music to wider European audiences, and the Stabat Mater's success in England all contributed to the confidence and ambition evident in these works. After No. 11 (1882), Dvořák wrote no string quartets for nine years.
No. 12 — The American Quartet 1893  ·  1 quartet  ·  The most beloved quartet since Schubert's Death and the Maiden
No.Op. / B.KeyNicknameYearNotes
12 Op. 96 / B. 179 F major American 1893 Written in a fortnight at Spillville, Iowa, during the summer of Dvořák's first year as director of the National Conservatory of Music in New York. The nickname reflects the American folk music — particularly African-American spirituals and Native American songs — that Dvořák had absorbed during his time in the United States, though the work is equally rooted in Czech melodic thinking. The pentatonic opening theme of the first movement has one of the most instantly recognisable beginnings in all chamber music. The slow movement's famous oboe-like melody in the cello, accompanied by a bird-call figure in the violin, was allegedly inspired by the scarlet tanager Dvořák heard outside his window at Spillville. The scherzo is irresistible; the finale dances with the energy of a furiant. Written at a moment of intense creative happiness and it shows on every page — among the most naturally joyful pieces ever written for quartet. One of the ten most recorded works in the entire chamber repertoire
The American Quartet has become so popular that it has somewhat overshadowed the two quartets that followed it — Nos. 13 and 14 — which are of equal stature but rarely reach the concert platform. The three works form a late trilogy comparable to Schubert's three late quartets: each complete in itself, each illuminating the others.
Nos. 13–14 — The Final Quartets 1895  ·  2 quartets  ·  The undervalued masterpieces
No.Op. / B.KeyNicknameYearNotes
13 Op. 106 / B. 193 G major — 1895 Written first but published second (after Op. 105). Dvořák had returned to Bohemia from America and was in a period of intense creative activity. The G major quartet is the most expansive and outwardly warm of the final three — four substantial movements, richly scored, with an extraordinary slow movement of long, arching melodies and a finale of tremendous rhythmic energy. Regularly cited by performers as their favourite Dvořák quartet; rarely reaches the concert hall. Deserves far wider recognition
14 Op. 105 / B. 192 A♭ major — 1895 Written second but published first — hence the lower opus number. More autumnal in character than No. 13, with a slow movement of deep, almost Brahmsian gravity and a first movement of unusual harmonic richness. The finale returns to the dance energy of the American Quartet but with a more complex emotional undertow. Dvořák's last completed string quartet and a worthy conclusion to the most distinguished body of Czech chamber music ever written
Dvořák died on 1 May 1904, aged 62, having composed no further string quartets after Op. 106 in 1895. His final decade was devoted primarily to opera — Rusalka (1900) in particular — and he left several chamber projects unfinished. The fourteen quartets span thirty-three years and trace one of the most complete and satisfying arcs in the chamber repertoire: from apprentice imitation to full individual mastery.
B. numbers from the Burghauser thematic catalogue (1960), the standard Dvořák catalogue. Total: 14 string quartets, 1862–1895. The essential repertoire: No. 10 Op. 51 (Slavonic) · No. 12 Op. 96 (American) · No. 13 Op. 106 · No. 14 Op. 105.
Recommended recordings: Amadeus Quartet (DG) · Prague Quartet (Supraphon) · Vlach Quartet (Naxos) · Pražák Quartet (Praga/Hyperion)

Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart — Complete String Quartets

23 quartets in five groups  ·  1770–1790
Note on numbering: Mozart’s quartets are identified by their Köchel (K.) catalogue number, which is more reliable than the sequential quartet numbering (No. 1–23) used in some editions. The sequential numbers given here follow the standard Köchel chronological order. The six ‘Haydn’ Quartets (K.387–465) and the three ‘Prussian’ Quartets (K.575–590) are the heart of the repertoire; the earlier sets are apprentice and transitional works of variable quality. Note: Mozart’s string quintets (K.174, 406, 515, 516, 593, 614) are not included here — they form a separate and equally important body of work.
First Quartet  —  K. 80 1770  ·  1 quartet  ·  Written in Lodi, Italy, aged 14
No.K.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 80 G major — 1770 Written in a single evening at a Lodi inn during the Italian journey. Originally in three movements; a fourth (Rondeau) added later. Charming but slight — a fourteen-year-old’s work
Italian Quartets  —  K. 155–160 1772–1773  ·  6 quartets  ·  Written in Milan and Bolzano
No.K.KeyNicknameYearNotes
2 155 (134a) D major — 1772 Six quartets written during the third Italian journey, probably for performance in Milan. Light, divertimento-style writing in three movements. Cheerful and technically undemanding
3 156 (134b) G major — 1772
4 157 C major — 1772–73 Begins to develop more expressive slow movements
5 158 F major — 1772–73
6 159 B♭ major — 1773
7 160 (159a) E♭ major — 1773 Most ambitious of the Italian set; slow movement shows growing depth
Vienna Quartets  —  K. 168–173 1773  ·  6 quartets  ·  Written in Vienna, aged 17
No.K.KeyNicknameYearNotes
8 168 F major — 1773 Written after hearing Haydn’s Op. 20 in Vienna — a direct response and learning exercise. Now fully in four movements. The most ambitious quartets Mozart had yet written
9 169 A major — 1773
10 170 C major — 1773
11 171 E♭ major — 1773 Contrapuntal finale shows Haydn’s influence
12 172 B♭ major — 1773 Minuet with canon; growing formal ambition
13 173 D minor — 1773 Only minor-key quartet among the Vienna set; most intense and emotionally searching. Fugal finale directly modelled on Haydn Op. 20. The finest of the early quartets
After K.173 (1773), Mozart wrote no string quartets for nine years. When he returned to the form in 1782, it was on an entirely different level.
Six ‘Haydn’ Quartets  —  K. 387, 421, 428, 458, 464, 465 1782–1785  ·  6 quartets  ·  The summit of the form
No.K.KeyNicknameYearNotes
14 387 G major Spring 1782 Dedicated to Haydn in a celebrated preface describing them as “the fruit of long and laborious effort.” The fugal finale of K.387 directly honours Haydn’s Op. 20. Mozart called these six works his finest chamber music
15 421 (417b) D minor — 1783 Mozart’s most emotionally searching quartet. Unusually intense throughout; the variations finale one of his most original movements. Constanze was reportedly in labour during its composition
16 428 (421b) E♭ major — 1783 Often underrated; slow movement of great chromatic richness and depth
17 458 B♭ major The Hunt 1784 Opening theme’s horn-call rhythm in compound time gives the nickname. The most immediately appealing of the six; outgoing and joyful in character
18 464 A major — 1785 Beethoven copied out the entire score by hand as a study. The most contrapuntally complex of the set; variations movement of exceptional inventiveness
19 465 C major Dissonance 1785 Slow introduction of extraordinary chromatic density — contemporaries assumed engraving errors. The dissonances are entirely deliberate, resolved by the sunlit Allegro. Haydn himself heard it and said: “Before God and as an honest man, I tell you that your son is the greatest composer known to me.”
Haydn heard the six dedicated quartets at a private performance in February 1785, with Mozart playing viola and Leopold Mozart (visiting Vienna) in the audience. His famous tribute to Leopold followed immediately afterwards.
Hoffmeister Quartet  —  K. 499 1786  ·  1 quartet  ·  Commissioned by the publisher Franz Anton Hoffmeister
No.K.KeyNicknameYearNotes
20 499 D major Hoffmeister 1786 Named after its publisher; stands alone between the ‘Haydn’ and ‘Prussian’ sets. More lyrical and less contrapuntally dense than its predecessors. Minuet of exceptional elegance; finale unusually relaxed and song-like. Unjustly overshadowed by the two flanking sets
After K.499 (1786) Mozart again wrote no string quartets for three years, turning his energies to the piano concertos, Le Nozze di Figaro, Don Giovanni, and the three last symphonies. The Prussian set of 1789–90 was never completed as planned.
Three ‘Prussian’ Quartets  —  K. 575, 589, 590 1789–1790  ·  3 of a planned 6  ·  Mozart’s last chamber works
No.K.KeyNicknameYearNotes
21 575 D major — 1789 Commissioned by King Frederick William II of Prussia, an amateur cellist — hence the prominent, technically demanding cello parts throughout all three. K.575 is the most immediately lyrical; slow movement of pellucid beauty
22 589 B♭ major — 1790 Cello given prominent singing lines throughout. Minuet of particularly refined elegance. Often considered the most perfectly proportioned of the three
23 590 F major — 1790 The last string quartet Mozart completed. Finale combines a searching D minor episode with a delightfully light F major conclusion. The three Prussian quartets as a set show a style increasingly spare and refined — less note, more meaning. Mozart died the following year, aged 35
Mozart was commissioned to write six Prussian quartets but completed only three. He wrote to a friend in 1790: “I am composing a little for some money which I badly need.” The three completed quartets are among the most refined music he ever wrote.
K. = Köchel catalogue number (the standard Mozart catalog, named after Ludwig von Köchel, 1862; revised several times thereafter). Total: 23 string quartets, 1770–1790. Not included: String Quintets K.174, 406, 515, 516, 593, 614 (a separate and equally important series). The six ‘Haydn’ Quartets (K.387–465) are universally regarded as the essential Mozart chamber repertoire for string quartet.

Joseph Haydn — Complete String Quartets

68 quartets in 16 opus groups  ·  c.1757–1803
Note on numbering: Haydn's quartet numbers vary between editions. This table uses the Hoboken catalogue (Hob.III:) as the definitive reference. Opus-internal numbering is inconsistent across sources. Op. 2 Nos. 3–4 (Hob.III:9–10) are excluded as spurious. Op. 3 (attributed to Romanus Hofstetter, not Haydn) is excluded. The Seven Last Words Op. 51 is a special arrangement of an orchestral work and is listed separately.

Why Haydn matters: Haydn did not invent the string quartet, but he defined it. Over forty-five years and 68 works he transformed a domestic entertainment into the most demanding and intimate form in Western chamber music — the medium in which composers from Mozart and Beethoven to Bartók and Shostakovich would later do their most searching and personal work. Mozart dedicated his six greatest quartets to Haydn; Beethoven studied his scores exhaustively. The Op. 33 set of 1781 — the first in which Haydn achieved full mastery of the form — was the model from which everything subsequent derived. The Op. 76 Erdődy quartets of 1797–99 remain his supreme achievement and among the greatest works ever written for the medium.
Op. 1 — Six Quartets (Divertimenti) c.1757–1762  ·  6 quartets  ·  Five movements each; origins in serenade tradition
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
11B♭ major—c.1757Haydn's earliest quartet writing — five-movement divertimenti closer to serenades than true quartets. Written for Baron Fürnberg, at whose country estate Haydn was employed. The first violin is dominant throughout; the other three parts are essentially accompaniment. Charming and unpretentious
22E♭ major—c.1757Follows the same pattern of two minuets flanking a slow movement and finale. The Op. 1 set was popular enough that Haydn's publisher later added two more works to the set that were not originally part of it
33C major—c.1757The most vivacious of the set; the first violin's singing melody in the Adagio already shows the lyrical gift that will characterise the mature quartets
44D major—c.1757Occasionally performed in complete Haydn surveys; otherwise rarely encountered. The five-movement structure was abandoned in later opus groups in favour of the four-movement plan that became standard
55G major—c.1762The last of the set in composition order. Haydn's own later assessment of these early works was modest; he regarded Op. 33 as his true beginning in the quartet form
Op. 2 — Four Quartets c.1760–1765  ·  4 quartets (Nos. 3–4 spurious, excluded)
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
16A major—c.1760Still in the five-movement divertimento format. A Haydn biographer has described these works as 'the larval stage of the mature quartet' — the formal instinct is there but the conversational equality between the four voices has not yet arrived
27E major—c.1760Unusual key for the period; the E major slow movement has a quality of slightly mysterious lyricism
58B♭ major—c.1765Hob.III:8; the most accomplished of the Op. 2 set and the one most likely to appear in concert. Beginning to move towards the four-movement plan
611D major—c.1765Hob.III:11. Sometimes classified elsewhere in the catalogue. The early numbering of Haydn's works was chaotic; the Hoboken catalogue brought order to a confused situation
Op. 9 — Six Quartets c.1769–1770  ·  6 quartets  ·  The four-movement plan established
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
113C major—c.1769The Op. 9 set marks a decisive step forward. The five-movement divertimento structure is abandoned for good; four movements become the norm. The cello and viola are beginning to acquire real independence from mere accompaniment — the embryo of the 'four equal voices conversing' ideal that Haydn would fully realise in Op. 33
214E♭ major—c.1769The slow movement is notably more developed than anything in the earlier sets — Haydn was now fully employed at the Esterházy court and had access to skilled professional players who could realise more demanding writing
315G major—c.1769Spirited and fluent; the minuets are becoming more characterful and less formulaic
416D minor—c.1769One of the earliest minor-key quartets — the emotional intensity of D minor pushes Haydn into more dramatic territory than he had previously explored in the form
517B♭ major—c.1769Often considered the finest of the set; the slow movement's extended singing melody anticipates the great Adagios of Op. 33 and Op. 76
618A major—c.1770Closes the set with characteristic good humour; the finale's perpetual motion has a clockwork energy that became a Haydn fingerprint
Op. 17 — Six Quartets 1771  ·  6 quartets  ·  The 'Sturm und Drang' influence
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
125E major—1771Written in Haydn's 'Sturm und Drang' period — a brief but intense phase of emotional intensity and dramatic contrast that also produced the 'Farewell' Symphony. The Op. 17 quartets are more emotionally wide-ranging than their predecessors, with minor-key passages of real urgency
226F major—1771The Sturm und Drang influence is particularly audible in the development sections, which are more dramatically charged than anything Haydn had written before
327E♭ major—1771The most serene of the set; the slow movement has a Baroque grandeur that recalls the influence of C.P.E. Bach, whose music Haydn greatly admired
428C minor—1771The minor key allows Haydn to explore a more troubled emotional world; the first movement's angular theme and driving rhythms are among the most forward-looking things he had yet written
529G major—1771Returns to a more relaxed world; the finale's wit and rhythmic playfulness are characteristic of the lighter side of Haydn's personality that even the Sturm und Drang period never entirely suppressed
630D major—1771Closes the set confidently; the cello is becoming noticeably more independent, anticipating the fully conversational texture of Op. 33 a decade later
After Op. 17 (1771) Haydn wrote no string quartets for ten years. The Op. 20 set of 1772 stands apart — a group of six works of such originality that they are treated separately. Then a silence until 1781, when Op. 33 appeared and changed everything.
Op. 20 — Six Quartets 'Sun Quartets' 1772  ·  6 quartets  ·  The first masterpieces
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
131E♭ major—1772The 'Sun' nickname comes from a later publisher's title page showing a sunrise. These six quartets are the first works in which Haydn achieves something close to the ideal of four equal voices in genuine conversation — the cello in particular is given thematic material for the first time, rather than mere bass-line support
232C major—1772The range of emotional expression is unprecedented: from the grave opening of No. 1 to the playful finales, from chromatic slow movements to fugal finales. Brahms said these quartets showed him 'what it was possible to do with four instruments'
333G minor—1772One of the most dramatic works of the set — the minor key unleashes an emotional intensity comparable to the C minor Symphony No. 52 of the same year. The slow movement in particular has a depth of feeling that looks forward to Mozart's G minor works
434D major—1772More relaxed and good-humoured; the finale's easy flow masks considerable contrapuntal skill. Haydn was by now the most accomplished contrapuntist in Vienna after the deaths of Fux and Caldara
535F minor—1772The most serious and sustained of the set. The opening Moderato in F minor has a brooding intensity that was startling in 1772; the Adagio is one of the longest and most developed slow movements Haydn had yet written. The finale is a fully worked-out fugue — Haydn demonstrating his contrapuntal mastery as if in response to critics who had called him a 'mere popular composer'
636A major—1772Ends the set on a note of relative warmth and accessibility; the finale is another fugue, this time lighter in character. The Op. 20 set convinced Haydn's contemporaries — and subsequent historians — that he was the supreme master of the quartet form. Mozart studied these scores intensively before writing his own six quartets dedicated to Haydn
The Op. 20 quartets were long known as the 'Sun' quartets but are also sometimes called the 'Great' quartets to distinguish them from the earlier sets. Their use of fully worked fugues in three of the six finales was a response to critics who saw Haydn as too popular and insufficiently learned — he demonstrably refuted the charge. After Op. 20, no new Haydn quartets for nine years.
Op. 33 — Six Quartets 'Russian' / 'Gli Scherzi' 1781  ·  6 quartets  ·  'Written in an entirely new and special manner'
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
137B minor—1781Haydn's own description — 'written in an entirely new and special manner' — announces the breakthrough. After nine years away from the form, he returned with quartets in which all four instruments are genuinely equal partners for the first time. The minuets are replaced by scherzos (hence 'Gli Scherzi'). B minor is an unusual and characterful key; the first movement is turbulent and searching
238E♭ majorThe Joke1781The famous nickname comes from the finale — Haydn repeatedly tricks the listener into thinking the movement has ended with pregnant silences and false returns, before the real final bar arrives almost inaudibly. One of the wittiest and most perfectly constructed quartet movements ever written; even audiences who know the joke laugh every time
339C majorBird1781The nickname comes from the chirping, bird-like grace notes in the first movement. A work of delightful charm and perfect proportions — often cited as the most immediately accessible of the set and an ideal entry point for listeners new to Haydn's quartets
440B♭ major—1781Dedicated to the Grand Duke Paul of Russia (hence the 'Russian' nickname for the set as a whole — they were premiered at a concert for his visit to Vienna). More serious in character than No. 3; the slow movement achieves a depth of lyricism that was new in Haydn's quartet writing
541G majorHow Do You Do?1781The nickname (sometimes given in German: 'Wie gehts?') refers to the conversational quality of the opening — the four instruments seem to be greeting each other. A particularly fine work, with a scherzo of irresistible rhythmic energy and a slow movement of great beauty
642D major—1781Closes the set with confident warmth; the finale's wit and formal control are the work of a composer completely at ease with the form he has reinvented. Mozart received the dedication of Op. 33 with particular gratitude — he was working on his own 'Haydn' quartets at the same time
The Op. 33 quartets were the most influential in Haydn's entire output. Mozart's six 'Haydn' Quartets K.387–465, dedicated to Haydn in 1785, were a direct response to them — Mozart spent three years on them, describing them as 'the fruit of long and laborious endeavour.' Beethoven in turn studied both sets before writing his Op. 18 quartets. Op. 33 is where the history of the string quartet truly begins.
Op. 42 — Single Quartet 1785  ·  1 quartet
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
—43D minor—1785A single quartet, shorter and more concentrated than its neighbours in the catalogue. Written for a Viennese publisher in haste; Haydn later said he was not entirely satisfied with it. Despite this, the D minor key gives it a seriousness and a dark emotional quality unusual in his output. Sometimes overlooked between the celebrated Op. 33 and Op. 50 sets but worth knowing
Op. 50 — Six Quartets 'Prussian Quartets' 1787  ·  6 quartets  ·  Dedicated to King Frederick William II of Prussia
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
144B♭ major—1787Dedicated to King Frederick William II of Prussia, himself an accomplished cellist — hence the cello parts throughout the set are more prominent and technically demanding than in earlier Haydn. A richly argued first movement and a finale of considerable wit open the set impressively
245C major—1787Perhaps the most inward and searching of the set; the slow movement achieves a depth of feeling that Haydn's contemporaries found both admirable and slightly unsettling. The development sections are becoming more adventurous harmonically
346E♭ major—1787Warm and expansive; the cello's thematic prominence in the opening movement shows Haydn exploiting the royal dedicatee's instrument with particular care. The finale's playful energy is irresistible
447F♯ minorThe Dream1787The extraordinary key of F♯ minor — the most remote in the set — creates an immediately distinctive and slightly uneasy atmosphere. The 'Dream' nickname refers to the slow movement's murmuring, hypnotic quality. One of the most original and haunting of all the Haydn quartets, and a particular favourite of performers
548F majorHow Do You Do?1787Sometimes given the same nickname as Op. 33 No. 5, for similar reasons — the conversational opening. A fluent and good-humoured work; the scherzo has a teasing rhythmic ambiguity (is it in two or three?) that Haydn exploits with characteristic playfulness
649D majorThe Frog1787The finale's unusual 'bariolage' technique — rapidly alternating the same note between an open string and a stopped string, producing a buzzing, croaking effect — gives rise to the nickname. A brilliantly inventive last movement that shows Haydn still finding new expressive possibilities in a technique he had used for thirty years
Op. 51 — The Seven Last Words of Our Saviour on the Cross 1787  ·  Special arrangement for string quartet
Originally composed as an orchestral work commissioned by Cádiz Cathedral for Good Friday 1786. The cathedral authorities required seven slow meditations on each of Christ's seven last words from the cross, preceded by a slow introduction and followed by an earthquake finale. Haydn later arranged the work for string quartet (1787) and for chorus (1795–96). The quartet version consists of a slow introduction ('Il Terremoto'), seven extended Adagio movements each representing one of the seven words, and a Presto finale depicting the earthquake. Hob.III:50–56. Not a conventional quartet in any structural sense, but a profound and moving meditation of extraordinary sustained beauty — one of the most unusual and rewarding works in the quartet literature. Rarely performed complete; individual movements sometimes appear in church concerts.
Op. 54 — Three Quartets 'Tost Quartets I' 1788  ·  3 quartets  ·  First post-London works
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
157G major—1788The 'Tost' quartets (Opp. 54, 55 and 64) are named after Johann Tost, a violinist in Haydn's orchestra at Esterháza who acquired the rights to them and sold them in Paris and Vienna. Haydn's expanding European reputation — fed by the immense success of the Paris Symphonies — is reflected in a new grandeur and ambition. The G major opens with an unusually assertive gesture
258C major—1788The most celebrated of the three; the Adagio is one of Haydn's finest slow movements — a long, freely ornamented melody in C major of extraordinary depth and beauty. The finale's unusual return to the slow tempo of the opening creates a satisfying arch structure
359E major—1788The unusual key of E major gives this quartet a slightly exotic, brilliant quality. The dramatic slow movement is one of the most emotionally intense things in the Tost sets; the finale's energy is irresistible
Op. 55 — Three Quartets 'Tost Quartets II' 1788  ·  3 quartets
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
160A major—1788Warm and expansive; the opening's bold unison gesture announces the more public and confident manner that characterises the Tost sets generally. Haydn was now the most famous living composer in Europe
261F minorThe Razor1788Legend has it that Haydn, in need of a good razor, offered this quartet to his publisher Bland in exchange for one. Whether true or not, it is the most dramatically intense of the Tost quartets — the F minor first movement drives relentlessly, and the passionate slow movement has an almost Beethovenian directness of expression. The most frequently performed of the Op. 55 set
362B♭ major—1788A relaxed and charming conclusion to Op. 55; the slow movement's lyrical ease and the finale's good humour make it an agreeable companion to the more intense Razor quartet
Op. 64 — Six Quartets 'Tost Quartets III' 1790  ·  6 quartets  ·  Written just before the first London visit
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
165C major—1790Written in the year of Mozart's death and just before Haydn's first London visit — the journey that would transform his public career. The Op. 64 set shows Haydn at the height of his powers before the Apponyi and Erdődy quartets of the London years
268B minor—1790B minor is a rare key in Haydn — the brooding, searching quality of the first movement is quite unlike the usual character of his major-key works. A particularly fine and undervalued quartet
367B♭ major—1790Light and sociable in character; the finale's wit is particularly evident. Haydn's ability to write music of apparently effortless charm while simultaneously deploying considerable formal and contrapuntal skill is nowhere more evident than in these middle movements of Op. 64
466G major—1790One of the warmest and most immediately appealing of the Op. 64 quartets; the slow movement's long, singing melody is pure Haydn at his most lyrical
563D majorThe Lark1790The most celebrated of the set and one of Haydn's most famous quartets. The nickname comes from the first movement's opening: the first violin soars freely above a quiet accompaniment in the other three instruments, like a lark in flight. The effect is of breathtaking simplicity and naturalness — and yet the technical control required of the first violinist is considerable. The most frequently performed of the Op. 64 quartets
664E♭ major—1790A worthy close to the set; the slow movement's sustained lyricism and the finale's rhythmic energy are characteristic of Haydn's craft at its most assured
Op. 71 — Three Quartets 'Apponyi Quartets I' 1793  ·  3 quartets  ·  Written after the first London visit
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
169B♭ major—1793Haydn's two London visits (1791–92 and 1794–95) transformed him from a court composer to an international celebrity. The Apponyi quartets (Opp. 71 and 74) were written between the two visits, dedicated to Count Apponyi, and show the influence of London's large public concert audiences: bolder opening gestures, more orchestral dynamics, a more immediately communicative manner. The B♭ major opens with a forthright unison statement before settling into its conversational manner
270D major—1793The opening gesture — a grand unison chord followed by silence before the main theme begins — is almost orchestral in its effect. Haydn had discovered in London that public audiences needed a more direct and immediate manner of address than the Esterházy court had required
371E♭ major—1793The most lyrical of the three; the slow movement is particularly fine — a long, decorated melody of great warmth. The finale's energy and rhythmic drive conclude the set impressively
Op. 74 — Three Quartets 'Apponyi Quartets II' 1793  ·  3 quartets
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
172C major—1793Strong, spacious and confident; the first movement's broad architecture reflects the 'grand manner' Haydn had developed in response to London audiences. The slow movement achieves a depth and seriousness that anticipates the Erdődy quartets to come
273F major—1793The most lyrical of the three — the Andante grazioso slow movement has a singing quality of great charm, and the finale's easy good humour is entirely characteristic. Often overlooked beside the more dramatic Rider quartet but consistently rewarding
374G minorThe Rider / Horseman1793The most celebrated of the Apponyi quartets and one of Haydn's most dramatic works. The finale's galloping, perpetual-motion rhythm — six-eight time at full speed — gives rise to the nickname and creates an exhilarating conclusion to the set. The slow movement is a set of deeply felt variations in G major; the contrast between its seriousness and the finale's energy is one of Haydn's most effective large-scale structural contrasts
Op. 76 — Six Quartets 'Erdődy Quartets' 1797–1799  ·  6 quartets  ·  Haydn's supreme achievement in the form
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
175G major—1797Dedicated to Count Erdődy. Haydn was sixty-five when he wrote these six quartets — and they represent his supreme achievement in the form. The G major opens with an immediate sense of arrival and confident authority; the Adagio sostenuto slow movement is one of the most profound he ever wrote, the four voices woven together with a polyphonic richness that no other composer could then match
276D minorFifths / Quinten1797The most immediately striking of the set. The opening — two plunging perfect fifths (D–A, D–A) in unison — is one of the most arresting beginnings in all quartet music. The entire first movement is built from this falling-fifth motif in a feat of motivic economy that Beethoven would study carefully. The slow movement Andante or Adagio is in the major key of A major; the contrast with the fierce D minor outer movements is deeply affecting
377C majorEmperor1797The most famous Haydn quartet. The slow movement consists of four variations on the hymn 'Gott erhalte Franz den Kaiser' — written by Haydn in the previous year as a birthday anthem for Emperor Franz II of Austria, and later adopted (in a different text) as the Austrian and then German national anthem ('Deutschland über alles'). Each variation gives the theme to a different instrument while the others ornament it. Of luminous beauty. The outer movements are among the most vigorous and confident in the entire set
478B♭ majorSunrise1797The nickname comes from the opening: the main theme gradually emerges from a sustained, murmuring accompaniment in the lower three strings, like the sun rising over the horizon. An image of great poetry achieved with minimal means. The slow movement in the minor key provides emotional depth; the finale dances to a conclusion of effortless charm
579D majorLargo / Laudon1797Sometimes called 'Laudon' after the Austrian field marshal Count Laudon. The slow movement — marked Largo, Haydn's slowest tempo marking — is one of the most deeply felt in his entire output: a long, unornamented melody of almost painful beauty, accompanied with the barest minimum of harmony. It is said that Haydn wept when he played it through for the first time
680E♭ major—1799The last of the set and the last Haydn quartet to be published in his lifetime. A work of extraordinary harmonic richness and formal mastery; the slow movement in B major (the most remote possible key from the home key of E♭ major) is one of the most harmonically adventurous things Haydn ever wrote. A worthy and deeply satisfying conclusion to what is, in the judgment of most scholars and performers, the greatest single set of string quartets ever composed by one composer
The Op. 76 Erdődy quartets represent the summit of Haydn's quartet achievement and, arguably, the summit of the Classical quartet before Beethoven's late works. At sixty-five, working on the Seasons and the Creation simultaneously, Haydn produced six works of such concentrated mastery that his younger contemporaries were left speechless. Beethoven, who had just published his own Op. 18 quartets, called them 'truly the greatest quartets ever written.'
Op. 77 — Two Quartets 'Lobkowitz Quartets' 1799  ·  2 quartets  ·  A planned third was never completed
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
181G major—1799Dedicated to Prince Lobkowitz — Beethoven's great patron, who had also championed Haydn. Written alongside the Seasons oratorio, when Haydn was already beginning to feel the effects of the exhaustion and ill health that would prevent him from completing a third quartet. Of extraordinary quality — the first movement's main theme is one of his most perfectly proportioned; the slow movement Adagio is deeply felt and harmonically inventive. Among the finest things he ever wrote
282F major—1799The finale's hunting-call theme in the cello — a bold, horn-like figure — is one of the most immediately memorable in his quartet output, and the movement builds from it to a conclusion of tremendous joyful energy. The slow movement's quiet lyricism makes the finale's arrival all the more exhilarating. A fitting near-valediction to a life's work in the form
Op. 103 — Unfinished Quartet (Two movements only) 1803  ·  Haydn's last work
No.Hob.III:KeyNicknameYearNotes
—83D minor—1803Haydn's last attempt at the form, abandoned through failing health — he had been debilitated by a series of strokes and the exhaustion of completing The Seasons (1801). Only two inner movements survive: an Andante grazioso in B♭ major and a Minuet in D minor. The outer movements were never written. Haydn attached a calling-card to the manuscript on which he had written: 'Gone is all my strength, old and weak am I.' Both completed movements are of high quality — the Andante in particular has a peaceful, autumnal beauty. Sometimes performed between the two Op. 77 quartets to form a kind of triptych
Hob. = Hoboken Catalogue (Hob.III = String Quartets). Total: 68 quartets in 16 opus groups, c.1757–1803.
The essential Haydn quartet repertoire: Op. 33 (all six) · Op. 50 No. 4 (The Dream) · Op. 64 No. 5 (The Lark) · Op. 74 No. 3 (The Rider) · Op. 76 (all six) · Op. 77 (both).
Recommended recordings: Kodály Quartet (Naxos — complete, budget) · Amadeus Quartet (DG — selected) · Takács Quartet (Decca — Opp. 71, 74) · Festetics Quartet (Astrée — period instruments, complete)

Ludwig van Beethoven — Complete String Quartets

16 quartets + Grosse Fuge in five groups  ·  1798–1826
Note on numbering: Beethoven’s quartets are identified by their opus number, which broadly reflects the order of publication rather than strict composition order. The sequential numbering (No. 1–16) follows standard convention. The six Op. 18 quartets are accomplished but essentially Classical works; the three Op. 59 ‘Razumovsky’ quartets mark a decisive break with that world; the late quartets (Op. 127–135) stand apart from everything — among the most profound music ever written, composed while Beethoven was completely deaf. Op. 133 (the Grosse Fuge) was originally the finale of Op. 130; Beethoven’s publisher persuaded him to replace it with a lighter movement and publish the fugue separately. It is included here as a companion to Op. 130.

In your collection: Op. 59 Nos. 1–3 (Lindsay Quartet, CD 77)  ·  Op. 127, 130, 133 & 135 (Quartetto Italiano Vol. 1, CD 78)  ·  Op. 131 & 132 (Quartetto Italiano Vol. 2, CD 79)  ·  Op. 130, 133 & 135 (Amadeus Quartet, CD 88)
Op. 18 Nos. 1–6 1798–1800  ·  6 quartets  ·  Published 1801; dedicated to Prince Lobkowitz
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 18 No. 1 F major — 1799 Composed second but placed first in publication. The slow movement was inspired, Beethoven told a friend, by the tomb scene in Romeo and Juliet. Technically the most demanding of the set for all four players; the most frequently performed of the Op. 18 quartets
2 18 No. 2 G major Compliments 1798 The earliest composed of the set. The nickname refers to the bowing and scraping figure in the first movement. A lighter, more social work than No. 1; Mozart’s influence is audible throughout
3 18 No. 3 D major — 1798 Placed third in publication but probably composed first. Haydn’s influence prominent; slow movement in B flat — one of Beethoven’s earliest experiments with remote-key slow movements, a device that would become a hallmark of his maturity
4 18 No. 4 C minor — 1799–1800 The only minor-key quartet of the Op. 18 set. The forceful, driven character of C minor — the key of the Fifth Symphony and the Pathétique Sonata — is already present. Scherzo of remarkable rhythmic energy. The most forward-looking of the six
5 18 No. 5 A major — 1799 The most overtly Mozartian of the six — modelled consciously on Mozart’s K. 464 in the same key (whose entire score Beethoven had copied out as a student). A deliberate homage, clearly acknowledged
6 18 No. 6 B♭ major — 1800 The last composed and most ambitious. The finale is headed ‘La Malinconia’ (Melancholy) — a slow, dissonant introduction of startling strangeness that prefigures the late quartets. The cheerful Allegretto that follows it was itself considered bizarre by contemporaries. The most prophetic of the Op. 18 set
The Op. 18 quartets were published in 1801 when Beethoven was thirty. He had studied all of Haydn and Mozart and had absorbed the form completely. The three ‘Razumovsky’ quartets that followed in 1806 were a complete rupture with everything he had done before.
