Op. 59 Nos. 1–3 ‘Razumovsky’ CD 77 1806  ·  3 quartets  ·  Commissioned by Count Razumovsky; dedicated to him
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
7 59 No. 1 F major Razumovsky No. 1 1806 The first movement alone is longer than many complete earlier quartets. The opening cello theme — the other voices entering a bar apart in a cascade — was described by a contemporary as “a new trick.” The slow movement quotes a Russian theme in tribute to Count Razumovsky’s nationality. A work of revolutionary scale and seriousness
8 59 No. 2 E minor Razumovsky No. 2 1806 Perhaps the most inward of the three. The slow movement in E major is one of Beethoven’s great visionary passages — Beethoven told a friend it came to him while contemplating the starry sky and thinking of the music of the spheres. The finale quotes another Russian folk theme. Profoundly searching
9 59 No. 3 C major Razumovsky No. 3 1806 Opens with a slow introduction of remarkable mystery — bare unison C, then chromatic groping in the dark — before the Allegro erupts. The fugal finale, a full-scale sonata-fugue, was the most ambitious contrapuntal movement Beethoven had yet written for quartet. The most outwardly dramatic of the three
The Razumovsky quartets caused consternation on their first performance. A professional violinist who played through them declared that Beethoven “must be crazy.” Count Razumovsky, a diplomat and amateur cellist, kept a resident professional quartet in his Vienna palace — the Schuppanzigh Quartet, which became the pre-eminent Beethoven quartet ensemble of its day. The Lindsay Quartet’s Japanese CBS/Sony pressing is prized for its combination of intellectual rigour and emotional directness (CD 77).
Op. 74 & Op. 95 — The Middle Period Quartets 1809–1810  ·  2 quartets  ·  The bridge to the late period
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
10 74 E♭ major Harp 1809 The nickname comes from the pizzicato arpeggios in the first movement, which suggest a harp. More expansive and lyrical in character than the Razumovsky set; the slow movement (Adagio ma non troppo) is one of Beethoven’s most beautiful. The finale is a set of variations — a form he was increasingly drawn to. Dedicated to Prince Lobkowitz
11 95 F minor Serioso 1810 Beethoven’s own subtitle. The most compressed and concentrated quartet he wrote — every movement cut to its essentials, with none of the expansiveness of Op. 74. The opening unison F, the violent sforzandi, the spare textures — all anticipate the late style. Beethoven noted it was “written for a small circle of connoisseurs” and not intended for public performance
After Op. 95 (1810), Beethoven wrote no string quartets for thirteen years. He was occupied with the Seventh and Eighth Symphonies, the Violin Sonatas, the Piano Sonatas Op. 109–111, and the Missa Solemnis. When he returned to the quartet in 1823, it was in a world entirely his own.
Op. 127, 130, 131, 132, 135 — The Late Quartets CD 78  ·  CD 79  ·  CD 88 1823–1826  ·  5 quartets  ·  Commissioned by Prince Galitzin; composed in complete deafness
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
12 127 E♭ major — 1824–25 The first of the five late quartets. Three massive chords open the work — they return transformed in each subsequent appearance, like a fixed point around which everything else evolves. The slow movement (Adagio ma non troppo e molto cantabile) is a set of variations of extraordinary depth and beauty, the theme stated with a simplicity that disguises its profound harmonic richness. The Quartetto Italiano account is definitive (CD 78)
13 130 B♭ major — 1825–26 Six movements — the longest quartet Beethoven wrote. The Cavatina (fifth movement), marked ‘beklemmt’ (oppressed, anguished), is one of the most intimate and heartbreaking pages in all chamber music; Beethoven said it made him weep every time he thought of it. Originally ended with the Grosse Fuge; Beethoven replaced it with the present finale at his publisher’s request. Both endings are legitimate. The Quartetto Italiano (CD 78) and Amadeus Quartet (CD 88) preserve the original finale
14 131 C♯ minor — 1826 Seven movements played without a break. Beethoven considered it his finest quartet. Opens with a slow fugue of unsettling intensity — bare, modal, ancient-sounding — that dissolves into a sequence of movements of extraordinary emotional range, from playful to transcendent. The finale’s coda, with its hammered repeated notes and sudden silence, is one of the most devastating endings in music. The Quartetto Italiano’s account (CD 79) remains the benchmark after fifty years
15 132 A minor — 1825 Composed during and after a serious illness; the slow movement is headed ‘Holy song of thanksgiving from a convalescent to the Divinity, in the Lydian mode.’ The Lydian harmonies — modal and ancient-sounding — alternate with sections marked ‘feeling new strength,’ the contrast between stillness and energy sustained across twenty minutes without a single bar of rhetoric. Among the most extraordinary slow movements in the entire repertoire. Quartetto Italiano (CD 79)
16 135 F major — 1826 The last quartet Beethoven completed, written in the months before his death. The shortest of the late quartets; more compact and less demanding than its predecessors, but no less searching. The finale carries a motto: ‘Der schwer gefasste Entschluss’ (The difficult decision), with the question ‘Muss es sein?’ (Must it be?) answered by ‘Es muss sein!’ (It must be!). Whether cosmic or comic, no one entirely agrees. Amadeus Quartet (CD 88) and Quartetto Italiano (CD 78)
Op. 133 — Grosse Fuge CD 78  ·  CD 88 1825–26  ·  Originally the finale of Op. 130  ·  Published separately 1827
No.Op.KeyNicknameYearNotes
— 133 B♭ major Grosse Fuge 1825–26 Igor Stravinsky called it “an absolutely contemporary piece of music that will be contemporary forever.” A single-movement work of approximately sixteen minutes combining fugue, variation, and sonata principles in a structure of extraordinary complexity and density. The opening ‘Overtura’ states the main subject in four simultaneous forms (normal, inverted, diminished, augmented) before the fugue proper begins. The violence of its dissonances and the ferocity of its rhythmic drive shocked contemporaries; Beethoven’s publisher asked him to remove it from Op. 130 on the grounds it was too difficult. Beethoven complied — reluctantly. The Quartetto Italiano (CD 78) and Amadeus Quartet (CD 88) both include it
Beethoven died on 26 March 1827, aged 56, having completed Op. 135 the previous October. He had sketched ideas for a seventeenth quartet in C major but wrote nothing beyond fragments. The sixteen quartets and the Grosse Fuge span thirty years of his creative life, from the Classical world of Op. 18 to the late style that pointed beyond Romanticism entirely, anticipating harmonic and formal possibilities that European music would not fully explore for another century.
Total: 16 string quartets + Op. 133 (Grosse Fuge), 1798–1826. Opus numbers reflect publication order, not strict composition order. The five late quartets (Op. 127, 130, 131, 132, 135) and the Grosse Fuge are universally regarded as the summit of the string quartet repertoire. In your collection: Op. 59 (Lindsay Quartet, CD 77)  ·  Op. 127, 130, 133, 135 (Quartetto Italiano, CD 78)  ·  Op. 131, 132 (Quartetto Italiano, CD 79)  ·  Op. 130, 133, 135 (Amadeus Quartet, CD 88).

Franz Schubert — Complete String Quartets

15 quartets + Quartettsatz in five groups  ·  1811–1826
Note on numbering: Schubert’s quartets are identified by their Deutsch (D.) catalogue number, compiled by Otto Erich Deutsch and published in 1951 — the standard Schubert catalogue. The sequential numbering (No. 1–15) follows conventional practice but can vary between editions; the D. number is always reliable. The early quartets (D. 18 through D. 353) were written primarily for domestic performance by Schubert’s family; the mature works from D. 703 onwards represent an entirely different artistic world. The Quartettsatz (D. 703) is a single-movement fragment — Schubert broke off after completing one movement and a sketch of a second — and is listed separately here as it occupies a pivotal position in his development. The String Quintet D. 956 and Piano Quintet ‘Trout’ D. 667 are not included here; both are separate and equally important works.

In your collection: Quartettsatz D.703 & Nos. 13 & 14 (The Lindsays, CD 402/407)  ·  No. 15 D.887 & Quartettsatz (Fitzwilliam Quartet, CD 406)  ·  Nos. 11 & 14 (Alban Berg Quartett, CD 404)  ·  No. 15 & No. 14 & Quintet (Belcea Quartet, CD 408)  ·  Late chamber music inc. last three quartets (Brandis Quartet, CD 397)  ·  Quartettsatz (Allegri Quartet, CD 400)
Early Quartets — D. 18, 32, 36, 46, 68, 74, 87 1811–1813  ·  7 quartets  ·  Written for family performance, Schubert aged 14–16
No.D.KeyNicknameYearNotes
1 18 Mixed (C/B♭) — 1811 Schubert’s first attempt at the quartet form, written when he was fourteen. A composite work in several movements drawing on various keys; clearly experimental and not yet a coherent whole. Remarkable as the work of a self-taught boy composer with no formal quartet training
2 32 C major — 1812 More formally coherent than D.18. Schubert was now a pupil of Salieri, though his quartet writing was essentially self-directed. Already shows melodic fluency well beyond his age
3 36 B♭ major — 1812–13 The slow movement shows growing lyrical confidence. Schubert was absorbing Haydn and Mozart through practical performance with his family quartet — his father played cello, two brothers violin, and Schubert himself viola
4 46 C major — 1813 A lost work, surviving only in fragments. Included in the standard numbering
5 68 B♭ major — 1813 The Haydn influence is now dominant and fully absorbed. Four well-proportioned movements; the minuet in particular shows real rhythmic character. A competent and occasionally charming work
6 74 D major — 1813 The most accomplished of the earliest quartets. The first movement shows a grasp of sonata form unusual for a sixteen-year-old; the slow movement already has a Schubertian quality of inward lyricism that sets it apart from its predecessors
7 87 E major — 1813 Composed in the same extraordinarily productive year as D.74. Schubert was now sixteen and writing with a facility that astonished his teachers. The key of E major — warm, slightly pastoral — is handled with increasing confidence
Transitional Quartets — D. 94, 112, 173, 353 1813–1816  ·  4 quartets  ·  Growing ambition and harmonic individuality
No.D.KeyNicknameYearNotes
8 94 B♭ major — 1813 A step forward in harmonic range; the development sections are becoming more adventurous. Schubert is beginning to find his own voice within the inherited Classical framework, particularly in the slow movements
9 112 B♭ major — 1814 Composed in the year Schubert wrote Gretchen am Spinnrade, his first great song. The creative acceleration is evident: the quartet’s slow movement has a sustained, songlike quality that links it to the extraordinary song production of the same months. The first quartet that might be called individually Schubertian in character
10 173 G minor — 1815 The only minor-key quartet of the early period, and the most forward-looking. The turbulent first movement and intense slow movement are already pointing towards the emotional world of the late quartets. A pivotal work, showing Schubert absorbing Beethoven’s influence and beginning to transform it
11 353 E major — 1816 Sometimes called No. 11 in the ‘Rosamunde’ numbering, which causes confusion (see No. 13 below). A relatively relaxed, lyrical work in the sunny key of E major; the most polished of the transitional quartets, and the last before a gap of four years. The Alban Berg Quartett recording (CD 404), which pairs it with D.810, gives this neglected quartet its most distinguished modern advocacy
After D.353 (1816), Schubert wrote no string quartets for four years. He was pouring his creative energy into the songs — over 140 in 1815 alone — and into larger orchestral and choral works. When he returned to the quartet in 1820, everything had changed.
Quartettsatz — D. 703 CD 400  ·  CD 402  ·  CD 406  ·  CD 407 1820  ·  One completed movement + sketch  ·  The pivotal fragment
No.D.KeyNicknameYearNotes
— 703 C minor Quartettsatz 1820 Schubert completed only the first movement (Allegro assai) and forty bars of a slow movement in A flat before abandoning the work. Why he stopped is unknown. The completed movement is one of the most dramatic and concentrated things he ever wrote — turbulent, harmonically restless, with a second theme of aching lyrical beauty that appears briefly in the major before the stormy music returns. It stands as the gateway between the early and late quartets: nothing before it prepares you for what it contains. In the collection in four separate performances — by the Allegri Quartet (CD 400), the Lindsays (CD 402 and CD 407), and the Fitzwilliam Quartet (CD 406)
The Quartettsatz was not published until 1870, forty-two years after Schubert’s death. He wrote no further quartets for three years after abandoning it. His next quartet, D.804, was the first he heard performed professionally.
The Three Late Quartets — D. 804, 810, 887 CD 397  ·  CD 402  ·  CD 404  ·  CD 406  ·  CD 407  ·  CD 408 1824–1826  ·  3 quartets  ·  Among the greatest works in the chamber repertoire
No.D.KeyNicknameYearNotes
13 804 A minor Rosamunde 1824 The nickname comes from the slow movement, which uses a theme from Schubert’s incidental music to Rosamunde. The first quartet Schubert had performed publicly (by the Schuppanzigh Quartet in Vienna, 1824) and the only one he heard in professional performance during his lifetime. More lyrical and less turbulent than its successors; the first movement’s quiet resignation and the slow movement’s singing theme give it a particular warmth and accessibility. The Lindsays’ account (CD 402) remains among the finest available
14 810 D minor Death and the Maiden 1824 The nickname comes from the slow movement, a set of variations on Schubert’s own song Der Tod und das Mädchen (1817). The most dramatic and frequently performed of the three late quartets: the first movement’s hammering repeated chords and its long, fatalistic opening theme set a tone of inexorable intensity that is sustained across all four movements. The finale — a tarantella in D minor, the dance of death — drives relentlessly to its conclusion. In the collection in four recordings: the Lindsays (CD 402 / CD 407), the Alban Berg Quartett (CD 404), the Belcea Quartet (CD 408), and the Brandis Quartet (CD 397). Among the most recorded works in the entire quartet repertoire
15 887 G major — 1826 The last and most ambitious of the three — and the least performed, partly because of its exceptional length (around fifty minutes) and technical demands. Sometimes called Schubert’s ‘fifth symphony for strings.’ The first movement alone runs to some twenty minutes. The shimmering tremolo texture of the opening, the radical harmonic shifts, and the sustained emotional range across four enormous movements mark this as a work of total maturity. The slow movement’s central crisis — a sudden eruption of violence from the prevailing quiet — is one of the most alarming moments in Schubert. The Fitzwilliam Quartet on Linn (CD 406) and the Belcea Quartet (CD 408) give distinguished modern accounts; the Brandis Quartet (CD 397) completes the set
Schubert died on 19 November 1828, aged 31. He wrote D.887 in June 1826, two and a half years before his death, and composed no further string quartets. In those final years he turned instead to the Piano Sonatas, the song cycle Winterreise, the String Quintet D.956, and the last three piano sonatas — all written in 1828, the year he died. The three late quartets and the Quartettsatz together form one of the great bodies of chamber music: compact, devastating, and in the case of D.887, still not fully absorbed by the wider musical public.
D. = Deutsch catalogue number (Otto Erich Deutsch, 1951; the standard Schubert catalogue). Total: 15 string quartets + Quartettsatz D.703 (fragment), 1811–1826. Not included: String Quintet D.956 (with second cello); Piano Quintet ‘The Trout’ D.667 (with double bass) — both in the collection separately. The three late quartets (D.804, D.810, D.887) and the Quartettsatz (D.703) are the essential Schubert quartet repertoire.

In your collection: CD 397 (Brandis Quartet — late chamber music)  ·  CD 400 (Allegri — D.703)  ·  CD 402 (Lindsays — D.703, 804, 810)  ·  CD 404 (Alban Berg — D.353, 810)  ·  CD 406 (Fitzwilliam — D.703, 887)  ·  CD 407 (Lindsays — D.703, 810)  ·  CD 408 (Belcea — D.810, 887 + Quintet)
A Concert Guide

Schubert: String Quartet in D minor D.810

“Death and the Maiden”  ·  Franz Schubert  ·  composed 1824

Before You Go: The Essentials

The quartet you are about to hear is forty minutes of music written by a man of twenty-seven who already knew he was seriously ill and quite possibly dying. Franz Schubert had contracted syphilis in 1822, two years before he composed this work, at a time when the disease was incurable and its long-term consequences well understood. He did not die immediately — he lived another four years and wrote some of his greatest music in the time remaining — but the shadow of illness and premature death is present in this quartet in a way that is not merely biographical speculation. It is in the music itself: in its key, in its themes, in the song from which it takes its nickname, and in the particular quality of desperation and resignation that runs through all four movements.

None of this means the music is unrelentingly gloomy. It is not. There are passages of fierce energy, moments of lyrical warmth, and a finale of demonic rhythmic drive. But the emotional world of this quartet is darker and more turbulent than almost anything Schubert had written before it, and knowing something about its origins and construction will help you hear what is actually happening — why certain moments feel the way they do, and what Schubert is doing with his materials to produce their effect.

Schubert and the Song

The quartet takes its nickname from a song Schubert wrote seven years earlier, in 1817, when he was twenty. Der Tod und das Mädchen (“Death and the Maiden”) sets a short poem by the minor German poet Matthias Claudius in which a young girl, terrified at Death's approach, begs him to pass by; Death replies, calmly and with terrible gentleness, that he comes as a friend, not an enemy: “Give me your hand, you beautiful and tender creature. I am your friend and come not to punish. Be of good cheer. I am not fierce. You shall sleep softly in my arms.”

What makes the song remarkable is its musical characterisation of Death. He does not arrive in a blaze of terror. His music is quiet, even consoling — a slow, rocking figure in the piano, harmonically ambiguous, that sounds at once soothing and inexorable. It is precisely this music — Death's theme, not the girl's anguished plea — that Schubert takes as the basis for the slow movement of the quartet. He uses it as the theme for a set of variations: the same rocking, gentle, quietly inevitable melody, presented five times in different emotional guises. By the time you have heard the song, even once, you will hear the slow movement differently. The calm surface of Death's music, repeated and varied across twenty minutes, becomes one of the most sustained meditations on mortality in Western chamber music.

You do not need to know the song in advance to be moved by the slow movement. But if you can find a recording beforehand — it lasts about three minutes and is among Schubert's most celebrated songs — it will open the movement in a particular way.

The Quartet in Context

Schubert wrote fifteen string quartets in all, the first when he was fourteen, producing them primarily for performance by his family at home. His father played cello, two brothers the violins, and Schubert himself viola. These early quartets are accomplished but not yet individual; they follow the models of Haydn and Mozart with affectionate faithfulness.

The D minor quartet of 1824 belongs to an entirely different world. By this point Schubert had absorbed Beethoven's middle-period quartets and had felt the full force of the Razumovsky set — works that had stretched the quartet form to accommodate a weight of expression that the Classical tradition had not imagined it could bear. His own illness, and the deepening seriousness of his artistic ambitions, pushed him in the same direction. The D minor quartet was the first he considered good enough to offer for professional performance, though it was not performed publicly until after his death, and not published until 1831, three years after he died.

He wrote it alongside the A minor quartet D.804 (“Rosamunde”) in the same year — the two works make a complementary pair, the A minor lyrical and resigned, the D minor turbulent and fierce — and both represent the moment when Schubert moved decisively from the private domestic sphere of his early quartet writing to the same level of ambition and public seriousness as Beethoven.

The Performers: What a String Quartet Does

Before turning to the individual movements, it is worth saying something about the medium itself, since if you are not a regular concert-goer the string quartet can initially feel austere compared to an orchestra or even a piano recital. There are four players: two violinists, a violist, and a cellist. Each plays a single melodic line, so the maximum “texture” — the number of notes sounding simultaneously — is four. The dynamic range is narrower than an orchestra. There are no percussion effects, no brass fanfares, no woodwind colours. Everything that happens must happen through the four strings.

This limitation is also the form's particular power. With four players, every line is exposed; every player is individually audible; nothing can be hidden in the mass of a larger ensemble. The relationship between the four voices — who has the melody, who provides the harmonic support, how they pass ideas between them — is always transparent. And because there is no conductor, the four players must listen to each other with a degree of attention that creates a particular kind of ensemble communication, a musical conversation of equals, that the concert experience at its best allows you to witness directly.

In this quartet, notice especially the relationship between the first violin and the cello. Schubert often gives the cello melodic prominence that earlier quartet writing reserved for the first violin, and the dialogue between the top and bottom of the ensemble — the highest and lowest voices — is one of the expressive resources he uses with particular skill.

Movement by Movement

The quartet has four movements, played in sequence without significant pauses. Here is what to listen for in each.

First Movement: Allegro — The Storm

The first movement opens with one of the most immediately striking beginnings in all chamber music. There is no gentle introduction, no throat-clearing. The four instruments attack together on a single chord — forte, aggressive, in D minor — and then the first violin launches into a wide-arching theme of fierce energy. Listen to the rhythm of this opening: it is driving and relentless, a repeated figure in the accompaniment (the lower three strings) that will persist, with only brief interruptions, for much of the movement. This rhythmic pulse — insistent, almost obsessive — is one of the movement's defining characteristics, and it contributes powerfully to the sense of something inescapable, something that cannot be stopped.

The first theme is turbulent and in the minor key. What comes next — after a transition — is the second theme, and here Schubert does something characteristic: he moves to the major key (F major, the relative major of D minor) and introduces a melody of quiet, almost pastoral warmth. This contrast between the fierce minor-key first theme and the consoling major-key second theme is the emotional engine of the movement, and it recurs in different forms throughout. The contrast is not simply between loud and soft, or fast and slow; it is between two emotional states — anguish and consolation, storm and momentary shelter — that the movement refuses to resolve in favour of either. In the recapitulation (when the opening material returns after the development section), pay attention to what happens to the second theme: Schubert brings it back in D major rather than the expected D minor, a small harmonic decision that gives the moment a quality of bittersweet, almost painful beauty, as if the consolation is real but cannot last.

The movement ends not in triumph or resolution but in a fierce, abrupt D minor chord. The storm has not passed.

Second Movement: Andante con moto — Death Speaks

This is the emotional heart of the quartet, and the movement from which everything else takes its meaning. It is a theme and five variations, and the theme is Death's music from the song of 1817.

The theme is presented first by all four instruments together, quietly, in G minor. It is slow, regular, and gently rocking — the same quality of calm inevitability that characterises Death's words in the song. Notice how Schubert writes it: the rhythm is almost hypnotic in its regularity, and the harmony, while not dissonant, has a quality of modal ambiguity — it does not quite resolve in the way tonal music normally does — that gives it an archaic, other-worldly quality. This is not the major-key warmth of a lullaby, though it has some of a lullaby's rocking quality. It is something cooler and stranger.

The five variations that follow each illuminate a different aspect of the theme's emotional content. The first variation keeps the rocking accompaniment in the lower strings while the first violin floats a lyrical melody above it — the contrast between the inexorable bass and the singing treble creates a sense of the human voice trying to rise above what the accompaniment represents. The second variation is more agitated, with faster figuration — a moment of resistance or distress. The third variation moves to G major and has a quality of warmth that is almost consoling, the major key transforming the theme's character from cold to gentle. The fourth variation is the darkest: slower, more harmonically chromatic, with a quality of exhaustion and resignation. And the fifth brings back the rocking theme in something close to its original form but in a hushed, distant register — as if heard from very far away.

Throughout this movement, keep your ear on whichever instrument has the melody, and notice how the others respond to it. The conversation between the four voices is particularly subtle here: Schubert distributes the thematic material across all four players in ways that give each variation a slightly different ensemble texture.

Third Movement: Scherzo — Fierce and Brief

The third movement is a scherzo — literally a “joke,” though the character here is fierce rather than comic. It is the shortest movement of the four, and it arrives after the slow movement like a sudden shock: fast, loud, rhythmically driven, in D minor again. The opening has a stamping, almost violent energy — the accents fall in unexpected places, giving the music a lurching, off-balance quality.

The central section (the “trio”) offers a brief contrast: slower, in D major, with a folk-like simplicity that momentarily suggests the world of Schubert's dance music and songs. But the dark, stamping scherzo music returns, and the movement ends as abruptly as it began.

The function of this movement in the quartet's overall emotional arc is important: it provides a fierce, energetic interlude between the profound meditation of the slow movement and the demonic finale that follows. It does not offer consolation or resolution; it simply exists as a concentrated burst of violent energy before the final reckoning.

Fourth Movement: Presto — The Dance of Death

The finale is one of the most extraordinary movements Schubert ever wrote. It is marked Presto — very fast — and it is in a 6/8 time signature that gives it the character of a tarantella: the frenzied Italian dance that legend associated with the bite of the tarantula spider and the only cure — to dance until exhaustion drove the poison out. Whether Schubert consciously intended the tarantella association is uncertain, but the result is a movement that combines frantic forward motion with a quality of driven, almost helpless energy — a dance that cannot stop.

The movement is in D minor throughout, with almost no major-key relief. The opening theme is a headlong, rushing figure in the first violin, taken up by the other instruments, that never loses its momentum. Listen to the cello in particular: it drives the harmonic rhythm from the bottom of the texture with a relentlessness that contributes powerfully to the sense of something inescapable. When the music briefly touches the major key — and it does, fleetingly — the return to the minor feels all the more inevitable.

There is a second theme of slightly different character, more lyrical and arching, but it too is swept up in the movement's driving energy. The development section takes the material through a series of harmonic sequences that push the music to points of considerable intensity before the recapitulation brings the opening theme back with full force.

The coda — the final section — accelerates even further, the tempo pushing to Più mosso (faster still), and the quartet drives to its close in a flurry of D minor arpeggios that cut off with characteristic Schubertian abruptness. There is no triumphant resolution, no earned major-key conclusion. The movement ends as it began: in D minor, at full speed, going somewhere it cannot quite name.

The Whole and Its Meaning

When the four movements are heard in sequence, what emerges is a structure of considerable emotional and architectural coherence. The fierce D minor of the first movement establishes the emotional world; the slow movement meditates on the central theme of mortality with extraordinary depth and patience; the scherzo provides a burst of dark energy; and the finale drives relentlessly to a conclusion that offers no resolution beyond the sheer fact of forward motion. D minor is the key of the first movement and the last; the major-key moments that appear in the middle movements are temporary and ultimately overcome by the minor. This tonal architecture — the refusal of the major-key consolation that Schubert's music sometimes offers — gives the quartet its particular quality of unflinching seriousness.

It is worth knowing, finally, that Schubert never heard this quartet performed. It was played privately in 1826, two years after its composition and two years before his death, but the performance that would have mattered to him — a public professional concert — did not happen in his lifetime. He died in November 1828, aged thirty-one, having written in his last years some of the greatest music in the Western tradition. The D minor quartet is one of the handful of works that any fair assessment of that tradition must place at its centre.

In Your Collection

Three recordings of the Death and the Maiden quartet are in your collection. The Lindsays on ASV — held in both the earlier ‘Lindsay String Quartet’ pressing CD 407 and the later ‘The Lindsays’ cover CD 402 — are widely regarded as among the finest British quartet recordings of the late twentieth century: direct, emotionally committed, and technically superb. The Alban Berg Quartett on EMI Classics CD 404 pairs the work with the earlier E major quartet D.353, and brings the Viennese ensemble's characteristic tonal beauty and technical perfection to music they had played together for decades. The Belcea Quartet on EMI CD 408 is the most recent of the three, part of a two-disc set that also includes the G major quartet D.887 and the String Quintet D.956; their approach is more searching and less comfortable than the Alban Berg, with a rawness that suits the work's emotional world. The Brandis Quartet's three-disc late chamber music set CD 397 also includes the work as part of a complete survey of the late string music.

Listening to the same work in two or three different recordings before or after a concert is one of the most rewarding things a collector can do: the differences between ensembles — in tempo, in tone, in the weight they give to individual moments — reveal aspects of the music that a single performance cannot exhaust.

An Exploration

Quartets of the Lesser-Known Composers

Schnittke  ·  Szymanowski  ·  Janáček  ·  Prokofiev  ·  Schulhoff  ·  Honegger  ·  Myaskovsky

Why These Composers?

The string quartet repertoire is far wider than the canonical cycle of Haydn, Mozart, Beethoven, Schubert, Brahms and Bartók that fills most concert programmes. Beyond that familiar canon lies a rich and largely unexplored landscape of works by composers who were either too geographically remote from the Western European mainstream, too politically constrained by the circumstances of their lives, or simply too individual in their language to win the regular programming that their music deserves. The seven composers gathered here represent some of the most rewarding territory in that landscape — music ranging from the sensuous late-Romanticism of Szymanowski to the traumatised Modernism of Schnittke, from the folk-inflected directness of Prokofiev to the harrowing personal testimony of Schulhoff. None of them is truly obscure — all seven have significant reputations in specialist circles — but none has yet achieved the wider recognition that their quartet writing merits.

They share, despite their very different personalities and nationalities, a common circumstance: all seven wrote in the shadow of historical catastrophe. The First World War, the Russian Revolution, the rise of Nazism, the Stalinist terror, the Holocaust, the Second World War and its aftermath — these events shaped the music of each composer in ways that give it a particular urgency and seriousness that purely abstract musical concerns could never have produced. To listen to Schulhoff's quartets knowing what happened to him, or to Myaskovsky's knowing the pressures he worked under, is to hear music that carries historical weight as well as aesthetic value.

Karol Szymanowski (1882–1937) — 2 String Quartets

Szymanowski is the great Polish composer of the early twentieth century — a figure of international stature who remains unjustly underperformed outside his homeland. His two string quartets, separated by fifteen years, represent the two distinct worlds of his creative development: the first belongs to his opulent late-Romantic period, the second to his mature nationalist style.

The String Quartet No. 1 in C major Op. 37 (1917) was written at the height of Szymanowski's most luxuriant creative period — the years that also produced the Third Symphony and the Violin Concerto No. 1. The influence of Scriabin and the French Impressionists is palpable: the harmonies are dense and chromatic, the textures rich and sensuous, the overall atmosphere suggesting a world of heightened, almost feverish beauty. It is music that makes no concessions to austerity; the four movements glow with a warmth and colour rarely encountered in the string quartet medium. The slow movement in particular — marked Andantino tranquillo e dolce — achieves a sustained lyrical intensity that recalls the most ravishing pages of Ravel.

The String Quartet No. 2 Op. 56 (1927) is a different and arguably more individual work. By this point Szymanowski had immersed himself in the folk music of the Tatra highlands of southern Poland and had developed a nationalist style that was simultaneously rooted in genuine folk melody and inflected with his own highly sophisticated harmonic language. The result is music of considerable rhythmic vitality and melodic directness, without sacrificing any of the formal command he had built over the previous two decades. Three movements, the last a set of variations of great inventiveness. A work that fully deserves its growing reputation as one of the finest Polish chamber works of the century.

Recommended recording: The Carmina Quartet on Denon gave a fine account of both quartets; more recently the Szymanowski Quartet (appropriately named) on CD Accord have become the leading advocates, bringing insider knowledge and idiomatic warmth to both works.

Leoš Janáček (1854–1928) — 2 String Quartets

Janáček's two quartets are already discussed in the Reference Guides section of this app, but they deserve inclusion here as the most immediately approachable works in this survey. Both were written in the final decade of his life, when Janáček experienced an extraordinary creative renaissance in his late sixties and early seventies — driven in large part by his obsessive love for Kamila Stösslová, a married woman thirty-eight years his junior.

The First Quartet ‘Kreutzer Sonata’ (1923) takes its inspiration from Tolstoy's novella of jealousy and violence, and the music has a corresponding rawness and intensity. Short, fragmentary motifs rather than developed themes; abrupt dynamic contrasts; a nervous energy that makes everything else in the quartet repertoire sound slightly over-upholstered. The Second Quartet ‘Intimate Letters’ (1928) is even more naked in its emotional directness — a musical love letter to Stösslová, completed two months before his death at seventy-four. Both works are essential listening, and both are relatively short (around twenty minutes each), making them ideal entry points into this repertoire.

Recommended recording: The Škampa Quartet on Supraphon bring Czech authenticity and complete technical command; the Kronos Quartet's early recording on Nonesuch has a rawness and risk-taking that suits the music particularly well.

Sergei Prokofiev (1891–1953) — 2 String Quartets

Prokofiev's two string quartets are among the most unjustly neglected works in his entire output — consistently overshadowed by the symphonies, concertos and ballet scores that dominate his reputation, but of comparable quality and in some respects more revealing of his musical personality.

The String Quartet No. 1 in B minor Op. 50 (1930) was written during his years in the West, when Prokofiev was dividing his time between Paris, America and occasional visits to the Soviet Union. It was commissioned by the Library of Congress in Washington and is dedicated to the Coolidge Foundation — the same organisation that had commissioned Bartók's Fourth and Fifth Quartets. The work has three movements of considerable economy and formal clarity, with Prokofiev's characteristic combination of lyrical directness and dry, slightly sardonic wit. The first movement's opening theme — angular, rhythmically incisive, harmonically elusive — immediately announces a composer thinking in quartet terms rather than transposing his orchestral manner into a smaller medium. The slow movement achieves a quality of autumnal lyricism; the finale, a rondo of considerable rhythmic energy, brings the work to a brisk and satisfying conclusion.

The String Quartet No. 2 in F major Op. 92 (1941) is the more distinctive of the two and one of the most original quartet works of the century. It was written at the Nalchik Music School in the Kabardino-Balkar region of the North Caucasus, where Prokofiev had been evacuated after the German invasion of the Soviet Union. Immersed in the folk music of the Kabardian people — music quite unlike anything in the Western tradition, with unusual modal scales and asymmetrical rhythms — Prokofiev produced a quartet that integrates this exotic material into his own language with astonishing naturalness. The slow movement's long, hypnotic folk-inflected theme is one of the most haunting things he ever wrote; the scherzo crackles with rhythmic energy; the finale dances with an irresistible momentum. A work of complete originality that sounds like nothing else in the quartet repertoire.

Recommended recording: The Emerson String Quartet recorded both on DG in a coupling that remains the finest available. The Moscow-based Borodin Quartet's older recording of No. 2 (available on various labels) brings an idiomatic authority to the folk material that Western ensembles rarely match.

Arthur Honegger (1892–1955) — 3 String Quartets

Honegger is best known for the orchestral work Pacific 231 (1923) and the dramatic oratorio Joan of Arc at the Stake (1938), but his chamber music — including three string quartets — is of considerable quality and reveals a more intimate and searching side of his personality than the large public works suggest.

Honegger was Swiss-born but spent most of his creative life in Paris, where he was a member of the group of composers known as Les Six alongside Milhaud, Poulenc, Tailleferre, Auric and Durey. His musical language, however, was always more serious and contrapuntally rigorous than most of his colleagues — the influence of Bach was profound and lasting, and it gives his quartet writing a structural backbone that the more impressionistic French composers often lack.

The String Quartet No. 1 (1916–17) is a student work of considerable promise but limited individuality. The Second Quartet (1934–36) is the masterpiece of the three — four movements of great formal control and emotional depth, with a slow movement of sustained lyrical intensity and a finale of considerable rhythmic energy. The harmonic language is tonal but freely chromatic, the counterpoint rigorous without being academic. The Third Quartet (1936–37) is more compressed and austere, written in a period of personal and political anxiety as the clouds gathered over Europe; its three movements have a concentrated seriousness quite unlike the relative ease of No. 2.

Recommended recording: The Sine Nomine Quartet on Claves have recorded all three quartets and remain the leading advocates; their playing combines technical precision with genuine musical understanding of Honegger's sometimes austere idiom.

Erwin Schulhoff (1894–1942) — 2 String Quartets

Erwin Schulhoff is the most poignant figure in this survey — a Czechoslovakian composer of extraordinary talent and versatility who died in the Wülzburg concentration camp in Bavaria in August 1942, aged forty-eight. His music was largely forgotten after his death and has only been rediscovered and rehabilitated since the 1980s; the rehabilitation has been enthusiastic, and with good reason.

Schulhoff absorbed an extraordinarily wide range of influences in his short career: the expressionism of Schoenberg and Berg, the jazz idiom that he encountered in the 1920s and integrated into his own language with remarkable naturalness, the Czech folk tradition, and the Dadaist spirit of post-war bohemian culture. The result is music of remarkable variety and energy — sometimes wild, sometimes lyrical, always distinctive.

The String Quartet No. 1 (1924) is one of the most exhilarating chamber works of the 1920s. Five movements, the third of which is a full-scale jazz movement — a Blues for string quartet of infectious rhythmic vitality — sandwiched between more conventionally structured movements. The mixture of jazzy syncopation, Czech folk idiom and post-Romantic lyricism should not work and yet it does, completely: the quartet has a unity of character that transcends its stylistic eclecticism. The finale's explosive energy is simply irresistible.

The String Quartet No. 2 (1925) is more concentrated and arguably more individual. Five movements again, but here the folk and jazz elements are more fully integrated into a personal language that is recognisably Schulhoff's own. The slow movement — marked Alla Serenata — achieves a quality of warm nocturnal lyricism; the finale's rhythmic drive carries everything before it. A work of complete and distinctive originality.

Knowing what happened to Schulhoff gives these works an added dimension that is impossible to ignore. Written in the mid-1920s, when he was at the height of his powers and the world still seemed full of possibility, they have a vitality and joy that makes his subsequent fate all the more devastating. These are not tragic works in themselves — but history has made them so.

Recommended recording: The Petersen Quartet on Capriccio have produced the finest complete recording of the two quartets, capturing both the exuberance and the more inward qualities of the music with equal success. The Pražák Quartet on Praga also give an idiomatic Czech perspective.

Nikolai Myaskovsky (1881–1950) — 13 String Quartets

Myaskovsky is the most substantial and least-known figure in this survey — a composer who produced an enormous body of work of consistent quality across a long career, yet who remains almost entirely unknown outside Russia. His thirteen string quartets span nearly three decades of Soviet musical life and constitute one of the most significant bodies of quartet writing of the twentieth century.

Myaskovsky was a contemporary of Prokofiev — they were close friends from their student days — and like Prokofiev he spent his career navigating the increasingly treacherous waters of Soviet cultural politics. Unlike Prokofiev, he never left the Soviet Union and never achieved an international reputation; his music circulated almost exclusively within the Soviet cultural system, winning considerable prizes and official recognition while remaining largely unknown in the West. The 1948 Zhdanov Decree attacked him alongside Prokofiev and Shostakovich, though he was less severely condemned than the other two.

His quartet writing spans an extraordinarily wide range of expression, from the turbulent late-Romantic world of the early works to the concentrated austerity of the late quartets. The First Quartet in A minor Op. 33 No. 1 (1930) is a good starting point — four movements of assured craftsmanship, with a slow movement of considerable depth. The Third Quartet in D minor Op. 33 No. 3 (1930) is perhaps the finest of the early works: three movements of great emotional directness, with a slow movement of Schubert-like lyricism that is among the most beautiful things he wrote.

The Ninth Quartet in D minor Op. 62 (1943) was written during the Second World War and is the most immediately dramatic of the set — three movements of considerable intensity, the slow movement harrowing in its sustained grief. The Thirteenth Quartet in A minor Op. 86 (1949), his last, is the most austere and concentrated: two movements of late-style economy, clearly written in the shadow of the Zhdanov Decree and the political constraints of his final years. It is a work of considerable courage as well as beauty.

Myaskovsky's music rewards patient exploration. It does not offer the immediate accessibility of Prokofiev or the dramatic intensity of Shostakovich, but it has a depth and seriousness that becomes more apparent with repeated listening. He is one of the great undiscovered composers of the twentieth century.

Recommended recording: The Taneyev Quartet recorded several of the quartets for Melodiya in Soviet-era recordings that remain the most idiomatic available, though the sound quality is variable. More recently the Moscow Quartet has recorded selected works. Complete cycles remain rare; this is fertile territory for adventurous labels.

Alfred Schnittke (1934–1998) — 4 String Quartets

Alfred Schnittke is the most immediately powerful figure in this survey and the one most likely to be encountered in concert. His four string quartets, written between 1966 and 1989, are among the most important chamber works of the late twentieth century — visceral, technically demanding, and emotionally overwhelming in a way that few works in any medium can match.

Schnittke was a Soviet composer of German heritage who developed, from the early 1970s onwards, the technique he called ‘polystylism’ — the deliberate juxtaposition of musical styles from different periods and traditions within a single work. Baroque counterpoint, Romantic harmony, jazz idioms, twelve-tone technique, folk music and brutal avant-garde dissonance collide in his scores with an intentional violence that creates a kind of musical whiplash. The technique is not merely provocative: it expresses something real about the fragmented, traumatised world of late Soviet culture, where historical certainty had collapsed and nothing could be taken at face value.

The String Quartet No. 1 (1966) is the most accessible of the four — still rooted in a recognisable post-war modernism, with a clear formal structure and a slow movement of considerable lyrical beauty. A good entry point for listeners new to Schnittke's world.

The String Quartet No. 2 (1980) is a different matter entirely. It begins with a chaconne of extraordinary intensity — a passacaglia over a ground bass that accumulates emotional weight with each repetition until it becomes almost unbearable. The second movement is a toccata of violent energy; the third is an Adagio of devastating stillness. This is music that has been compared to a scream — not metaphorically but structurally: it builds to a point of such sustained intensity that release becomes a physical necessity. One of the great chamber works of the twentieth century.

The String Quartet No. 3 (1983) quotes Bach's Musical Offering, Beethoven's Grosse Fuge and his own musical monogram (Es-C-H-A, the German note names) throughout its three movements — a meditation on the relationship between past and present, tradition and innovation, that is simultaneously intellectually rigorous and emotionally devastating. The juxtapositions between the quoted materials and Schnittke's own language create moments of jarring dislocation that are among the most striking effects in any twentieth-century quartet.

The String Quartet No. 4 (1989) was written after Schnittke had suffered the first of several strokes that would progressively disable him. It has the quality of a man speaking with great effort and great deliberation — slower, sparser, more concentrated than its predecessors, with long silences between statements. A work of immense gravity and a worthy conclusion to one of the most significant quartet cycles of the late twentieth century.

Recommended recording: The Kronos Quartet's recordings of Nos. 1–3 on Nonesuch are definitive — their commitment to this music over many years has been total, and the performances combine technical mastery with genuine emotional understanding. The Keller Quartet on ECM give a more considered and perhaps more European reading of Nos. 2 and 3. For No. 4, the Arditti Quartet's account is the most searching available.

Where to Begin

If this repertoire is new to you, the most natural entry points are probably Janáček's Intimate Letters (the most immediately communicative), Schulhoff's First Quartet (the most immediately enjoyable), and Schnittke's Second Quartet (the most immediately powerful). Prokofiev's Second is the most original and perhaps the most surprising. Szymanowski's First is the most beautiful. Honegger's Second is the most formally satisfying. Myaskovsky requires the most patience but offers the deepest rewards over time.

None of these composers wrote music that is merely interesting as a historical or musicological curiosity. All seven produced quartet works of genuine artistic value that stand fully on their own terms alongside the canonical masterpieces. The main obstacle to their wider appreciation is simply unfamiliarity — and the remedy for that is listening.

Further Reading

Books
The String Quartet: A History
Paul Griffiths  ·  Thames and Hudson, 1983

The most comprehensive single-volume history of the quartet from Haydn to the late twentieth century, with substantial chapters on many of the composers discussed here. Griffiths writes with clarity and enthusiasm, and the book covers a much wider range of repertoire than most comparable volumes. Essential reading for anyone wanting to understand where these composers fit in the broader story of the form.

Erwin Schulhoff: His Life and Work
Various contributors  ·  Schott, 2000

The most thorough English-language study of Schulhoff's life and music, covering his career from its promising beginnings through the jazz experiments of the 1920s to his arrest and death. Includes detailed analyses of both string quartets and places them in the context of his wider output and the political circumstances of his life.

Online
AllMusic Classical
allmusic.com

The most useful general-purpose resource for classical discography, with composer biographies, work catalogues and recording reviews covering all seven composers discussed here. Particularly helpful for tracking down available recordings of lesser-known repertoire and understanding the discographical landscape before committing to a purchase.

Presto Music — Contemporary & 20th Century
prestomusic.com

The best online source for tracking down recordings of specialist repertoire, with a comprehensive catalogue covering all the composers discussed here. The editorial recommendations and listening samples make it easier to make informed choices in unfamiliar repertoire.

An Exploration  ·  Part 2

Quartets of the Lesser-Known Composers

Weinberg  ·  Villa-Lobos  ·  Martinů  ·  Reger  ·  Fauré  ·  Bridge  ·  Enescu

Introduction

The seven composers in this second survey share a quality common to all the most rewarding unexplored repertoire: once encountered, they are impossible to understand why you had not found them sooner. Several of them — Weinberg, Reger, Enescu — are composers whose names are known but whose music remains almost entirely unfamiliar to all but a handful of enthusiasts. Others — Fauré, Bridge, Villa-Lobos — are well enough known in other genres but rarely encountered as quartet composers. The connecting thread is consistent quality: none of these composers produced quartet writing that is merely interesting or historically significant. All seven wrote works of genuine artistic value that deserve regular performance and attentive listening.

As with Part 1, several of these composers worked in circumstances of personal or historical difficulty that left unmistakable traces in their music. Weinberg was a Holocaust survivor whose entire family was murdered by the Nazis; Reger died of a heart attack at forty-three, burned out by decades of overwork; Bridge was so traumatised by the First World War that he transformed himself from a late Romantic into a modernist almost overnight. The music absorbs these experiences without becoming merely autobiographical: it transcends its origins while remaining shaped by them.

Mieczysław Weinberg (1919–1996) — 17 String Quartets

Weinberg is the great discovery of the early twenty-first century in classical music — a composer of extraordinary range and depth who spent his entire career in Shostakovich's shadow, was largely ignored by the West during his lifetime, and has only begun to receive his due recognition since his death. His seventeen string quartets, written between 1937 and 1996, constitute one of the most significant bodies of chamber music of the twentieth century. If you respond to Shostakovich's quartets, Weinberg is not simply comparable — he is essential.

The biographical context is important. Weinberg was born in Warsaw to a Jewish family; he fled to the Soviet Union in 1939 just days before the Nazi invasion of Poland, and his entire family — parents, sister, brother-in-law — were subsequently murdered in the Holocaust. He spent the rest of his life in Moscow, befriended and championed by Shostakovich, composing with extraordinary productivity under the constant constraints of Soviet cultural politics. In 1953 he was arrested on charges of ‘Jewish bourgeois nationalism’ and spent several months in prison before Stalin's death led to his release.

The quartets span his entire creative life and absorb all of this experience without becoming merely confessional. The early works — particularly the Third Quartet Op. 14 (1944) — show a composer already fully formed: the harmonic language is personal and distinctive, neither imitative of Shostakovich nor derivative of the Western modernism he had never directly encountered. The slow movements throughout the cycle are consistently extraordinary — long, searching, often heartbreaking in their sustained lyricism.

The Seventh Quartet Op. 59 (1959) is perhaps the finest of the cycle and the best starting point: four movements of remarkable emotional range, from the sardonic energy of the scherzo to the devastating stillness of the slow movement. The Twelfth Quartet Op. 103 (1970) is more austere and concentrated — three movements of late-style economy that reward patient listening with considerable depth. The Seventeenth and last quartet, Op. 146 (1987), was written when Weinberg was sixty-eight and already seriously ill; it has the quality of a valediction — quiet, deeply inward, and profoundly moving.

Weinberg's music has a quality that is difficult to characterise but immediately recognisable once encountered: a kind of melancholy tenderness, a combination of emotional directness and formal sophistication, that is entirely his own. Shostakovich, who knew his music better than anyone, considered him a composer of the first rank. That assessment is increasingly shared.

Recommended recording: The Quatuor Danel on Fuga Libera have recorded the complete cycle in a project of extraordinary dedication — twelve discs covering all seventeen quartets, with playing of complete technical and musical authority. Individual quartets are available separately. The Kuss Quartet have also recorded selected works to high acclaim.

Heitor Villa-Lobos (1887–1959) — 17 String Quartets

Villa-Lobos is the most performed Latin American composer in the concert hall, celebrated above all for the Bachianas Brasileiras and the guitar preludes; but his seventeen string quartets, spanning nearly fifty years from 1915 to 1957, are almost entirely unknown outside Brazil. This is a significant omission from the international chamber music repertoire, and one that is gradually beginning to be corrected.

Villa-Lobos's musical language is unlike anyone else's — a synthesis of Brazilian folk and popular music (including choro, the urban popular genre he absorbed as a young man playing guitar in Rio cafés), the European classical tradition (particularly Bach, whose influence is pervasive), and a quality of raw natural energy that seems to reflect the particular character of Brazilian geography and culture. The early quartets are rough and exuberant, occasionally untidy, always individual; the later works show increasing formal discipline without losing the melodic vitality and rhythmic energy that make his music instantly recognisable.

The String Quartet No. 1 (1915) is one of the most immediately striking first quartets in the literature — not polished, not particularly well-organised, but bursting with melodic invention and rhythmic vitality that no amount of technical imperfection can suppress. The Sixth Quartet (1938) is generally considered the finest of the set: four movements of considerable formal control, with a slow movement of sustained lyrical beauty and a finale of irresistible rhythmic energy. The folk and popular music elements are now fully integrated into a personal compositional language rather than merely superimposed upon it.

The later quartets — from No. 14 (1953) onwards — show Villa-Lobos moving towards a more abstract and chromatic language under the influence of the mid-century avant-garde, without entirely abandoning his characteristic melodic directness. The Seventeenth Quartet (1957), his last, is austere by his own standards but still unmistakably his own voice. A fascinating body of work that deserves far wider exploration.

Recommended recording: The Quartet Brasileiros have recorded the complete cycle for Naxos in performances of idiomatic authority that make the strongest possible case for the music. The Cuarteto Latinoamericano have also recorded selected quartets to considerable critical acclaim.

Bohuslav Martinů (1890–1959) — 7 String Quartets

Martinů is the second great Czech composer of the twentieth century after Janáček, though his musical personality is entirely different — where Janáček is angular, concentrated and emotionally raw, Martinů is fluent, rhythmically vital and melodically inventive in a way that owes as much to French neo-Classicism as to the Czech folk tradition. His seven string quartets, written between 1917 and 1947, are among the most rewarding and least-performed works in the mid-century chamber repertoire.

Martinů left Czechoslovakia for Paris in 1923 and spent seventeen years there, absorbing the influence of Stravinsky, the French neo-Classical school and jazz — influences that permanently shaped his language without obliterating the Czech melodic character that was his birthright. When the German invasion of France forced him to flee in 1940, he eventually reached the United States, where he spent most of the rest of his life in exile, longing for a homeland he was never able to return to.

The String Quartet No. 4 (1937) is the acknowledged masterpiece of the set — three movements of remarkable energy and formal clarity, with Martinů's characteristic combination of driving rhythmic momentum and warm lyrical melody. The second movement's sustained lyricism provides a perfect foil to the outer movements' energy. The Fifth Quartet (1938), written on the eve of the Munich crisis, has a more anxious quality — the melodic material is as inventive as ever, but the emotional atmosphere is shadowed by the political events unfolding around the composer.

The Seventh and last quartet (1947) was written in New York during his American years and is the most concentrated and formally ambitious of the set. Three substantial movements show a composer at the height of his powers, with a slow movement of considerable depth and emotional weight. An ideal introduction to a composer who, once discovered, proves endlessly rewarding.

Recommended recording: The Pražák Quartet on Praga have recorded the complete cycle in performances that combine Czech authenticity with complete technical command. The Stamitz Quartet on CPO offer a fine alternative, with particularly strong accounts of the later quartets.

Max Reger (1873–1916) — 9 String Quartets

Reger is the most underrated major composer in the entire German tradition — a figure of extraordinary technical mastery and genuine musical depth who has never achieved the reputation his music deserves, partly because of its formidable difficulty, partly because of its sheer quantity (the catalogue runs to 146 opus numbers), and partly because the musical world moved in directions that made his late-Romantic polyphonic complexity seem old-fashioned almost from the moment it was written. His nine string quartets, spanning the years 1900 to 1911, are among the finest works in the form between Brahms and Bartók.

Reger was a direct inheritor of Brahms — the same chromatic harmonic language, the same contrapuntal rigour, the same preference for absolute rather than programmatic music. But he pushed the Brahmsian inheritance further than Brahms himself had: the chromatic language is denser, the textures more complex, the formal structures more elaborate. The results can initially seem overwhelming, but patient listening reveals a composer of consistent melodic invention and deep musical seriousness.

The String Quartet in D minor Op. 74 (1903–04) is a good starting point — four movements of considerable emotional range, with a slow movement of warm, sustained lyricism that shows Reger at his most immediately accessible. The String Quartet in E♭ major Op. 109 (1909) is the acknowledged masterpiece: four substantial movements of extraordinary polyphonic richness, the slow movement a passacaglia of remarkable sustained beauty. This is one of the great unperformed quartets of the late Romantic era.

Reger died of a heart attack in Leipzig in May 1916, aged forty-three, having worked himself into an early grave through decades of intense creative activity combined with heavy drinking and a restless, driven personality. The late quartets — particularly Op. 109 — have a quality of desperate creative energy that makes them all the more poignant in retrospect. He left behind a body of quartet writing that deserves far more regular performance than it currently receives.

Recommended recording: The Artemis Quartet on Virgin Classics recorded Op. 109 in a performance of complete authority that remains the finest available. The Merel Quartet have recorded a broader selection of the quartets for MDG with considerable success. For the complete survey, the Mannheim String Quartet's cycle on CPO is valuable if uneven.

Gabriel Fauré (1845–1924) — 1 String Quartet

Fauré wrote only one string quartet, completed in 1924 when he was seventy-nine years old and almost entirely deaf — and it is one of the most beautiful works ever written for the medium. That a composer who had lived his entire creative life in the shadow of the piano, the voice and chamber combinations involving piano should produce, at the very end of his life and in conditions of almost total sensory deprivation, a work of such serenity and formal perfection is one of the more remarkable facts in musical history.

The String Quartet in E minor Op. 121 (1923–24) has three movements. The first is of unusual harmonic richness — Fauré's late harmonic language, which had been moving steadily away from conventional tonal progressions throughout his career, here achieves a luminous, floating quality that defies easy description. The harmonies shift and dissolve into each other with an almost impressionistic fluidity, yet the underlying formal structure is perfectly clear. The slow movement — Andante — is the emotional heart of the work: a long, gently arching melody in the first violin, accompanied by the simplest possible harmonies in the other three instruments, that achieves a quality of transcendent calm that is quite unlike anything else in the repertoire. It does not resolve into consolation so much as it simply exists — serene, still, and deeply beautiful. The finale brings a return of rhythmic energy without disturbing the overall atmosphere of peaceful withdrawal from the world.

Fauré completed the quartet in September 1924; he died in November of the same year. It is impossible not to hear the work as a valediction — not a tragic one, but a profoundly peaceful leave-taking, the final statement of a composer who had spent eighty years in pursuit of a quality of beauty that he here, at the last, fully achieved. It is one of those rare works that changes slightly in character with every listening — revealing new harmonic details, new subtleties of voice-leading, new aspects of its apparently simple surface — without ever losing the quality of immediate natural beauty that makes the first hearing so striking.

Recommended recording: The Ébène Quartet on Virgin Classics give the most beautiful modern account; their tone quality and sense of the work's unusual harmonic language are perfectly matched to the music. The Quatuor Ysaÿe on Valois offer a slightly more searching interpretation. Both are outstanding.

Frank Bridge (1879–1941) — 4 String Quartets

Frank Bridge is best known to posterity as Benjamin Britten's teacher — the man who gave Britten his early lessons and whose influence shaped the younger composer's development in ways Britten always acknowledged. But Bridge's own music deserves serious attention quite independently of his pedagogical role, and nowhere more so than in his four string quartets, which trace one of the most remarkable transformations in twentieth-century British music.

The String Quartet No. 1 in E minor (1906) and String Quartet No. 2 in G minor (1915) are works of accomplished late Romanticism — fluent, well-crafted, clearly indebted to Brahms and the German tradition. The Second in particular, written in the early months of the First World War, has a quality of autumnal warmth and formal assurance that places it in the front rank of Edwardian chamber music. Fine works, but not yet distinctively individual.

Then something happened. The First World War destroyed Bridge's faith in the world he had grown up in, and it transformed his music. By the time of the String Quartet No. 3 (1926), the Romantic idiom had been entirely discarded in favour of a chromatic, dissonant language closer to Berg and the Viennese Second School than to anything in the British tradition. The transformation was so complete that British audiences and critics were largely baffled — the music seemed to have no connection with the composer they thought they knew. The Third Quartet is four movements of considerable complexity and emotional intensity, with a slow movement of harrowing beauty that could not have been written by the same man who composed the First Quartet twenty years earlier.

The String Quartet No. 4 (1937), his last, is the most austere and concentrated of the four — three movements of remarkable economy, the harmonic language now fully settled into its mature dissonant idiom, the emotional atmosphere one of grave seriousness. Written as the clouds gathered again over Europe, it has a prophetic quality that subsequent history makes impossible to ignore.

Recommended recording: The Britten Quartet on Collins Classics recorded all four quartets in a complete cycle of great authority — appropriately named for a group that understood the Bridge-Britten connection from the inside. The Allegri Quartet have also recorded selected works to fine effect.

George Enescu (1881–1955) — 2 String Quartets

George Enescu is the greatest Romanian composer and one of the most remarkable figures in early twentieth-century music — simultaneously a composer of international stature, a violinist considered by many of his contemporaries to be the finest of the century, a celebrated conductor, and a legendary teacher (Yehudi Menuhin was among his pupils). His two string quartets are among the most extraordinary and least-known works in the chamber repertoire.

Enescu's musical language is the product of an unusual cultural synthesis: he was educated in Vienna and Paris, absorbing the German Romantic tradition and the French Impressionist school with equal thoroughness, while remaining deeply rooted in the folk music of Romania — a tradition whose modal scales, asymmetrical rhythms and ornamental complexity are quite unlike anything in Western Europe. The synthesis he achieved is entirely personal: his music sounds like nobody else's, and it rewards repeated listening with a richness of detail that initially invisible layers gradually reveal.

The String Quartet No. 1 in E♭ major Op. 22 No. 1 (1920) is a work of extraordinary ambition and formal complexity — four movements of considerable length and emotional range, with a slow movement of sustained beauty that draws on Romanian folk scales in a way that sounds neither folksy nor academic, but simply inevitable. The harmonic language is dense and chromatic, the textures richly polyphonic, the melodic invention consistently generous. A masterpiece that has been waiting for the wider audience it deserves.

The String Quartet No. 2 in G major Op. 22 No. 2 (1950–51) was written nearly thirty years later, when Enescu was already in his seventies and living in Paris following the Communist takeover of Romania. More austere than its predecessor, but no less individual: the folk elements are more deeply embedded in the musical language, the textures more transparent, the emotional atmosphere one of profound and autumnal beauty. A valedictory work of considerable power.

Recommended recording: The Voces Quartet on Electrecord give the most idiomatic account of both quartets — Romanian musicians bringing insider knowledge to music that rewards familiarity with its folk sources. The Sine Nomine Quartet on VDE-Gallo offer a fine Western European perspective. Both quartets remain underrecorded relative to their quality.

Where to Begin

The most natural entry points into this second survey depend on what you responded to most strongly in Part 1. If Schulhoff's energy and eclecticism appealed, try Martinů's Fourth Quartet — similar rhythmic vitality and melodic invention. If Schnittke's emotional intensity was what drew you, Weinberg's Seventh Quartet is the obvious next step — comparable depth without the polystylistic dislocation. If Szymanowski's late-Romantic lushness was your starting point, Fauré's single quartet offers a similarly ravishing but more concentrated beauty. And if you found Myaskovsky's patient inwardness rewarding, Reger's Op. 109 will give you more of that quality at a higher level of contrapuntal complexity.

Bridge is perhaps the most surprising discovery for a British listener: the transformation between the Second and Third Quartets is one of the most dramatic in any composer's career, and hearing the two works in sequence makes the nature and scale of that transformation viscerally clear. Enescu rewards the most patient listening of any composer in either survey, but the rewards are proportionate to the patience.

Villa-Lobos is the wildcard — the furthest from the Central European tradition that dominates both lists, and the most immediately energetic and exuberant. If the others occasionally feel weighty, Villa-Lobos is the antidote: music that takes its pleasures seriously without taking itself too seriously.

Further Reading

Books
Mieczysław Weinberg: In Search of Freedom
David Fanning  ·  Wolke Verlag, 2010

The most comprehensive English-language study of Weinberg's life and music, written by the scholar who has done more than anyone to bring his work to international attention. Fanning covers the quartets in detail and places them in the context of Weinberg's biography and the broader Soviet musical world. Essential reading for anyone wanting to explore the quartets seriously.

Frank Bridge: A Composer's Life
Anthony Payne  ·  Thames Publishing, 1984

The standard biography of Bridge, written by a composer who understood his music from the inside. Particularly good on the transformation of Bridge's style after the First World War and on the relationship with Britten that gave him his posthumous reputation. Includes detailed discussion of all four string quartets.

Online
The Weinberg Project
weinberg-project.com

A dedicated resource for Weinberg's music, with biography, work catalogue, discography and news of performances and recordings. The most useful single online resource for a composer whose profile is rising rapidly and whose discography is expanding year by year. Includes links to recordings and streaming sources.

Musicweb International — 20th Century
musicweb-international.com

The most comprehensive archive of reviews of specialist classical recordings, with extensive coverage of all seven composers in this survey. Particularly valuable for tracking down reviews of recordings that mainstream publications rarely cover, and for understanding the relative merits of competing versions in unfamiliar repertoire.

A Buyer's Guide

Choosing a Complete Beethoven Quartet Cycle

Eight outstanding boxed sets compared

Why This Is Hard

There is no other body of work in the chamber repertoire with so many recorded complete cycles of consistently high quality, which is precisely what makes choosing one so difficult. Unlike most composers, where a handful of cycles stand obviously above the rest, Beethoven's sixteen quartets and the Grosse Fuge have been recorded complete dozens of times since the Léner Quartet made the first attempt in the 1930s, and a remarkable number of those cycles are genuinely excellent. The reviewer Lindsay Kemp once observed that there will never be a definitive recording of the complete quartets, since there are simply too many valid ways of playing music this rich, and every serious collector eventually arrives at their own personal favourite through years of comparative listening rather than through any objective ranking.

That said, some cycles have earned a more secure place in the critical consensus than others, whether through major awards, sustained critical acclaim over decades, or the simple fact that successive generations of critics and performers keep returning to them. The eight described below represent a genuinely useful cross-section: from historic mono recordings made before the Second World War to modern digital cycles still in the catalogue, from the intensely Viennese to the brilliantly contemporary. Any one of them would serve as an excellent foundation for exploring this repertoire; the differences between them are differences of temperament and emphasis rather than competence.

Takács Quartet (Decca, 2002–2005)

The most consistently garlanded modern cycle and the obvious starting point for anyone wanting an up-to-date, beautifully engineered set without sacrificing interpretative depth. The Takács received a Grammy and two Gramophone Awards for their 2002 recording of the Razumovsky and Harp quartets, and in 2006 their account of the late quartets won the BBC Music Magazine's Disc of the Year and the Classical Brits Award for Ensemble Album of the Year. One commentator went so far as to suggest the Takács might play this repertoire better than any quartet of the past or present — a bold claim, but indicative of the esteem in which the cycle is held.

The complete set is available in a handsome Decca box that, in its most recent reissue, even includes a Blu-ray audio disc alongside the standard CDs. What distinguishes the Takács from many rivals is the sense of four genuinely equal voices in constant dialogue — nothing here feels like first-violin-with-accompaniment — combined with an unforced, almost conversational naturalness that never feels effortful even in the most harmonically knotted passages of the late quartets. The recorded sound, made over several years at various venues, is consistently full-bodied and well-balanced. For a single modern recommendation to put ahead of all others, this would be it.

Quartetto Italiano (Philips, 1967–1975)

If the Takács represents the finest modern cycle, the Quartetto Italiano represents the most beloved of the analogue era — a set that critics have been returning to for over fifty years with undiminished affection. A Gramophone review of the remastered set noted that in precision of phrase and articulation, in purity of intonation and homogeneity of tone, the Italians have few rivals, adding that their playing repeatedly recalled the still-unsurpassed Busch Quartet recordings of decades earlier. The same review observed that the Italians were not primarily interested in the more sensual aspects of quartet playing, but this never tipped into ugliness or abrasiveness — they captured Beethoven's roughness as well as his lyricism, with no attempt to prettify him.

What gives this cycle its particular character is the famous warmth and blend of the four players' tone — a sound so unified that individual lines sometimes seem to merge into a single instrument of extraordinary richness. More than one commentator has singled out the Italiano's slow movements in the late quartets as a particular strength, suggesting only the Smetana Quartet and perhaps the Takács come close to a similar depth of insight in this music.
