The recordings, made for Philips across the early-to-mid 1970s, have a slightly older analogue warmth that some listeners adore and others find a touch soft-grained by modern standards — but the musicianship is never in question.

Busch Quartet (EMI/Warner, 1932–1942)

The historic benchmark against which subsequent generations have measured themselves, and arguably still the most spiritually penetrating recording of the late quartets ever made, despite being over eighty years old. Led by the German violinist Adolf Busch, the quartet secured an imposing reputation by the late 1920s and began recording the Beethoven cycle across the years 1932 to 1941 — a period disrupted by the fascist takeover of Germany and the ensemble's relocation to the United States, yet the recordings stand as a monument to classically conceived ethos and a singularity of purpose that endures to this day.

Producer Fred Gaisberg and Busch decided that single or first takes would best communicate a sense of studied spontaneity, and the results bear this out: the slow movements in particular carry an unrepeatable intensity, with the Op. 95 'Serioso' recording of 1937 standing as a uniquely fevered document. Pianist Alfred Brendel has written of how the Busch Quartet's profound control of line and movement in late Beethoven demonstrates the composer's characteristic use of sudden dynamic change with unmatched conviction. This is, inevitably, mono sound from the 1930s — restored with great skill by Pristine Classical and others, but unmistakably of its era. For a listener prepared to make that allowance, however, this remains one of the most profound musical documents in the entire recorded history of the string quartet.

Alban Berg Quartett (EMI, 1978–1983, plus 1989 live cycle)

The benchmark Viennese cycle of the digital age, recorded by four professors from the Vienna Academy of Music whose combination of analytical precision and idiomatic authority in this repertoire has rarely been matched. Over their career the Alban Berg Quartett received more than thirty major international awards including the Grand Prix du Disque, the Deutsche Schallplattenpreis and the Gramophone Magazine Award, and much of their recorded output — including the Beethoven cycle — is regarded by critics and the public alike as definitive.

A review of the reissued complete set observed that the members are often described in publicity material as four professors from Vienna, and that if this conjures images of penetrating logic and analysis, it is not too wide of the mark. Unusually, the quartet recorded the cycle twice: an analogue and early-digital studio set from the late 1970s and early 1980s, and then, a decade later, a complete live cycle taped at the Konzerthaus during the 1989 Vienna Festival. Opinion is divided on which is preferable — some collectors prefer the analogue early and middle quartets from the first cycle but the late quartets from the live version — but both display the same combination of polish, intellectual rigour and idiomatic conviction that made this ensemble's reputation.

Végh Quartet (Auvidis Valois, 1972–1974)

The most searching and, to many ears, the most spiritually penetrating cycle of the stereo era — a set that consistently divides opinion between those who find it the most profound Beethoven on disc and those who find its recorded sound merely serviceable. Led by the Hungarian violinist Sándor Végh, the quartet recorded their cycle between 1972 and 1974, just six years before disbanding in 1980, and critics have noted that like almost no other ensemble except perhaps the Busch and the Quartetto Italiano, the Véghs seem to access something close to Beethoven's own mind and soul in this music.

A detailed comparative review praised the phrasing, intonation and subtlety of attack throughout, braced with a sureness that unobtrusively underlines both humour and pathos — performances, the reviewer stressed, that are never mere effect or projection, but consistently illuminate the profundity, breadth and reach of Beethoven's writing. The cello is unusually prominent in the recorded balance, lending the playing a freshness and vibrancy that some listeners find revelatory. This is a cycle for the collector who already owns one polished modern set and wants something rawer and more confrontational alongside it.

Emerson String Quartet (Deutsche Grammophon, 1994–1997)

The most technically brilliant and arguably the most consistently exciting modern American cycle, recorded by an ensemble whose later achievements would include nine Grammy Awards and three further Gramophone Awards. The Emerson's complete Beethoven cycle on Deutsche Grammophon won a Grammy in 1998, cementing the quartet's authority in a repertoire they had performed complete since their first full Beethoven cycle in concert in 1980.

A widely circulated assessment of the cycle describes performances forged in what amounts to white heat — a breathless intensity in the fast movements and a leashed power in the slow ones, with the finale of the third Razumovsky quartet, taken at exhilarating speed, needing to be heard to be believed. The same source suggests that while the unrelenting intensity can occasionally be fatiguing across an entire sitting, this may be precisely what Beethoven demanded of his interpreters. Where the Takács are conversational and the Quartetto Italiano blended and warm, the Emerson are visceral and constantly thrilling — an outstanding choice for a listener who wants Beethoven's quartets to grip from the first bar to the last.

The Lindsays / Lindsay String Quartet (ASV, 1980s)

Already represented in this collection through several individual discs, the Lindsays' complete cycle deserves a place on any shortlist for its unique combination of English directness and unguarded emotional commitment, particularly in the late quartets. One survey of the late-quartet repertoire singled out the Lindsays' account of the Cavatina from Op. 130 for its quiet, radiant beauty, suggesting there is real value in their vision of this music even where the overall cycle may not match the polish of its continental rivals.

The Lindsays were never interested in the kind of immaculate surface finish that characterises ensembles like the Alban Berg Quartett; theirs is a rougher, more spontaneous and at times more obviously human approach to this music, prizing emotional truth over textbook precision. Critics have occasionally noted minor intonation lapses, and devotees of the cycle tend to regard this as entirely beside the point — a small price for performances of unusual warmth and conviction. Given that several individual Lindsays discs already feature in your own collection, this cycle offers an obvious and rewarding way to hear the same musicians' approach extended across the whole of Beethoven's quartet output.

Endellion Quartet (Warner Classics, 2011–2012)

The cycle most often recommended as the best modern, fully comprehensive entry point for a serious new collector — scholarly, complete, and consistently well played without any single overriding interpretative agenda. One detailed survey of available Beethoven quartet cycles counsels making the Endellion package the first choice for a modern digital cycle, not merely because it is so complete and so thoroughly grounded in scholarship, but because the playing of violinists Andrew Watkinson and Ralph de Souza, violist Garfield Jackson and cellist David Waterman is consistently worthy of the music itself.

What the Endellion offers, in other words, is reliability across the board: there is no quartet here that disappoints relative to the others, and no period of Beethoven's quartet writing in which the ensemble seems less than fully inside the music. For a listener who wants a single, modern, traversal of the complete cycle without needing to weigh up the particular virtues of more idiosyncratic or historically located ensembles, this is consistently the cycle that specialist reviewers reach for first.

How to Choose

If you want a single modern recommendation with the broadest critical support and the best recorded sound, choose the Takács. If you want the most beloved cycle of the analogue era, with a famous unity and warmth of tone, choose the Quartetto Italiano. If you want to hear what made this music feel urgent and important to the generation that first championed it on disc, and can make peace with mono sound, choose the Busch Quartet. If Viennese precision and idiomatic authority matter most, choose the Alban Berg Quartett. If you already own a polished cycle and want something rawer and more searching alongside it, choose the Végh. If you want maximum excitement and visceral thrill, choose the Emerson. If English directness and emotional truth appeal more than continental polish, choose the Lindsays — and given that several of their individual discs are already in your collection, this is arguably the natural next step. And if you simply want one dependable, comprehensive, scholarly modern cycle without needing to weigh up competing temperaments, choose the Endellion.

Whichever you choose first, the likelihood — borne out by the experience of nearly every serious collector who has gone before you — is that it will not be the last. Beethoven's quartets reward exactly the kind of comparative listening that owning two or three complete cycles makes possible, and the works themselves are inexhaustible enough that no single recorded approach, however fine, can ever feel like the final word.
